title: 2026 Permanent Magnet Supply Chain Update From Permag
authors: Heeju Choi, Scott Murr
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Rare earth materials essential for magnet production are regularly subject to supply chain risk. In 2025, the global permanent magnet industry entered a transformative period defined by aggressive trade barriers. The $25 \%$ duty on imported Nd-Fe-B magnets, effective January 1, 2026, has shifted the cost-benefit analysis for original equipment manufacturers in the automotive and renewable energy sectors. This paper analyzes the impact of the Section 301 tariff escalations on the operation of major domestic magnet manufacturing facilities in the United States including Electron Energy Corporation (EEC). It further proposes a multiple-source model and long-term agreements for each material type to counteract these challenges. Our analysis indicates that 2026 serves as a definitive proof-of-concept year for non-China sourcing strategies, although full mineral independence remains a long-term challenge contingent on sustained recycling innovations.

title: Development of High-Power Ku Band Transmitter for Mobile Troposcatter System
authors: Khilawan Choudhary, Arun Kumar Singh, Anurag Shrivastava, Deepu Augustin, Sujata Byrappaji, S K Datta
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This paper discusses the development of a high power-density Electronic Power Conditioner (EPC), specifically engineered to pair with a high-power TWT. The EPC ensures high power RF output, achieved through precise regulation, enhanced voltage stability, and minimal ripple in the high-voltage cathode supply. Notably, the high efficiency of the EPC enables long-duration operation of the transmitter without active cooling, making it highly suitable for mobile and field-deployable communication platforms. The Ku-band transmitter consists of a helix TWT and a -14.5kV EPC. The transmitter could provide greater than 700 W of output power in Ku Band with CW mode. The maximum pulse width is 2 ms. The dimension of the transmitter is $400 \mathrm{~mm} \times 300 \mathrm{~mm} \times 600 \mathrm{~m}$, and the weight of the transmitter is less than $\mathbf{5 0 ~ K g s}$.

title: Asymptotic Full Actuation Control for Rail-Less Electric Bus Trains
authors: Shuo Wang, Fei Yan, Guoxiang Gu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper presents asymptotic full actuation control for the proposed rail-less electric bus train (REBT), composed of multiple electric buses coupled together. Its dynamics involve model nonlinearities and parameter uncertainties, posing considerable challenges to control system design. To mitigate the nonlinearities and uncertainties, we propose a novel adaptive control method, integrating gamma-projection operators with time-varying gains in adaptive estimation of uncertain parameters and linearizing the REBT system through feedback cancelation. Under the persistent excitation condition, the proposed method guarantees that both the system state tracks the reference motion state and the adaptive parameter estimation errors converge exponentially to zero, thereby achieving asymptotic full actuation control. Moreover, disturbance rejection in the framework of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{H}_{\infty}$</tex>-control is studied for the linearized REBT system. It is shown that the proposed adaptive control law ensures the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{H}_{\infty}$</tex>-norm from the input disturbance to the output tracking errors for velocity and inter-EB distance controls to be strictly smaller than any given <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\gamma>0$</tex>, effectively suppressing energy bounded disturbances in the worst-case. The simulation study validates the asymptotic full actuation control for the REBT system.

title: Dielectric-Loaded Miniaturized EBG Structure with Enhanced High-Voltage Endurance
authors: Chenrui Wei, Jianxun Wang, Hao Li, Xinjie Li, Yixin Wan, Wei Jiang, Zewei Wu, Yong Luo
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This paper presents a miniaturized high-voltage electromagnetic bandgap (EBG) structure utilizing high-permittivity dielectric materials. The miniaturization is achieved based on the principle that electromagnetic wavelengths in dielectrics are shorter than in vacuum. Through parameter optimization, the size of the proposed EBG structure is reduced to one-half to one-fifth of conventional designs. Additionally, by increasing the thickness of the intermediate gap, the maximum electric field in the proposed structure is reduced by approximately $20 \%$ compared to conventional structures under identical applied voltage conditions, significantly enhancing its voltage withstand capability.

title: Distributed Adaptive Containment Control of High-Order Fully Actuated Multiagent Systems with Actuator Attacks
authors: Songsong Liu, Liuliu Zhang, Cheng Qian, Xianglin Liu, Jiaojiao Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this study, the distributed containment control problem for high-order fully actuated (HOFA) multiagent systems (MASs) with actuator attacks was investigated for a fixed directed topology. Compared to existing actuator attack results, a highly stealthy actuator attack model was proposed, in which the attack signal is generated by an unmodeled system to receive falsified action control signals. First, a distributed containment observer is designed to reconstruct the corresponding virtual leader trajectory, which lies in the convex hull by leaders. With the help of the observer, the considered containment control is transformed into tracking control. Subsequently, the exchange supply function method is integrated into the fully actuated system approaches method control design to address the unmodeled dynamic attack signals and reduce the computational complexity. Then, we establish a strict proof that all states of the systems converge to a bounded region based on the Lyapunov stability theory. Finally, a simulation example is provided to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed containment control scheme.

title: Using TFE-Based MGAWE Method for Traveling-Wave Tube With input/output Structure
authors: Ke Xu, Li Xu, Hangxin Liu, Bingqi Liu, Hang Wang, Bin Li
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Accurate and efficient broadband analysis of slow-wave structures (SWS) is crucial for the design of highpower traveling wave tubes (TWTs). However, the traditional Multipoint Galerkin Asymptotic Waveform Evaluation (MGAWE) method, based on impedance truncation, often suffers from limited boundary accuracy and the inability to handle frequency-dependent ports without normalization. To address these issues, this paper proposes an improved TFE-based MGAWE method. By integrating the Transfinite Element Method (TFE), the proposed algorithm rigorously models the port boundaries. Furthermore, the method utilizes $L U$ decomposition to significantly accelerate the calculation speed for broadband frequency sweeps. Numerical validation is performed using a pillbox window structure. Results demonstrate that the proposed TFE-MGAWE method yields superior accuracy compared to the traditional method and eliminates the need for port impedance normalization, while maintaining high computational efficiency.

title: VKS-8250A Klystron Upgrade for TRADEX for Improved Power Output, Bandwidth, and Reliability
authors: Edward L. Eisen, Rasheda Begum, Brad Stockwell, Takuji Kimura, Kevin Childs, Joseph T. Garvie, Mark Pippit, Dexter Beals
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: The Target Resolution and Discrimination Experiment (TRADEX) radar system, part of the U.S. Army’s Reagan Test Site (RTS) on Kwajalein Atoll in the Marshall Islands, became operational in 1962 using a Varian VKS-8250 klystron amplifier. In 2024, a development project was initiated to design a modern, reliable replacement designated as the VKS-8250A. This new amplifier is an Extended Interaction Klystron (EIK) that utilizes a two-cavity, coupled-cavity, standing-wave output circuit. Operating in an overlapping mode configuration, it yields an instantaneous bandwidth (-1 dB) of $ \gt 400 \mathrm{MHz}$. The predicted peak power output is $ \gt 3.0 \mathrm{Mw}$, averaged over the specified bandwidth centered at 2.95 GHz. The minimum $R F$ duty factor is $ \gt 4$ %, resulting in a minimum average power output $ \gt 120 \mathrm{~kW}$. Predicted gain and efficiency are $ \gt 38 d B$ and >38%, respectively. All major klystron subassemblies utilize state-of-the-art designs baselined from klystrons currently in production, and all design modeling was performed using advanced computer codes. This paper provides details on the predicted performance for this new klystron.

title: Trajectory Tracking of Glide-Phase Hypersonic Vehicles Via Nonsingular Fast Terminal Sliding Mode Control with HSMO-DO Under Multi-Source Disturbances
authors: Yinsheng Wang, Kexin Liu, Qilong Sun, Qingzhen Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper presents a nonsingular fast terminal sliding mode control with a higher-order sliding mode disturbance observer for trajectory tracking of glide-phase hypersonic vehicles under multi-source disturbances, including initial condition deviations, gusts, and parameter perturbations. First, a higher-order sliding mode disturbance observer is constructed to estimate lumped disturbances in finite time. Then, a nonsingular fast terminal sliding mode manifold is designed to ensure rapid convergence of the tracking errors. Based on the disturbance estimation, a composite continuous control law is developed to maintain tracking accuracy while suppressing chattering. Finite-time stability of the closed-loop system is established via Lyapunov analysis. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves faster convergence and stronger disturbance rejection while guaranteeing smoother control inputs.

title: Path Following of Quadrotor UAVs Based on High-Order Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: XingQuan Yin, MinHao Wu, Peng Wang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper constructs a control framework for quadrotor UAV based on a high-order fully actuated representation of nonlinear dynamics. The position and yaw outputs are differentiated to approach the physical control inputs, so as to clarify the high-order input-output structure of the quadrotor system itself. Since the quadrotor actuator has inherent physical constraints: the yaw torque does not generate translational force, and the generalized thrust does not generate yaw moment, the obtained decoupling matrix presents a structured zero-block state. To deal with this structural characteristic, a full-actuated control method based on block structure is designed to realize the explicit decoupling of translational and yaw dynamics, and retain the physical interpretability of the control command. The controller directly calculates the thrust and torque commands without relying on cascade assumptions, pseudo-inverse-based allocation or singular matrix inversion. The stability analysis carried out by Lyapunov makes the closed-loop exponentially convergent. This is used for path following with adaptive speed regulation and yaw tracking, and constructs a control framework with physical perception and stability guarantee for the autonomous control of agile quadrotors.

title: Advancing Smart City Transformation: Using Connected and Intelligent Vehicular Systems as Resources
authors: Issam W. Damaj, Mehmet E. Aydin, Fiona Carroll, Abdallah H. Salem, Omer F. Rana
publish: IEEE Vehicular Technology Magazine
abstract: This article advocates for the transformative exploitation of connected and intelligent vehicular systems (CIVS) in smart cities. It highlights the opportunity presented by underutilized CIVS resources, including processing and communication hardware, roadside installations, mobile spaces, large batteries, and sensors, to improve urban performance, sustainability, and the overall human experience. The article proposes a shift toward optimal resource utilization, enabling on-demand services that move beyond traditional CIVS roles and support adaptive human-centric smart cities. When effectively integrated, these resources can power intelligent, context-aware services that address real urban challenges, such as congestion, energy demand, public safety, and infrastructure monitoring. The article reviews recent advancements in CIVS technologies and proposes a quality of experience (QoE)-driven approach that incorporates system performance, contextual awareness, and human factors into the design and optimization of service delivery. A service-oriented architecture (SOA) is introduced to automate the integration of vehicular resources using intelligent multiagent systems and to extend cloud-based services. The architecture is discussed in the context of several smart city applications, including real-time traffic management, cooperative driving, environmental monitoring through sensor data, and autonomous vehicle navigation, demonstrating its potential to turn untapped vehicular capabilities into meaningful urban solutions. Simulation results and case study analysis confirm that the proposed framework is both feasible and effective in enabling scalable, QoE-aware, and human-centered smart city transformation.

title: ACL-SSA Optimized Particle Filtering for UWB/LiDAR Fusion-Based Indoor Localization Under NLOS Conditions
authors: Baoyu Zhou, Hao Wu, Bo Chen, Yanling Shang, Fangzheng Gao
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To address the problems of particle degeneracy, slow convergence, and degraded positioning accuracy of the particle filter in UWB/LiDAR fusion localization systems under complex non-line-of-sight (NLOS) environments, an Adaptive Chaotic Levy Sparrow Search Algorithm (ACL-SSA) optimized particle filter-based fusion localization method is proposed. Within the original UWB/LiDAR fusion particle filtering framework, Tent chaotic mapping is introduced to initialize the sparrow population, thereby improving the uniformity of particle distribution and enhancing the global search capability. An adaptive step-size adjustment mechanism is further designed to achieve a dynamic balance between global exploration and local exploitation during the optimization process. In addition, a Levy flight-based perturbation strategy is incorporated to perform stochastic mutation on particles, effectively improving the algorithm's ability to escape from local optima. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with the conventional SSA-optimized particle filter, the proposed ACL-SSA method significantly improves fusion localization accuracy under complex NLOS conditions, alleviates particle degeneracy, and achieves faster convergence speed with better stability. These results verify the effectiveness of the proposed improved algorithm in UWB/LiDAR fusion localization.

title: Output Feedback Control of Discrete-Time Nonlinear High-Order Fully-Actuated Systems with Input and Output Delays
authors: Yu-Xuan Chen, Shuo-Yi Zhang, Ai-Guo Wu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this paper, the stabilization problem is considered for a class of discrete-time nonlinear high-order fully actuated systems (HOFASs) with both input and output delays. The basic method is to design a prediction feedback control law. For this end, an observer-predictor is developed by utilizing the current and past output information to predict the future state of the investigated system. Linear matrix inequalities (LMIs) are used to determine the gain matrix to ensure the convergence of the prediction error. Based on the prediction states generated by the designed observer-predictor, the control laws are constructed according to the HOFAS approach. Further, the stability analysis is given to verify that the resultant closed-loop system converges to the origin. At last, a numerical simulation is provided to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Fully-Actuated Inverse Reinforcement Learning Control for Satellite Formation
authors: Chenhao Zhao, Peng Zhang, Yingjie Wang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this paper, a fully-actuated multi-satellite control method is studied based on inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). Firstly, the dynamic model of the satellite relative motion is established and expressed as a fully-actuated system. Under the framework of multi-agent system, the distributed tracking error models and cost functions of the expert and learner systems are constructed, respectively. Meanwhile, the distributed optimal control strategy of the expert system is designed based on Riccati equation. In order to imitate the trajectory of the expert system, a model-based IRL algorithm is proposed. By iteratively updating the weight matrix of state and control, the control gain of the learner converges to the target gain of the expert system. Theoretical analysis proves the convergence and stability of the algorithm. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified by a simulation example of satellite formation.

title: Trajectory Tracking of Robotic Manipulators Via a Fully-Actuated System Combined with Control Barrier Functions Strategy
authors: Mengkun Zhang, Hui Zhang, Zhanyuan Cao, Heliang Zhang, Jilong Wang, Jinlei Zhuang, Shijie Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To overcome the computational burdens of multi-step prediction and the limitation in rigidly bounding coupled physical torques in trajectory tracking systems, the present research proposes a control strategy integrating the Fully-Actuated System (FAS) strategy with Control Barrier Functions (CBF). First, the FAS approach is employed to compensate for nonlinear dynamics, providing a nominal linear closed-loop system with high-precision tracking capabilities. Subsequently, the CBF theory is introduced as an active safety filter, mapping the state and input constraints into a low-dimensional, single-step quadratic programming problem. Simulation results on a 2-DOF robotic manipulator demonstrate that the formulated FAS-CBF strategy guarantees strict forward invariance under extreme reference trajectories. Compared to the FAS-MPC, the proposed approach overcomes the limitation of bounding highly coupled physical torques, achieving strictly zero constraint violations. Additionally, it drastically reduces the computational overhead, showcasing superior real-time responsiveness and mechanical safety.

title: Rotation of uniaxial magnetic anisotropy induced by interface roughness in polycrystalline Co/Ru/Co multilayers
authors: Yuichi Hisada, Shigehiro Asai, Sachio Komori, Yukiko K. Takahashi, Tomoyasu Taniyama
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: We demonstrate an unconventional rotation of the magnetic easy axis in Co/Ru-based interlayer-coupled multilayers by modifying the interfacial morphology. These multilayers are deposited on a Nb buffer layer, which can control interface roughness precisely by tuning the Nb deposition conditions. The easy axis of uniaxial magnetic anisotropy rotates as a function of the root-mean-square roughness (Rq), a measure of an interfacial roughness, measured by atomic force microscopy for both antiferromagnetically (AFM) and ferromagnetically (FM) coupled Co/Ru multilayers. Note that single Co layers without a Ru spacer exhibit no such behavior even under various interfacial conditions. This rotation originates from the roughness-dependent coexistence of antiferromagnetic interlayer exchange coupling and pinhole-mediated direct ferromagnetic coupling, which alters the relative magnetization alignment between the Co layers. In addition, when the Rq value approaches the Ru spacer thickness, the anisotropy transitions from uniaxial to isotropic for both AFM/FM coupled multilayers. This is attributed to magnetic domain pinning caused by interfacial defects at the Co/Ru interfaces. These findings provide useful guidelines for designing roughness-induced magnetic anisotropy in interlayer-coupled multilayers for spintronic devices.

title: Compress Magnetic Sensing for ITSC Fault Diagnosis of Induction Motor by Reinforcement Learning and Reduced Order Modeling
authors: Xiangyu Guan, Hang Zhang, Xiaokun Chen, Chaoqun Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Magnetic field can be disturbed by current deviation when inter-turn short circuit (ITSC) fault happens on induction motor (IM). A reinforcement learning and reduced order modeling (RL-ROM) framework is proposed in this work to retrieve ITSC fault locations from a sparsely arranged magnetic sensor array attached to the end shield. Full-order transient magnetic field finite element analysis (FEAs) is conducted on Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) points. Surrogate magnetic reduced order model (ROM) of IM is then built by dynamic model decomposition (DMD) and the regression of artificial neural network (ANN) modal coefficient. By minimizing ANN loss as a part of reward function, sparse magnetic flux sensor location vector is optimized by Deep Q-learning (DQN) based RL algorithm. Effectiveness of proposed method is verified on prototype IM. FEAs contains the healthy and predefined ITSC faults. Results show that a 12th order ROM could reconstruct IM magnetic field with maximum error of 5.26% within 2.1 s from 12 optimized leakage flux sensors. Besides magnetic flux density distortion by ITSC can be identified within 1 slot from reconstructed field. Proposed technique can be integrated on magnetic-based IM inspect system thus realizing rapid internal magnetic field reconstruction and ITSC fault diagnosis.

title: Fault-Tolerant Control of Nonlinear Robotic Manipulators Based on Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Enpu Li, Heting Zhang, Wenqiang Ji
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the trajectory tracking control problem of robotic manipulators, and a fault-tolerant control method based on the fully actuated system (FAS) approach is proposed. Considering the actuator faults and reference trajectories, the fully actuated error system incorporating the fault terms is first constructed. Unlike the traditional reduced-order design approaches, the new fault-tolerant controller is designed directly by means of the FAS approach, which can also compensate the nonlinear dynamics of the manipulators and guarantee the manipulator states to converge into an arbitrarily small ellipsoidal neighborhood of the equilibrium point. Finally, simulation studies on a second-order robotic manipulator are conducted to show the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Power Allocation in ISAC Systems With Mobile Target
authors: Zhilin Fu, Sangmin Kim, Sangwon Hwang, Jihwan Moon, Jeongwon Kim, Jaewan Kim, Inkyu Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In this paper, we study the power allocation for an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system which tracks a mobile target. We first model the problem as a Markov decision process, and then tackle it with a soft actor-critic (SAC) based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach. We also combine a Dirichlet policy, which naturally produces normalized continuous actions under random target motion. To exploit different features of sensing and communication operations, we carefully design a reward function such that the system can dynamically control power allocation to conserve resources. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme enhances tracking performance compared to other baselines while sustaining communication performance.

title: Efficient UV-A MSM Photodetector with High Performance Based on a ZnS Nanowire
authors: Ajit Kumar, Vikram Pratap Singh, Sanjay Kumar Srivastava, Amritanshu Pandey
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: In this work, we report a UV photodetector utilizing crystalline ZnS nanowires (NWs) as a photo-sensing layer, integrated on a Si/SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> substrate. The ZnS NWs were synthesized via a cost-effective, scalable, one-pot hydrothermal method, yielding crystalline materials with uniform morphology. The resulting NWs were incorporated into an MSM device, and their electrical response was systematically evaluated under UV-A illumination over the 355-385 nm wavelength range. The device exhibits symmetric, nonlinear I-V characteristics, consistent with Schottky-limited transport at the metal-semiconductor interfaces. Notably, the ZnS NW-based detector achieves a peak photoresponsivity of 75.2 A/W and a specific detectivity of 1.57 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">13</sup> Jones at 385 nm. In addition, the detector demonstrates a fast response, with rise/fall times of 78.63/74.71 ms, respectively, highlighting efficient carrier transport with minimal trapping effects. This superior optoelectronic performance is attributed to the synergistic effect of light-modulated band-to-band excitation within the ZnS NWs, facilitated by the ZnS/Ag Schottky barrier. Collectively, these results establish a promising pathway toward the development of high-performance, solution-processable UV photodetectors for advanced optoelectronic applications.

title: Tractable Approximation of Labeled Multi-Object Posterior Densities_supp1-3702946.pdf
authors: Thi Hong Thai Nguyen
publish: N/A
abstract: Multi-object estimation in state-space models (SSMs) wherein the system state is represented as a finite set has attracted significant interest in recent years. In Bayesian inference, the posterior density captures all information on the system trajectory since it considers the history of the states. In most multi-object SSM applications, closed-form multi-object posteriors are not available for non-standard multi-object models. Thus, functional approximation is necessary because these posteriors are very high-dimensional. This work provides a tractable multi-scan Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli (GLMB) approximation that matches the trajectory cardinality distribution of the labeled multi-object posterior density. The proposed approximation is also proven to minimize the Kullback-Leibler divergence over a special class of multi-scan GLMB model. Additionally, we develop a tractable algorithm for computing the approximate multi-object posteriors over finite windows. Numerical multi-object tracking experiments, using a simulated social force model with uninformative observations, and a real-world social force pedestrian dataset are presented to validate the proposed approximation method.

title: Photonics-based Broadband MIMO Radar with Segmented Digital Beamforming Enabling Beam Squint-Free Fast Imaging
authors: Jiayuan Kong, Fangzheng Zhang, Guanqun Sun, Yuewen Zhou, Jinhu Li, Yihan Wang, Yuhui He, Shilong Pan
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Photonics-based broadband multi-input multi output (MIMO) radar is a promising solution for high-resolution 2D imaging. While the ultra-wide bandwidth enables centimeter level range resolution, it fundamentally conflicts with conventional narrowband beamforming, introducing severe beam squint and degrading angular accuracy. Although scanning time delay (STD)-compensated digital beamforming (DBF) can mitigate this issue, its point-by-point phase compensation imposes a computational burden, hindering real-time applications. To resolve this problem, this paper proposes a computationally efficient segmented DBF algorithm. Leveraging the time frequency mapping property of photonics-based de-chirped signals, the proposed algorithm divides the time-domain echo into sub-segments equivalent to frequency sub-bands. This allows for parallel narrowband processing that effectively eliminates beam squint and suppresses grating lobes without the heavy computational cost of STD compensation. The system is experimentally validated using a 2×4 photonics-based MIMO radar with an 8 GHz bandwidth. Results demonstrate a range resolution of 2.04 cm and an angular resolution of 1.0°. Notably, the proposed algorithm achieves an imaging speed 7.1 times faster than the STD-compensated DBF algorithm while maintaining comparable imaging quality.

title: Wearable Sensor-Based Knee Joint Angle Estimation: Modalities, Modeling, and Applications
authors: Yan Wang, Zhengqing He, Menghao Yuan, Xiaohu Zhou, Aihui Wang, Xiangyu Zhan, Hongnian Yu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Wearable sensor-based Knee Joint Angle Estimation (KAE) supports rehabilitation assessment, assistive control, sports biomechanics, and remote mobility monitoring, yet current evidence is fragmented by heterogeneous sensor setups, modality combinations, data granularity, and validation protocols. This review synthesizes 128 peer-reviewed studies (2008-2024) and provides a KAE-specific perspective on application domains, sensing modalities (e.g., sEMG, IMUs, pressure, optical, and emerging flexible sensors), data sources (experimental/public/synthetic), and modeling approaches from classical regression to deep networks. We consolidate commonly used evaluation metrics (MAE, RMSE, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, NRMSE, PCC/CCC) and discuss their roles in routine monitoring versus safety-critical control. To improve reproducibility, we summarize key challenges with paper-level quantitative evidence (n/N, %) and offer actionable recommendations. Major barriers include reliance on controlled self-collected datasets, limited subject-independent testing, and insufficient robustness reporting under drift and placement variability. We outline directions toward standardized multimodal benchmarks, consistent validation, and clinically relevant testing for reliable translation.

title: Event-Triggered Control for Safety-Critical Buck DC–DC Converters Using Robust Barrier Lyapunov Functions
authors: Xiao-Lin Cheng, Kun-Zhi Liu, Yi Dong, Xi-Ming Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: This article presents a robust event-triggered voltage regulation strategy for DC–DC converters that address both state constraints and disturbances. Conventional barrier Lyapunov functions (BLFs) may face limitations in handling transient constraint violations caused by external disturbances or abrupt reference changes and can lead to potential control singularities. To overcome this, we propose a novel robust BLF (RBLF) integrated with a dynamic safety boundary (DSB). The DSB adaptively adjusts based on the tracking error, enabling the RBLF-based controller to manage large state deviations and robustly guide the system back into the specified safe set with bounded control inputs. This approach effectively prevents singularities and offers resilient safety. To enhance resource efficiency, an RBLF-based event-triggered controller is developed within the backstepping framework. This controller specifically addresses the discontinuity and nondifferentiability of the virtual control law under event-triggered sampling by leveraging its partial derivatives and sampled states. Theoretical analysis confirms closed-loop signal boundedness, uniform ultimate boundedness (UUB) of the system, and Zeno-free operation. Experimental results demonstrate stable voltage regulation, robust performance under multiple disturbances, and a substantial reduction in control updates.

title: Evaluation of Feature Selection Techniques for LSTM- and GRU-Based Lower Limb Joint Angle Prediction Using Multimodal Biomechanical Data
authors: T S Sangeetha, S. N Muralikrishna, Arun P Parameswaran
publish: IEEE Access

title: Integrated by Design: A Hybrid Robotic Palm Fusing a Functionally-Dense Compliant Matrix with an Actuated Skeleton
authors: Oliver S. Neumann, Lena S. Ewering, Robert K. Katzschmann
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Inspired by the human hand, this work presents a hybrid soft-rigid architecture for a robotic palm to enhance dexterity and robustness. While soft-robotic hands often lack the structural integrity to handle heavy objects, classical rigid designs are sensitive to impacts and frequently neglect the palm's functional role. We bridge that gap by combining soft compliance with skeletal elements for robust force transmission. In our design, rigid metacarpal bones house local actuation and transmission units. These units receive torque from forearm-based motors via flexible shafts to drive tendon actuation. We embed these elements in a 3D-printed silicone matrix, which eliminates the design restrictions of traditional moulding. Ball joints for the thumb and little finger enable the formation of three palmar arches. In experiments, the palm achieved active and passive deformations of 74% and 67%, respectively, relative to its neutral width. This shape adaptability offers practical benefits in confined spaces, where internal palm movements enable new grasping and manipulation techniques previously difficult to achieve with traditional designs.

title: SleepXNet: DeepSeek-Guided Hybrid Transformer Model for Clinically Interpretable EEG-Based Sleep Stage Classification_supp1-3692556.pdf
authors: Rakesh Kumar Rai
publish: N/A
abstract: Sleep-stage classification using EEG signals is crucial for understanding brain activity during sleep; however, the black-box nature of most deep learning models limits their clinical adoption and practical use. This study presents a novel hybrid framework that enhances both classification accuracy and interpretability. The pipeline begins with Spectrum Attention-Based Bandpass Filtering (SABF), which denoises EEG signals and preserves important spectral features. A conditional GAN is used to address class imbalance and missing data by generating synthetic segments. The feature extraction process uses a dual-path strategy: (i) the Fourier-Temporal Fusion Network (FTFNet) captures time-frequency and inter-channel relationships by utilizing TKABNet and FTLNet, and (ii) parallel CNN streams extract spatially complementary features from both raw and wavelet-transformed EEG. These outputs are fused to form a unified representation that supports rich abstraction. The fused features are passed through a Transformer encoder to model long-range dependencies, a bidirectional GRU (Bi-GRU) for local temporal continuity, and a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to capture physiological transitions. The model feeds the combined output into a fully connected layer that uses softmax to predict the final sleep stage. For interpretability, the DeepSeek module integrates SHAP-based attribution, attention map visualization, and neuro-symbolic reasoning (e.g., spindle <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rightarrow$</tex-math></inline-formula> N2). DeepSeek also generates clinician-style reports with visual and textual justifications. Evaluations on the SleepEDF-20, CAP, and MASS-SS3 datasets show that the model achieves the highest Accuracy on SleepEDF-20 (88.1%) and CAP (82.0%), while maintaining competitive Accuracy on MASS-SS3 (87.5%), demonstrating strong generalization, transparency, and readiness for clinical deployment.

title: Fission: A Unified FHE Accelerator Supporting Variable Bit-Widths
authors: Tenghui Hua, Jianan Mu, Liang Kong, Bin Sun, Yi Chen, Ge Yu, Jing Ye, Xiaowei Li, Husheng Han, Huawei Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) is a fundamental technology for privacy-preserving computation, enabling arbitrary operations to be performed directly on encrypted data without exposing plaintext information. Currently, the arithmetic-oriented CKKS and the logic-oriented TFHE are the most widely deployed, and real-world workloads increasingly combine arithmetic computation with logical operations. Several unified FHE application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) accelerators have been developed to support both schemes. However, fixed-bitwidth accelerator designs struggle to meet the heterogeneous precision requirements of emerging applications and incur substantial hardware resource underutilization. In this paper, we propose Fission, the first unified FHE accelerator with fine-grained variable-bitwidth capability, efficiently supporting CKKS, TFHE, and their cross-scheme conversions while dynamically adapting bit-widths to maintain high hardware utilization. Our approach abstracts the computation flows of both schemes into a unified Multiply-Accumulate-Reduction (MAR) model and proposes a fused-operator word-level MAR algorithm that supports all operator types. Based on this, we design a high-throughput word-level MAR computing unit with support for 28∼64 bit-widths, using bit-slicing and temporal scheduling to enable flexible precision scaling. We further develop a unified architecture integrated with a workload-adaptive scheduler, ensuring consistently low latency and high hardware utilization across diverse workloads. Experimental results demonstrate that Fission achieves performance speedups of 1.52×, 7.49×, 1.47×, and 8.20× over the state-of-the-art (SOTA) unified accelerator on CKKS, TFHE, scheme conversion, and hybrid workloads, respectively. Notably, under different CKKS bootstrapping parameter configurations, Fission achieves an average performance improvement of 1.56× over the SOTA accelerators at the corresponding bit-widths, further validating its efficiency in multi-bitwidth support.

title: Enjoying What You are Reading?
authors: N/A

title: Octopus-Inspired Embodied Tactile Intelligence: A Universally Calibrated Miniature Vision-Based Suction Cup_supp7-3694100.mp4
authors: Rob B.N. Scharff
publish: N/A
abstract: The integration of high-resolution tactile sensors in suction cups is a key requirement for the realization of octopus-inspired control strategies in soft continuum robotic arms. However, existing solutions lack simultaneous force and orientation detection and either provide insufficient resolution or are too bulky for integration into soft continuum robotic arms. Moreover, inconsistent manufacturing often necessitates labor-intensive individual calibration for each suction cup. This article introduces a miniature suction cup (22 mm × 40 mm) with integrated vision-based detection of pressing force and contact orientation. Through consistent multimaterial additive manufacturing and a robust model architecture trained on augmented, cross-device datasets, this predictive model can be universally applied across devices of identical design, thus eliminating the need for individual calibration of each suction cup. High-resolution and high-accuracy orientation [pitch/roll mean absolute error (MAE): 1.43/1.45 degrees] and force detection (Fx/Fy/Fz MAE: 0.254/0.256/0.763 N) enable closed-loop, perpendicular approaches for optimal sealing. Real-world tests confirm robust handling of small, delicate, misaligned, and curved objects. These results represent an important step toward scalable, high-resolution tactile sensing for octopus-inspired soft robotic manipulation.

title: Octopus-Inspired Embodied Tactile Intelligence: A Universally Calibrated Miniature Vision-Based Suction Cup_supp8-3694100.mp4
authors: Rob B.N. Scharff
publish: N/A
abstract: The integration of high-resolution tactile sensors in suction cups is a key requirement for the realization of octopus-inspired control strategies in soft continuum robotic arms. However, existing solutions lack simultaneous force and orientation detection and either provide insufficient resolution or are too bulky for integration into soft continuum robotic arms. Moreover, inconsistent manufacturing often necessitates labor-intensive individual calibration for each suction cup. This article introduces a miniature suction cup (22 mm × 40 mm) with integrated vision-based detection of pressing force and contact orientation. Through consistent multimaterial additive manufacturing and a robust model architecture trained on augmented, cross-device datasets, this predictive model can be universally applied across devices of identical design, thus eliminating the need for individual calibration of each suction cup. High-resolution and high-accuracy orientation [pitch/roll mean absolute error (MAE): 1.43/1.45 degrees] and force detection (Fx/Fy/Fz MAE: 0.254/0.256/0.763 N) enable closed-loop, perpendicular approaches for optimal sealing. Real-world tests confirm robust handling of small, delicate, misaligned, and curved objects. title: Adaptive DBSCAN-Enhanced Oversampling for Imbalanced Network Intrusion Detection: A Boundary-Aware Approach
authors: Jingnan Dong, Boran Zhang, Shigen Shen, Jun Liu, Guangxia Xu, Zhiquan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Class imbalance is one of the primary challenges faced by machine learning-based network intrusion detection systems (NIDS), particularly when identifying rare yet high-risk attack types. Traditional oversampling techniques, such as random oversampling and SMOTE, often disrupt the inherent manifold structure of the data and introduce noise near class boundaries, thereby degrading detection performance for minority classes. To address this problem, this paper introduce the ADEO framework-an adaptive DBSCAN-enhanced oversampling method that effectively mitigates these issues by combining deep reinforcement learning with boundary-aware geometric modeling. The framework utilizes a Deep Q-Network (DQN) to automatically optimize DBSCAN parameters, enabling accurate identification of natural clustering structures within heterogeneous intrusion datasets. Building upon this foundation, ADEO constructs precise class boundary functions using methods such as convex hulls, minimum enclosing ellipses, or axis-aligned bounding boxes. It then generates samples within these constrained safe regions, preserving the true topological structure while avoiding noise near decision boundaries. Furthermore, this paper introduces hierarchical class mapping strategies and attack-specific feature engineering to enhance the framework's adaptability to different attack families. Experimental results on the NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS2017 benchmark datasets demonstrate that ADEO outperforms mainstream oversampling methods in terms of precision, accuracy, recall, F1 score, FPR, and AUC. Particularly notable improvements are achieved for extreme minority classes like Backdoor and Heartbleed, validating the proposed framework's effectiveness and efficiency in practical NIDS applications.

title: ML-Enabled Dynamic Duplexing for Diverse Traffic Scenarios: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Masoumeh Mokhtari, Petteri Kela, Guillermo Pocovi, Roberto Maldonado, Klaus I. Pedersen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Machine Learning in Communications and Networking
abstract: The emergence of different traffic types with diverse requirements, such as eXtended Reality (XR), in 5G-Advanced and upcoming 6G networks, requires more intelligent duplexing strategies. Sub-band full duplex (SBFD) enables simultaneous uplink (UL) and downlink (DL) transmissions over an unpaired spectrum, thereby providing more frequent UL opportunities. While SBFD has strong potential to improve UL performance without risking DL performance, static radio frame configurations are inherently suboptimal under dynamic traffic conditions. In such cases, dynamic radio frame configurations are required to adapt to varying traffic demands and service requirements and fully realize the benefits of SBFD. This paper proposes an ML-enabled dynamic radio frame selection framework based on a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning agent with a two-step bootstrap mechanism. A bootstrapped general model is trained across varied traffic loads and packet sizes to ensure scalability and robustness. System-level simulations in dense urban deployments demonstrate that the bootstrap generalized model achieves good performance, while maintaining adaptability to unseen traffic conditions. Compared with conventional static time-division duplexing (TDD) baseline with “DDDSU”, the proposed scheme increases UL capacity by up to 268% for large-packet file transfer protocol model 3 (FTP3) traffic and 195% for small packets. For XR services, the ML-based solution extends UL capacity by 25%, confirming its effectiveness in supporting latency-sensitive traffic scenarios. The simulation results further indicate that SBFD can deliver a high satisfaction ratio for XR users by ensuring sufficient throughput for cell-edge users.

title: Spatial Phase Compensation Based on 3D Spherical Wavefront Equivalence and Its Application Expansion in Spatial Curved Surfaces
authors: Yibo Lin, Zishu He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Designing imaging algorithms that balance efficiency, accuracy, and adaptability to various array structures remains a significant challenge in near-field sensing. To address the limitations of existing fast algorithms in handling complex or non-coplanar array geometries, this paper proposes a novel Spatial Phase Compensation method based on 3D Spherical Wavefront Equivalence Convolution (SWEC). By analyzing the geometric properties of spherical wavefronts for Electronic Scanning Arrays (ESA) and the phase history ellipsoids for Mechanical Scanning Arrays (MSA), we demonstrate that the complex 3D phase compensation can be reduced to 1D fast convolution operations within specific spatial curved surfaces. This approach unifies the imaging processing for both ESA and MSA under a generalized framework. The proposed method exhibits excellent adaptability to arbitrary linearly arranged sub-arrays, including folded-surface and cylindrical arrays, without requiring approximation for non-planar geometries. Theoretical analysis and experimental results confirm that the SWEC algorithm achieves imaging accuracy comparable to the Back-Projection Algorithm (BPA) while significantly reducing the computational complexity compared to state-of-the-art fast convolution methods like SAR-FFT and 2DSAR-FFT.

title: Benefiting From OOD Samples in Open-Set Semi-Supervised Object Detection
authors: Yiqi Zou, Kuo Wang, Jichang Li, Chuan Wang, Shuangyin Liu, Liang Lin, Guanbin Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Open-set semi-supervised object detection (OSSOD) is an emerging research area that relaxes the assumption of closed-set in semi-supervised object detection (SSOD), allowing unlabeled data to contain both in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) samples. In the face of OSSOD, existing approaches expect to completely filter out OOD samples from unlabeled data for conventional semi-supervised learning. Different from it, we find that the effective use of OOD samples in unlabeled data can also facilitate feature learning and thus boost the detection performance of ID categories under open set conditions. Specifically, we perform refined instance-level consistency regularization (ICR) on all the detected instances (including the OOD ones) on unlabeled images, while introducing a novel OOD-aware contrastive learning (OCL) that clusters ID objects and pushes away OOD samples in the intra-class feature space, enhancing the compactness of ID features while emphasizing the discrimination between ID and OOD objects. Based on the distinguished features, we further design a prototype-based multimetric adaptive matching (MAM) approach, which identifies ID/OOD samples by adaptively measuring multiscale feature similarity between samples and class prototypes according to the specific category. With the help of this approach, more reliable ID/OOD samples can be mined from unlabeled data for contrastive learning, while the consistency regularization (CR) can be weighted according to the matching scores, correcting the biasing effects of OOD objects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our design can effectively utilize OOD samples to optimize feature learning while avoiding their detrimental effects in semi-supervised learning, which ultimately greatly improves the detection capability of the model in open-set scenarios by a large margin over current state-of-the-art works.
