title: Hierarchical Progressive Transform with Learnable Prior for Hyperspectral Image Super-Resolution
authors: Zhihui Tu, Yisha Gao, Wenyu Hu, Yong Chen, Honghui Xu, Jian Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral image super-resolution (HSI-SR) aims to reconstruct a high-resolution HSI (HR-HSI) by fusing a low-resolution HSI (LR-HSI) with a high-resolution multispectral image (HR-MSI). Tensor Nuclear Norm (TNN) regularization exploits transform domains to capture the low-rank structure of HSIs. However, existing methods face a fundamental rigidity-flexibility trade-off: fixed transforms offer stability but lack adaptability, while data-driven transforms provide flexibility but suffer from optimization instability. To address this, we propose a novel HSI-SR model based on a Log-sum-based full-scale Learnable TNN (LLTNN) regularizer. The core of our framework is the Hierarchical Progressive Transform (HPT) operator, which establishes a principled dual-layer scheme. The explicit layer employs fixed multi-domain transforms to provide a robust structural anchor, while the implicit layer introduces a data-driven adaptive transform that captures scene-specific correlations which fixed bases cannot model. This explicit-to-implicit design yields more compact low-rank representations than single-domain or simple cascade methods. The full-scale framework imposes low-rankness jointly across all three tensor modes with a non-convex log-sum penalty to preserve essential features while suppressing noise. Extensive experiments on simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance compared with existing approaches. The code is available at https://github.com/Tuzhihui/LLTNN_code.

title: Detection of Submerged Heritage: Application of an Unmanned Surface Vehicle Equipped with a High-Resolution Multibeam Echosounder
authors: Rocío López-Juanes, Simón Peña-Villasenín, Manés Fernández-Cabanas, Mariluz Gil-Docampo
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The recent availability of unmanned surface vehicles (USVs) equipped with precise positioning systems and multibeam echosounders (MBES) enables high-resolution bathymetric surveys, yet their application in submerged heritage research remains limited. This study evaluates the feasibility and accuracy of using these tools in the Belesar reservoir (northwest of the Iberian Peninsula), under which lies the ancient village of Portomarín. Bathymetry was conducted using a USV equipped with a multibeam echosounder to generate a three-dimensional point cloud of a medieval bridge, as well as other village structures. The accuracy of the data was evaluated by comparing this point cloud with another acquired through conventional photogrammetry during a period of extreme drought, when the village and bridges were exposed to a significant extent. The findings enabled the assessment of the method's precision and demonstrated its potential for geometric accuracy while ensuring detection with operational safety and documentation of submerged cultural heritage, particularly in areas where conventional survey methods are limited, such as in shallow waters.

title: Implementation of Digital Twin for Optical Mesh Networks: Field Demonstration and Case Studies
authors: Yao Zhang, Yuchen Song, Jin Li, Yan Shi, Yu Tang, Shikui Shen, Xiongyan Tang, Shanguo Huang, Min Zhang, Danshi Wang
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: The integration of digital twin (DT) technology in optical networks has profoundly transformed various operational aspects, including design, deployment, prediction, optimization, and network upgrades. While most DT verifications have been limited to controlled laboratory environments, there is a pressing need to extend this technology to field-deployed optical networks. This requirement is especially crucial for supporting large-scale mesh networks and high-capacity transmission like 400 or 800 Gbps, which operate in non-ideal conditions and present significant challenges for accurate DT implementation. We proposed a practically executable four-step approach for constructing DT models of field-deployed optical mesh networks, involving physics-guided sequential parameter refinement to ensure precision and adaptability to real-time conditions. Validations of our approach through field-trial testbed demonstrated its accuracy, effectiveness, adaptability, and robustness, exemplified by five scenarios. Our results showed that the DT model could precisely predict network performance, with absolute errors in signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of less than 0.57 dB, and can continuously adapt to upgrade cases such as 800 Gbps transmission. The methodology bridges theoretical advancements with practical applications, offering enhanced efficiency and resilience in real-world optical network deployments.

title: Finite-iteration Tracking for Discrete-time Linear High-order Fully Actuated Systems
authors: Xiu-Juan Zhao, Ai-Guo Wu, Rui-Qi Dong
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this paper, the finite-iteration tracking problem is studied for repetitive discrete-time linear step backward highorder fully actuated (HOFA) systems. By using the HOFA system theory, a high-order fully actuated iterative learning control algorithm is proposed for the repetitive HOFA system. Specifically, an open-closed-loop proportional-type iterative learning control (ILC) algorithm is adopted, in which the tracking errors of both the past iteration and the current iteration are utilized to update the ILC input of the current iteration. With the lifting technique, the relationship between the errors of two consecutive iterations is derived. A sufficient condition is provided to guarantee the finite-iteration tracking of the system. In addition, the expression for the settling iteration is explicitly obtained. Finally, a numerical example is given to verify the proposed results.

title: Imitation Learning for Continuous Layered Ditch Excavation Trajectory Planning Based on DMP
authors: Ma Liu, Xiangda He, Jiannan Chen
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper addresses task discontinuity, limited geometric adaptability, and insufficient real-world validation in autonomous trench excavation, proposing a layered trajectory planning method based on a hybrid Dynamic Movement Primitive-Multilayer Perceptron (DMP-MLP) framework. The approach decomposes complex 3D trenching tasks into standardized 2D horizontal layers and modular five-phase execution cycles. DMPs ensure motion stability by encoding expert demonstrations, while the MLP enhances geometric adaptability by learning the mapping from trench specifications to the DMP's nonlinear forcing term. A dual hard-soft constraint mechanism is embedded to guarantee trajectory feasibility under real-world actuation limits. Simulation tests on two distinct trench groups and field experiments on a full-size Liugong 922F excavator verify performance in forcing term fitting, trajectory reproduction, and trench forming accuracy. Results show the method generates feasible trajectories, reproduces expert behavior, maintains stable performance across diverse geometries, and meets earthwork construction tolerances, advancing adaptive layered excavation technology.

title: MNCS Lab: A Mobile-Enabled Remote Laboratory Platform with Easy Integration of Control Experiment Units
authors: Jahan Zaib Bhatti, Liu Guo-Ping, Muhammad Noman Hassan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Remotely accessible control system laboratories remain difficult to extend and administer, particularly when new experiment hardware must be integrated without disrupting an existing infrastructure. This paper presents two architectural contributions to Mobile Networked Control System (MNCS) Lab, a mobile-oriented remote laboratory platform. First, a Remote Laboratory Administration Server (RLAS) built on Laravel 12 and hosted at https://mncs.online handles user authentication, time-slot scheduling, and resource management through a REpresentational State Transfer (REST) Application Programming Interface (API). An integrated Progressive Web App (PWA) dashboard lets administrators configure and register new experiment units directly from the management interface. Second, the MNCS Gateway is a locally deployed server utility that connects any hardware or virtual instrument with a Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) or User Datagram Protocol (UDP) port to the MNCS Lab platform, making it available under a dedicated public subdomain and validating every incoming command against RLAS-issued reservation tokens. The Air Bearing Spacecraft (ABS) platform at Harbin Institute of Technology, China (HIT) validates the efficacy of the system, confirming correct access enforcement, real-time command delivery, and closed-loop control through an Android-native Mobile Experiment Terminal (MET).

title: A Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation Method Based on Multiscale Feature Enhancement and Saliency Modeling
authors: Yuepeng Pan, Xia Liu, Yu Guo, Bing Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper addresses the challenges of complex brain tumor structures, difficulties in local feature extraction, and the ineffective fusion of information from different modalities of images. A brain tumor segmentation model (SPAENet) based on multi-scale feature enhancement and saliency modeling is proposed. The model combines grouped attention (CSSE) and atrous spatial pyramid pooling (ASPP) to fully extract both local and global features of brain tumor images. CSSE focuses on local features by allowing different groups to learn their own independent feature representations. Meanwhile, a salient position enhancement block (SPA Block) is introduced to improve the attention concentration of the model. The model is validated on the BraTS2021 dataset for three regions: enhancing tumor (ET), tumor core (TC), and whole tumor (WT). The Dice coefficients reached 0.84, 0.90, and 0.90, respectively, while the Hausdorff distances were reduced to 4.83, 11.09, and 6.03.

title: Dynamic Training Strategies for Domain Generalization in Self-Supervised Anomaly Sound Detection
authors: Yafei Jia, Jianfeng Chen, Jisheng Bai, Mou Wang, Dongyuan Shi, Zhenghua Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Anomaly sound detection (ASD) is a critical task for industrial predictive maintenance. However, in real-world applications, ASD methods are often exposed to diverse domain shift conditions, which can lead to performance degradation. To address this, we propose a dynamic training framework for domain generalization to improve the generalization ability of self-supervised ASD. First, a progressive training strategy with dynamic masking shifts the model's learning focus from fine-grained details to robust, domain-invariant features by progressively increasing the masking ratio. Second, a dynamic conditional generation technique leverages data similarity to generate transition samples, which helps regularize the decision boundary. Third, a dynamic domain mixing strategy dynamically aligns feature distributions across different domains. Experimental results on the DCASE 2023 and DCASE 2024 Task 2 demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach with state-of-the-art harmonic mean AUC scores of 74.39% and 68.34% on the respective evaluation sets.

title: Cumulative Damage Simulation and Self-learning Response Surface-Based Non-intrusive Online Monitoring of Random Wear Degradation for Shaft Products
authors: Bo Sun, Leyang Zhou, Zeyu Wu, Yeli Zhou, Qiang Feng, Junlin Pan, Tongshu Lin, Jiayu Hu, Mingyong Li, Jian Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: Non-intrusive online evaluation of mechanical products is essential for the health monitoring of long-term operating systems. For shaft components, damage simulation provides a direct means to assess online wear degradation without the need for additional sensors. However, simulating randomly accumulated wear is a challenging and time-consuming task, which poses a contradiction for real-time online applications. To address this issue, this paper proposes an online uncertainty quantification method for random wear based on cumulative damage simulation and a self-learning response surface. The accumulated wear damage is first modeled through high-cost model-updating simulations, after which a self-learning response surface—constructed using Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks—is developed as a low-cost surrogate to facilitate online wear uncertainty quantification. Finally, the proposed method is validated through a tracked-vehicle drive shaft case study, demonstrating its accuracy, feasibility, and timeliness in monitoring random degradation.

title: Disturbance Interval Observer-Based Flight Control for Unmanned Helicopter Using Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Kenan Yong
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This work presents a disturbance interval observer (DIO)-based fully actuated flight control scheme for unmanned helicopters subject to time-varying disturbances and uncertainties. The helicopter dynamics are transformed into fully actuated system representations for the position and attitude subsystems, enabling decoupled hierarchical control design. DIOs are developed to estimate disturbance bounds in real time and provide active compensation for robustness enhancement. Based on the DIO outputs, fully actuated control laws are constructed with smooth uncertainty suppression. Stability is guaranteed via Lyapunov analysis. Simulation results verify accurate trajectory tracking and effective disturbance rejection.

title: Engineering Students’ Critical Engagement With ChatGPT: Effects of Output Accuracy on Answers and Confidence in a Real-World Probability Context
authors: Marija Kaplar, Zorana Lužanin, Miloš Vučić, Lidija Ivanović, Sebastijan Kaplar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Education
abstract:
Contribution: This study investigates how the accuracy of artificial intelligence (AI)-generated explanations affects electrical engineering students’ solution correctness, confidence, inquiry behavior, and reasons for accepting or rejecting ChatGPT’s answers when solving a real-world probability problem grounded in Bayes’ theorem and susceptible to base-rate neglect. Unlike prior controlled or guided research, it captures authentic, unstructured student–AI interactions in genuine learning contexts.

Background: Although students are generally aware that large language models can produce errors, little is known about their ability to recognize such errors or the extent to which ChatGPT’s feedback shapes their confidence during problem-solving in real-world contexts.

Research Questions: This study explores: 1) how engineering students solve a real-life problem with ChatGPT shaping their accuracy; 2) how using ChatGPT affects students’ confidence in their own answers; and 3) what types of questions students ask ChatGPT and why they accept or reject its responses.

Methodology: Using a within-subjects design, N = 107 students solved a real-world task, first unaided and then with ChatGPT. We analyzed ChatGPT’s accuracy, students’ solution accuracy, written explanations, self-reported confidence, interaction logs, and the reasons students accepted or rejected ChatGPT’s answers.

Findings: Unaided accuracy was 14%. With ChatGPT, accuracy rose to 62% when its guidance was correct but dropped to 6% when it was incorrect. Few students used verification-oriented prompts, and many accepted plausible but incorrect answers without checking. Exposure to erroneous outputs increased confidence in wrong solutions, revealing a low level of critical engagement and highlighting the importance of integrating AI literacy with probability instruction.

title: Takeover Response Time Prediction Model Based on Observable Behavior
authors: Tianyi Qian, Hao Dong, Yanji Jiang, Hongyu Jiao, Gang Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In Level 3 automated driving takeover scenarios, the driver's takeover readiness is critical to traffic safety. However, the systematic evaluation of takeover responses based on directly observable driver behaviors remains underexplored. To address this research gap, a controlled driving simulation experiment was conducted. The experiment investigated hand occupancy and gaze position as directly observable behavioral indicators and their effects on takeover response time (TRT). A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times 6$</tex> factorial design was adopted, consisting of three hand occupancy conditions and six gaze location conditions. In addition, drivers' physiological signals and individual characteristics were recorded concurrently. On this basis, a Transformer-based multimodal model for Takeover Response Time prediction was developed, in which an observable behavior encoding mechanism was incorporated to better capture directly observable behavioral features. The results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves the prediction performance of Takeover Response Time, providing empirical evidence and practical insights for real-time assessment and deployment of driver monitoring systems in Level 3 automated driving.

title: New Theory of Emission for Scandate Cathodes
authors: Bernard K Vancil, Victor Schmidt, Charles Osborne, Doug Jones, Malcolm Caplan, Wayne L Ohlinger, T. John Balk
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: A new theory of emission is offered for scandia doped dispenser cathodes. It is based on data collected at ebeam and the University of Kentucky from 2016 to 2024. The data confirms that scandate cathodes do exhibit certain features of semi-conductor cathodes, such as anomalous Schottky Effect. But they also exhibit some of the features of metallic cathodes, such as insensitivity to duty factor variations. The conventional understanding [1] [2] is that an approximately 80 nm thickness of scandium rich material lies on the surface of these cathodes, and this accounts for their semiconductor properties. However, this layer was not found on any of the cathodes constructed at ebeam. This finding is based on SEM/EDS, XPS, TEM and Auger in situ studies. However, a $4-6 \mathrm{~nm}$ layer of scandium-rich material was detected on the surface of all tungsten particles in the matrix of activated and emission tested cathodes. This layer was observed using TEM (transmission electron microscopy) on thin cross-sections. It was also detected on the emitting surface of activated cathodes operated at normal emission and temperature by in situ Auger studies. As a result of these experimental results a new theory of emission is offered. It assumes a thin film semiconductor exists on each tungsten particle in the matrix. These particles in aggregate constitute a thick film semiconductor such as is seen on oxide cathodes.

title: Sliding Mode Fault-Tolerant Control for Nonlinear Fully Actuated Systems with Mismatched Disturbances
authors: Yuzhong Wang, Zhixin Yang, Chong Chen, Dan Ma
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper studies sliding mode fault-tolerant control for nonlinear fully actuated systems (FAS) with mismatched disturbances. To accurately estimate the mismatched disturbances, a disturbance observer is designed within the fully actuated system framework. Then, a n-1-order sliding surface based on the FAS approach and estimated value of the mismatched disturbances is designed. Based on the Lyapunov technology, input-to-state stability of nonlinear fully actuated systems is guaranteed. Moreover, the sliding mode controller is developed to compensate the mismatched disturbances and the system state can reach the sliding surface in a finite time. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed approach is demonstrated through a simulation.

title: Design and Research of an Intelligent Package Station Entry Robot System Based on Jetson Orin Nano
authors: Zhengyang Tan, Jiaxin Jin, Xinhe Guo, Jiayue Zhou, Aili Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In order to resolve the common inefficiencies of sorting and lack of adaptability of traditional automated equipment in small-to-medium-sized delivery stations, this paper proposes the design and implementation of an intelligent warehousing robot system on the basis of the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano. The system is based on the ROS 2 platform and incorporates the Qwen multimodal large language model that enables the proper understanding of natural language commands and independent planning of tasks. Moreover, it has all-around capabilities such as self-navigation, self-sorting, overload sensing and automatic recharging. Experimental findings confirm the viability and effectiveness of the proposed system in practical situations, which provides a very cost-effective solution to the smart transformation of last-mile logistics.

title: Structure-Preserving Virtual-Input ZVD Shaping for Fully Actuated Flexible Spacecraft Attitude Control
authors: Dian Wang, Peiran Li, Yalin Qiao, Yuan Liu, Yunhua Wu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a structure-preserving zero-vibration-derivative (ZVD) input shaping method for fully actuated flexible spacecraft to achieve simultaneous high-precision attitude control and multi-modal vibration suppression. Conventional torque-shaping approaches, when directly applied to control inputs, may degrade attitude precision due to the disruption of the fully actuated system structure. To overcome this issue, we propose a virtual input-based zero-vibration-derivative (ZVD) torque shaping method, in which the ZVD filter is applied to the virtual linearized control input rather than directly to the actual torque. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively suppresses flexible vibrations while maintaining high attitude tracking accuracy, outperforming both the unshaped control and conventional torque-shaped schemes. The approach is generalizable to flexible system with arbitrary fully actuated configurations.

title: Molecular Dynamics Simulations for Benchmarking Electron Emission Theories in Nonplanar Nanoscale Devices
authors: Allison M. Komrska, Mihajlo-Joshua F. Deganus, Kristinn Torfason, Ágúst Valfells, Allen L. Garner
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Operating electron devices at smaller scales with higher electric fields and temperatures necessitates better characterization of electron emission theories. One challenge with characterizing these devices is determining the electron emission area, particularly for nonplanar devices. We have recently developed an approach to calculate the transition between thermal emission (TE), field emission (FE), and the space-charge-limited current density (SCLCD) for nonplanar geometries. Using the Reykjavik University Molecular Dynamics code for Electron Emission and Dynamics (RUMDEED), a molecular dynamics code, we consider thermal-field emission from a tip and calculate the emitted current as a function of applied voltage. Using the asymptotic solutions for TE, FE, and the SCLCD, we determine the emission area in these regimes by adjusting the standard planar theories with the appropriate effective gap distance for the tip-to-tip geometry. These results demonstrate the utility of applying these techniques to assess simulation results and, in the future, experimental results for more complicated geometries.

title: Efficient Design Optimization of a Helix TWT Using Adjoint Equations
authors: Alexander N. Vlasov, David P. Chernin, Thomas M. Antonsen, Igor A. Chernyavskiy
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: We apply a previously published adjoint relation [1] governing the beam-wave interaction in a helix TWT to compute derivatives of certain amplifier figures of merit with respect to various design parameters. We employ these derivatives in a conjugate gradient optimization algorithm to find sets of design parameters that maximize the figures of merit. Both small signal and large signal cases are illustrated. The theory is based on a ID ‘rigid disk’ model of the beam, as implemented in the CHRISTINE helix TWT simulation code, in which the disks are allowed to move only in the axial direction, in response to the combination of circuit and space charge electric fields.

title: Optimal Grid-Tied Charging Station Configuration Considering Representative Traffic Flow
authors: Chenming Yan, Shuai Liu, Xingtang Wu, Yuhan Xu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: With the rapid adoption of electric vehicles (EVs), range anxiety during long-distance highway travel has become increasingly pronounced. Optimally configuring highway charging infrastructure while balancing service quality and construction cost remains a critical challenge. To address this issue, this paper proposes a two-stage optimization framework for siting grid-tied photovoltaic (PV) charging microgrids. In Stage 1, a Robust Representative Day (RDD) flow model is developed to construct a balanced traffic demand baseline. The model combines Huber loss with a peak-day penalty, thereby remaining robust to non-peak anomalies while preserving sensitivity to high-demand periods. In Stage 2, a bi-level programming model is formulated. At the upper level, a Genetic Algorithm (GA) optimizes charger counts and PV areas by minimizing the total social cost. At the lower level, a Stochastic Dynamic User Equilibrium (SDUE) model with M/M/c queuing theory simulates EV routing decisions under congestion. A case study on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$509-\text{km}$</tex> highway corridor with nine service areas validates the effectiveness of the proposed framework. After excluding three topologically infeasible nodes, the optimal solution deploys 40 piles and 41,880 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex> of PV panels. The resulting layout achieves a 100 % service rate, a 12.7 -minute average queuing time, and a 49.9 % green electricity ratio, with a daily total social cost of 107,047.53 CNY. These results show that the framework can balance investment, operational efficiency, and service quality, and can support sustainable highway charging infrastructure planning.

title: DuCCA: Dual-domain Cross-coupled Coordinate Attention for Reliable Visible Cues in RGB–IR Fusion Detection
authors: Jie Xu, Tian Zhou, Liwei Deng
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Rotated object detection is widely used in aerial imaging, remote sensing, and UAV-based perception systems. In RGB-IR multimodal detection, however, the visible branch is often sensitive to illumination variations, background textures, and compression noise, which may introduce redundant responses and weaken cross-modal fusion. To address this issue, we propose a Dual-domain Cross-coupled Coordinate Attention module (DuCCA) to enhance visible feature representations within an RGB-IR fusion detection framework. DuCCA builds upon coordinate attention and introduces three key designs: directional long-range modeling to preserve structural details, cross-direction coupling to suppress redundant activations between spatial branches, and a frequency-aware channel gate to adaptively modulate feature responses according to frequency-domain statistics. These mechanisms collectively improve the robustness and discriminative capability of visible features, providing more reliable complementary information for multimodal fusion. Experiments on the DroneVehicle dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, achieving 78.1 mAP, which validates that enhancing visible complementary representations can significantly improve RGB-IR cross-modal detection performance.

title: Further Results on the Fully Actuated System Approach to Tracking Control of Cart-Pole Pendulum System
authors: Haowen Liu, Mingjun Li, Ping Li, Guangren Duan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This study presents further results on the fully actuated system (FAS) approach to the tracking control of the cart-pole pendulum system. Unlike our previously proposed fully actuated model, this study constructs a new alternative FAS and derives a feasible trajectory for the underactuated cart-pole pendulum. This development greatly simplifies both the control-law design and the stability analysis, ultimately establishing local asymptotic tracking of the desired trajectory. A series of comparative simulations and robustness verifications is conducted to confirm the effectiveness of the proposed control framework.

title: A C-Band Parallel Coupled High Gradient Accelerating Structure
authors: Jibran Latif, Qiyu Kong, Ahong Li, Xingguang Liu, Yun Wang, Xiaolei Wu, Huachang Liu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: A C-band parallel-coupled high gradient accelerating structure is presented for operation with short power pulses. The core design principle involves individually feeding each cavity cell through a parallel network, thereby eliminating the sequential power transit time between cells and drastically reducing the overall structure filling time. The cavity geometry is optimized to maximize shunt impedance, while a dedicated parallel RF feed network comprising coupling irises, distribution waveguides, and a power divider is designed to deliver power effectively to each cavity cell with same amplitude and proper phase advance. The structure is designed in an overcoupled condition to minimize filling time, fully exploiting the short input pulse. Simulations demonstrate that this design achieves an accelerating gradient of $80 \mathrm{MVm}^{-1}$ for an input pulse of 150 MW and 50 ns. Keywords

title: Vacuum-Channel Silicon Edge Emitter for Light-Assisted Electron Emission
authors: Yudi Shi, Anika Tabassum Priyoti, Juan Sanchez Vazquez, Rehan Kapadia
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This work demonstrates a Hot Electron Light Assisted Cathode (HELAC) inspired vacuum-channel silicon edge emitter that enables emission directly from a Si edge exposed to vacuum, avoiding vertical transport through an insulating layer. Under high vacuum and edge-focused optical excitation, the device exhibits an electronically tunable photoemission process that is measurable across the vacuum gap. Optical modulation experiments demonstrate that the emission current tracks optical switching, indicating potential for high-speed modulation and further optimization.

title: Spectrum Aware Illumination Estimation Using Multispectral Image_supp2-3701975.pdf
authors: Jewon Kang
publish: N/A
abstract: Multispectral (MS) imaging extends beyond conventional RGB imaging by capturing more spectral bands, thereby improving illuminant spectrum estimation (ISE). However, existing methods often fail to fully exploit spectral information, resulting in suboptimal performance under diverse lighting conditions and across different sensor domains. Hence, we propose a deep learning framework with a spatio-spectral feature extraction block, which incorporates spectral attention mechanisms to enhance spectral correlation and preserve illuminant-relevant spatial features. Through the inclusion of an illuminant prior (IP), our approach prioritizes specific channels that provide more meaningful information in an MS image. We also propose a spectral-domain transform across different MS sensor spaces. The results demonstrate that illuminant spectra learned in high-dimensional sensor spaces can be effectively transformed to various lower-dimensional camera sensor spaces without any additional training. To facilitate evaluation, we introduce a real-world MS dataset containing high-dimensional ground-truth illumination spectra captured under diverse lighting conditions. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our method achieves superior accuracy compared to existing models, thus providing a practical solution for real-world ISE. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/hyejin5/Spectrum-Aware-Illumination-Estimation-Using-Multispectral-Image.

title: A Multimodal Model for Fault Diagnosis in Induction Motors Using Thermal Images and Large Language Models
authors: Luiz Fillipe Dahmer Dos Santos, Jorge Luiz Dos Santos Canuto, Linnyer Beatrys Ruiz Aylon, Viviana Cocco Mariani, Rodrigo Clemente Thom De Souza
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This study addresses the challenge of reliable and interpretable fault diagnosis in induction motors, a critical requirement for reducing unplanned downtime and maintenance costs in industrial systems. While deep learning models applied to infrared thermography achieve high diagnostic accuracy, they typically lack interpretability and integration with domain knowledge, limiting their practical adoption. To overcome this limitation, we propose a modular and sequential multimodal framework that combines convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for thermal image classification with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Large Language Models (LLMs) for knowledge-grounded, prescriptive reporting. A thermographic dataset comprising 1,779 images across six operating conditions was developed under controlled conditions. Multiple architectures were evaluated, with ShuffleNet V2 achieving the best trade-off between performance and efficiency, reaching 99.89% mean accuracy in 5-fold cross-validation and 99% accuracy on a held-out test set, while maintaining low computational complexity. The classification outputs were integrated with a structured technical knowledge base, enabling LLMs to generate standardized diagnostic reports aligned with industrial maintenance practices. Evaluation by eleven domain experts indicated over 90% agreement in technical accuracy, clarity, and decision support usefulness. Additionally, semantic similarity analysis demonstrated strong alignment between retrieved knowledge and generated reports (mean similarity up to 0.78), highlighting the effectiveness of the RAG mechanism in grounding model outputs. The results indicate that decoupling perception and reasoning enhances interpretability, scalability, and deployment flexibility, supporting the development of practical AI-assisted diagnostic systems for Industry 4.0 and 5.0 environments.

title: Cognitive Spectrum Sharing in Space-Air-Ground Integrated Networks: Optimizing Beamforming and AAV Trajectories with Two-Layer RSMA
authors: Mingyang Wang, Dawei Wang, Hongbo Zhao, Yixin He, Xiao Tang, Fuhui Zhou, Zhongxiang Wei, Li Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: The realization of space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGIN) is currently impeded by significant spectrum scarcity and complex cross-layer interference. To tackle these challenges, we propose a novel cognitive hierarchical spectrum sharing framework, wherein the satellite network operates as the primary network and high-altitude platforms (HAPs) and autonomous aerial vehicles (AAVs) collaboratively function as the secondary network. Based on this architecture, we propose a two-layer rate-splitting multiple access scheme that orchestrates the synergy between the wide-area coverage of HAPs and the flexible mobility of AAVs. This scheme robustly manages both intra-layer and inter-layer interference, thereby satisfying the heterogeneous quality-of-service requirements of terrestrial users. To maximize the system sum rate, a joint optimization problem is formulated for transmit beamforming and AAV trajectories, constrained by strict satellite interference temperature limits. To address this non-convex problem, we propose a deep iterative beamforming algorithm that leverages successive convex approximation and deep deterministic policy gradient techniques. Simulation results indicate that the proposed scheme achieves up to 25% higher throughput than conventional zero-forcing and non-orthogonal multiple access schemes for SAGIN.

title: Sliding Mode Robust Control of a Novel Tendon-Sheath Driven Knee Exoskeleton Based on Feedforward Compensation
authors: Hui Zhu, Huikun Hao, Jian Zhao, Qi Wang, Zonghe Guo, Mingxing Yang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: To tackle the issues of intricate structure and poor kinematic synergy of traditional lower limb exoskeletons, a novel compliant tendon-sheath actuated knee exoskeleton is proposed for assisted walking. Considering the simplicity and remote power transmission merits of the tendon-sheath transmission system, the mechanical structure of knee joint exoskeleton is designed by integrating the concept of dispersed load. Besides, the tendon-sheath driven actuator mimics muscle contraction and realizes compliant driving performance by adjusting the compression of intra-articular serial springs. Based on the Coulomb friction model, the input/output transmission model of the double tendon-sheath actuator system is derived. Furthermore, a nonlinear sliding mode robust control scheme based on feedforward compensation is developed and its corresponding stability is demonstrated by utilizing Lyapunov theory. Prototype experiments were carried out to validate the feasibility of the tendon-sheath actuator and evaluate the gait assistance performance of the knee exoskeleton. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed controller can track desired trajectory effectively under different conditions, confirming that the knee exoskeleton is capable of completing satisfactory assisted walking movements.

title: ProtoGEN: A Prototype-Guided Emotion Network for Multimodal Speech Emotion Recognition
authors: Levon Avetisyan, Shahane Tigranyan, Aram Avetisyan
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Speech Emotion Recognition in Russian remains underexplored compared to English, largely due to limited multimodal resources, heterogeneous annotation schemes, and the absence of strong baselines across datasets. To address these challenges, we propose a unified bimodal audio-text architecture that integrates speech representations with text embeddings and a cross-modal Co-Attention fusion module. Central to the framework is an auxiliary prototype-based objective that introduces trainable class-specific emotion prototypes and aligns acoustic embeddings with these anchors through cosine-based classification. While prototype-based representation learning has been explored previously in emotion recognition, we adapt this formulation to multimodal Russian SER and investigate its effectiveness as a stable auxiliary supervision mechanism for heterogeneous low-resource datasets. This objective provides an additional structural signal for the audio encoder without relying on batch-dependent pair construction. We evaluate the system on four diverse Russian datasets: RESD, REPV, SCES-1, and Dusha, covering both acted and spontaneous speech from adult and child speakers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed model achieves strong and consistent improvements over existing baselines, with multimodal fusion yielding substantial gains on datasets containing informative transcripts, whereas the audio-only variant remains competitive on lexically constrained corpora. Ablation studies further highlight the effectiveness of the auxiliary prototype-based objective. Overall, this work provides one of the first systematic multimodal evaluations across multiple Russian SER datasets and demonstrates the effectiveness of prototype-guided auxiliary supervision in this setting.

title: W-Diffusion: A Probabilistic Generative Framework for Traffic Forecasting via Shallow Diffusion Mechanism
authors: Zhepu Yang, Shuangxun Ma, Tao Dai, Peixin Chen, Ruixuan Zhang, Daniel Jian Sun, Xiangmo Zhao, Shirui Pan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Timely-accurate traffic forecasting is crucial for urban traffic guidance and control. Accurate forecasting of traffic flow and speed are essential for analyzing road usage, alleviating congestion, and enabling real-time monitoring and dynamic navigation. These capabilities contribute to enhanced road safety and more efficient infrastructure operation. Most existing methods neglect the inherent uncertainties within the traffic scenario, which reduces their practicality for real-world traffic control. The newly emerged denoising diffusion probabilistic models bring new inspiration to address this issue. However, these models only involve a single generation process, which is not appropriate for traffic forecasting task. Furthermore, the impact of Gaussian noise in generative process brings more challenges for modeling signal correlations. To overcome these limitations, we present the first attempt to introduce the shallow diffusion mechanism into traffic forecasting task, leading to a novel framework called W-Diffusion. The proposed model consists of separated shallow and deep diffusion stages, in order to better capture the signal correlations during the generative process. We also devise a new denoising network AST-Unet, to combine the spatiotemporal learning capabilities of spatiotemporal graph neural networks with the uncertainty measurements of diffusion models. Comprehensive experimental evaluations are conducted on the Caltrans Performance Measurement System (PeMS) benchmarks to validate the effectiveness of the proposed model.

title: Discrete Trajectory Tracking Control of Planar Cable-Actuated Platform via Recurrent Neural Network With Disturbance Rejection
authors: Yang Shi, Yilong Peng, Shuai Li, Xinwei Cao, Choo-Yee Ting, Jiyun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Conventional discrete-time recurrent neural networks (RNNs) often suffer from limited tracking accuracy and inadequate disturbance rejection when dealing with time-variant disturbances in the control of planar cable-actuated platform. To address these challenges, this article proposes a novel four-step discrete-form integral-reinforcing recurrent neural network (DF-IR-RNN) approach for disturbance-rejection trajectory tracking control. By incorporating an integral-reinforcing mechanism into the discrete RNN error dynamics, the proposed approach enhances disturbance-rejection capability and improves steady-state tracking precision. The effectiveness of the proposed DF-IR-RNN approach is first validated through numerical simulations under various disturbance conditions on a planar cable-actuated platform. Furthermore, physical experiments are conducted on the platform to further verify the effectiveness and practical feasibility of the proposed approach. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves high trajectory tracking accuracy and robust disturbance rejection compared to conventional neural network approaches. This work provides a practical and effective solution for discrete-time control of planar cable-actuated platform in real-world applications.

title: PurifyFD: Mining Latent Domains for Trustworthy Single-Domain Fault Diagnosis Under Open-World Shifts_supp1-3696400.pdf
authors: liuyang Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Single-domain generalization (SDG) is critical for Industrial Internet of Things edge scenarios where only limited single-source data are available. Existing SDG approaches predominantly rely on generative models or heavy augmentation to synthesize auxiliary domains, which often introduce unrealistic artifacts and training instability. In contrast, we articulate that single-source industrial data inherently contain latent nonstationarities (e.g., temporal drifts) that can be mined rather than synthesized. Based on this insight, we propose PurifyFD, a trustworthy and edge-efficient framework that reframes SDG as a feature purification problem. Specifically, we devise a closed-loop max–min adversarial game: the maximization step automatically amplifies the worst-case discrepancies of mined latent domains, while the minimization step suppresses feature sensitivity to these shifts via spatiotemporal disentanglement. This process yields purified representations with high intra-class compactness and shift-invariance. Rather than relying on heavy auxiliary out-of-distribution modules, the resulting compact representation naturally supports lightweight parameter-free energy scoring for more reliable open-set rejection in edge deployment. Extensive experiments on case western reserve university (CWRU), JNU, and a dual-testbed dataset demonstrate that PurifyFD achieves a favorable trade-off among cross-domain accuracy, open-set reliability, and inference efficiency compared to state-of-the-art representative methods.

title: One to Rule Them All: Reinforcement Learning Policy Selection with Closed-Loop Sensitivity_supp1-3703237.mp4
authors: Andrea Pupa
publish: N/A
abstract: In reinforcement learning-based control, reliable zero-shot transfer from simulation to real hardware remains a major open challenge. The offline training phase typically produces multiple models, each corresponding to a different policy, and choosing which model to deploy on physical hardware is a critical decision. However, there is no established methodology for this selection process. A common practice is to deploy the policy that achieves the highest reward in simulation, without evaluating its robustness to uncertainties. As a result, due to the sim-to-real gap, the selected policy may perform poorly when transferred to a physical robotic system, potentially leading to unsafe or undesirable behavior. This work proposes to exploit the notion of “closed-loop sensitivity” for applying it, for the first time, to the analysis and comparison of different reinforcement learning models, i.e., different policies, and to estimate their expected performance on real hardware. The proposed metric captures how the closed-loop system reacts to modeling errors, providing a quantitative measure of policy robustness. Both simulation results and real world experimental campaigns demonstrate that the proposed approach enables reliable zero-shot transfer to a real robot, while effectively handling uncertainties and reducing the risk of unsafe behaviors.

title: Physics-Informed Optimal Placement of Receivers for Indoor Localization via IQ Snapshots
authors: Melikcan Ölgün, Levent Ucun
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Indoor Angle-of-Arrival (AoA) localization performance is affected not only by the signal-processing algorithm but also by the placement of receivers with respect to walls, obstacles, and material-dependent propagation effects. In practical deployments, manually selected receiver locations may lead to redundant hardware, weak geometric diversity, and increased localization error. This paper presents a simulation-driven, physics-informed framework for optimizing the placement of Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) AoA receivers equipped with a 4x4 Uniform Rectangular Array (URA). The proposed framework synthesizes in-phase/quadrature (IQ) snapshots under free-space, wall-aware, and reflection-aware propagation regimes, using wall thickness information and material-dependent parameters based on International Telecommunication Union Radiocommunication Sector (ITU-R P.2040). Receiver subsets are selected by genetic algorithm using beamforming-derived fitness function defined as the ratio of cumulative local received-power variance to mean received power over transmitter test points. The simulator is evaluated by comparing synthetic and measured IQ snapshots processed with the same Multiple Signal Classification (MUSIC) estimator, showing consistent dominant pseudo-spectrum behavior under line-of-sight, obstructed, and reflection-affected conditions. The optimized layouts are then compared with a corner-based manual baseline in two office environments under −5 dB and +5 dB signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions. The results show that the optimized placements consistently reduce fitness values and positioning errors. The observed positive correlation between the proposed fitness value and root mean square error (RMSE) further supports its use as a practical surrogate objective for receiver-placement optimization. These results indicate that physics-informed IQ snapshot synthesis can support more reliable and practical AoA receiver placement in complex indoor environments.

title: Continuous Super-Resolution of Copernicus Atmosphere Images Using a Graph-Based Diffusion Model
authors: Angelo Casolaro, Massimiliano Giordano Orsini, Vincenzo Capone, Marco Lettiero, Thierry Bouwmans, Anastasia Zakharova, Francesco Camastra
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Image super-resolution aims to reconstruct high-resolution images from low-resolution inputs and plays a critical role in remote sensing. In environmental monitoring, particularly air quality assessment, super-resolution is essential for producing fine-scale pollutant concentration maps, since datasets, such as Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service, generally provide coarse spatial resolution, limiting the capture of localized variations. In this context, this study proposes the Improved Graph-based Implicit Diffusion Model (IGIDM). It is a diffusion probabilistic framework, combining Implicit Diffusion Model with the novel Superpixel-based Graph Attention Network, to enable continuous-scale super-resolution prediction with an associated uncertainty estimation. Moreover, a novel Extended-Scale Optimization Strategy is incorporated to guide the super-resolution process, improving generalization and perceptual fidelity, particularly under extreme scaling factors. Experimental validations are conducted on publicdomain benchmarks properly dedicated to air quality super-resolution. The proposed model achieves competitive reconstruction accuracy compared with state state-of-the-art discrete super-resolution methods and substantially outperforms probabilistic baselines. Notable gains are observed when the Extended-Scale Optimization Strategy is employed to leverage ground-station data. Furthermore, IGIDM provides well-calibrated uncertainty estimates across both standard and extreme scaling scenarios. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed super-resolution model is a robust framework for enhancing environmental datasets, providing more reliable and fine-grained air quality measurements than its direct competitors.

title: YOLO Based IoT Tracking for Academic Labs on Raspberry Pi
authors: Tamakorn Jiamrachada, Sarayut Nonsiri, Pichitchai Kamin, Nathaphon Nanthajirapong
publish: Proceedings of the 14th International Conference on Advances in Information Technology
abstract: None

title: Organizational Support as a Mediator: Understanding Security Behavior Toward Network-Connected Printers in the Workplace
authors: Nirachara Chuenjai, Tuul Triyason
