title: A Fully Actuated System Approach to Tracking Control for Bridge Crane System
authors: Tianyou Xu, Zhijie Li, Xianbo Hua, Jian Zhang, Wei Liu, Zelong Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel trajectory tracking control framework for underactuated bridge crane systems based on fully actuated system (FAS) theory. The dynamic model of the crane system is first established using Lagrangian mechanics and subsequently transformed into a second-order FAS representation through differential homeomorphic transformation. Based on this model, a tracking controller is designed to systematically compensate for inherent nonlinearities while ensuring precise trajectory tracking. The closed-loop system's eigenstructure can be arbitrarily assigned using parametric approaches, guaranteeing asymptotic stability and enhanced robustness. Compared with conventional linearization-based control strategies, the proposed method demonstrates superior performance in simulation, particularly in handling large-range motions. Simulation implementation results confirm the effectiveness and engineering feasibility of the proposed control scheme for real-world crane operations.

title: “Cold” and “Warm” Computer Simulations of a Standard, WR4-Based, Sinusoidally Corrugated, 220 GHz Slow-Wave Structure
authors: Andrey D. Andreev, Alexander Glick, Natalie B. Kostinski, Brian R. Poole, Edl Schamiloglu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: The cooperative effort between the University of New Mexico (UNM) and the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory (LLNL) on designing and prototyping a rectangular, sinusoidally corrugated, slow-wave structure (SWS) intended to operate in the millimeter-wave frequency range at 220 GHz is continued. The results below describe the effort toward the 220 GHz SWS design transition from the custom, not-standard rectangular-waveguide-based SWS to a standard, WR4-based SWS, abbreviated below as the WR4-SWS. The “cold” dispersion diagram of the lowest, fundamental $T E_{10}$ mode supported by the WR4-SWS is calculated by SuperFish code and the “warm” particle-in-cell simulations of the WR4 SWS driven by a $20 \mathrm{keV}, 100 \mathrm{~mA}$ electron beam are done with ICEPIC code. Results of simulations demonstrate strong, 100 W with $5 \%$ efficiency, sub-THz power generation at frequency $\sim 266 \mathrm{GHz}$ instead of the expected $\sim 220 \mathrm{GHz}$. The following analysis of both the “cold” and “warm” calculated results shows that the $20 \mathrm{keV}, 100 \mathrm{~mA}$ electron beam driving the WR4-SWS couples not the desired back-ward-traveling wave (BTW) of the $T E_{10}$ mode, but the forward-traveling wave (FTW) of the same lowest, fundamental $T E_{10}$ mode. The subsequent decision has been made to redesign the WR4-SWS into the standard, WR3-based SWS instead, or WR3-SWS.

title: Robust 3D Object Detection via Transfer-Learning Driven 2D-to-3D Fusion in Autonomous Loader Environments
authors: Dalong Jiang, Maoliang Yin, Jiannan Chen, Xi Luo
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Accurate environment perception is crucial for the autonomous operation of heavy machinery such as wheel loaders. However, acquiring extensive 3D annotations in complex work sites is exceedingly costly and time-consuming. This paper proposes a robust domain adaptation strategy to train a high-precision instance segmentation network specifically for challenging construction environments. The resulting model, developed through a mixed-dataset transfer learning approach, produces highfidelity masks that are critical for enabling accurate 3D object detection without requiring 3D labels. Our methodology begins by adapting a pre-trained YOLOv11-seg model on a small, custom-annotated dataset. The resulting high-quality masks are then employed in a 2D-to-3D transformation pipeline, where they serve as a precise filter on aligned depth maps to extract clean, object-specific depth information for geometric projection. Experimental results demonstrate that the enhanced accuracy of our domain-adapted segmentation network is directly responsible for achieving reliable 3D detection of key objects (e.g., trucks, personnel) in demanding loader environments.

title: Descriptor: Bangla Audio Spoofing With Four Conditions and Real Speech (BAS4R)
authors: Al Arian Ahmad, Nakib Aman, Durjoy Kumar Dutta, Md. Abdul Wadud
publish: IEEE Data Descriptions
abstract: This paper introduces BAS4R, a large-scale, multi-condition Bangla speech dataset designed for gender-aware real and spoofed voice analysis under diverse acoustic and signal manipulation scenarios. Although Bangla is one of the most widely spoken languages worldwide, publicly available datasets supporting robust voice authenticity verification and spoofing detection research remain limited. To address this gap, BAS4R comprises speech recordings collected from 200 native Bangla speakers, including 110 male and 90 female participants, representing diverse age groups. The inclusion of multiple age groups captures age-related acoustic variability, including pitch variation, physiological changes in vocal fold characteristics, voice strength differences, and spectral diversity, thereby reflecting natural vocal variation across the population. The dataset is organized into four structured classes-Female Real, Female Spoofed, Male Real, and Male Spoofed-captured under multiple real-world degradation and replay-based manipulation conditions. In total, the corpus contains approximately 143.88 hours of audio, including 5.75 hours of clean authentic speech, while the remaining data consist of systematically generated spoofed samples. Spoofing conditions include channel-based distortions, such as telephone and VoIP transmission, replay-based attacks, signal-processing transformations, including pitch shift, formant shift, and time stretch, and effect-based modifications, such as robotic and monster-like voice alterations. All audio files are systematically organized and consistently labeled to support reproducible experimentation. By relying exclusively on physical replay devices and real communication channels without synthetic text-to-speech augmentation, BAS4R preserves authentic device- and channel-induced acoustic artifacts. The dataset supports research in voice anti-spoofing, spoofing detection, gender-aware classification, and robust speaker verification, and aims to serve as a foundational resource for secure and trustworthy Bangla speech processing research.

title: Masthead
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Robotics & Automation Magazine

title: CapSpeech: Enabling Downstream Applications in Style-Captioned Text-to-Speech
authors: Helin Wang, Jiarui Hai, Dading Chong, Karan Thakkar, Tiantian Feng, Dongchao Yang, Junhyeok Lee, Thomas Thebaud, Laureano Moro-Velázquez, Jesús Villalba, Zengyi Qin, Shrikanth Narayanan, Mounya Elhiali, Najim Dehak
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Recent advancements in generative artificial intelligence have significantly transformed the field of style-captioned text-to-speech synthesis (CapTTS). However, adapting CapTTS to real-world applications remains challenging due to the lack of standardized, comprehensive datasets and limited research on downstream tasks built upon CapTTS. To address these gaps, we introduce CapSpeech, a new benchmark designed for a series of CapTTS-related tasks, including style-captioned text-to-speech synthesis with sound events (CapTTS-SE), accent-captioned TTS (AccCapTTS), emotion-captioned TTS (EmoCapTTS), and text-to-speech synthesis for chat agent (AgentTTS). CapSpeech comprises over 10 million machine-annotated audio-caption pairs and nearly 0.36 million human-annotated audio-caption pairs. In addition, we introduce two new datasets collected and recorded by a professional voice actor and experienced audio engineers, specifically for the AgentTTS and CapTTS-SE tasks. Alongside the datasets, we conduct comprehensive experiments using both autoregressive and non-autoregressive models on CapSpeech. Our results demonstrate high-fidelity and highly intelligible speech synthesis across a diverse range of speaking styles. To the best of our knowledge, CapSpeech is the largest available dataset offering comprehensive annotations for CapTTS-related tasks. The experiments and findings further provide valuable insights into the challenges of developing CapTTS systems.

title: Lightweight Neural Dynamics with Adaptive Grid Scaling for Real-Time AUV Path Planning
authors: Ning Zhao, Song Han, Jiaao Zhao
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper addresses the real-time path planning problem for autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) operating in dynamic three-dimensional underwater environments. We propose a Lightweight Dynamic Grid Neural Dynamics Method (LDGNM) that employs an event-triggered dynamic grid scaling mechanism to adaptively adjust the spatial resolution of the planning space based on local obstacle density. This approach aims to reduce computational load by focusing refinement only on regions of interest, such as areas near obstacles. Additionally, a neuron activity inheritance mechanism maintains planning continuity and gradient stability during resolution transitions. The proposed method is evaluated in simulations under various scenarios, demonstrating its capability to achieve efficient path planning with balanced performance in computational time, path quality, and safety.

title: IEEE Sensors Journal Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Characterization and Computation of Feedback Nash Equilibria in Scalar Discounted N-Player Linear Quadratic Games
authors: Chiara Cavalagli, Alberto Bemporad, Mario Zanon
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: This paper studies feedback Nash equilibria (FNE) in scalar discounted linear quadratic (LQ) games with N players. By explicitly incorporating the discount factor, we show that finite-cost equilibria may fail to stabilize the original system, motivating a distinction between FNE and stable FNE together with a sufficient stability condition. Based on a parametric characterization of the policies, we propose numerical methods for computing all equilibria. Particular attention is devoted to the symmetric game, where a closed-form expression of the symmetric FNE and conditions for the existence of up to M ≤ 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">N</sup> − 2 equilibria are derived. Numerical experiments illustrate how equilibrium multiplicity depends on the game configuration and highlight the emergence of finite-cost non-stabilizing equilibria.

title: KinematicRL: A Sim-to-Real Reinforcement Learning Framework For Social Navigation With Kinodynamic Feasibility
authors: Zhiming Xu, Haodong Yang, Chengju Liu, Qijun Chen, Chenpeng Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has shown promise for social navigation, yet its real-world deployment remains hindered by a persistent sim-to-real gap arising from simplified first-order dynamics and context-specific human state estimation pipelines. This work presents a unified framework that addresses these limitations to produce dynamically feasible navigation policies suitable for real-world deployment. First, theoretical analysis reveals that tracking error between simulated and actual robot position decays exponentially with increased control order, motivating the use of higher-order control inputs as DRL action space. A second-order control formulation tailored to differential drive robots is developed, complemented by a stochastic iterative Linear Quadratic Regulator (iLQR) that pretrains the policy via a divergence minimization objective. Second, to avoid the added system complexity of camera-LiDAR fusion, a cluster-based human tracking pipeline using only 2D LiDAR is introduced. Human detections are associated according to both spatial proximity and velocity similarity, enabling reliable differentiation of nearby pedestrians and yielding stable velocity estimates through temporal aggregation. Third, we introduce an unbiased residual gating block to balance reaction- and memory-based behaviors while handling time-varying crowd sizes, both critical for social navigation. The resulting policy, KinematicRL, consistently improves kinematic performance and adapts to varying number of detected humans. Experiments in real-world environments demonstrate that, when combined with the proposed tracking pipeline, KinematicRL can be deployed on a real differential drive robot with minimal modifications.

title: Dual Event-Triggered Safe Adversarial Apprentice Game for Constrained Robot Systems
authors: Xuejie Que, Zhenlei Wang, Guanghao Su, Hongchao Song, Yanqi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Robotic operations in constrained workspaces are highly susceptible to collisions under unknown adversarial disturbances, primarily because existing cost functions fail to account for dynamic uncertainties. To ensure safety, the framework integrates predefined state and torque bounds. The framework then employs a minimax game between control inputs and adversarial disturbances to derive optimal real-time policies. Specifically, a nonlinear transformation function maps constrained states onto an unconstrained manifold. At the same time, torque constraints are embedded directly within the cost functional. Through remote imitation of expert behavior, the learner autonomously refines its policy-evaluation mechanism, thereby compensating for cost-function biases in adversarial environments. Moreover, a Dual Event-Triggered (DET) mechanism is synthesized to identify high-value expert demonstrations, effectively minimizing computational and communication overhead. Extensive simulation results validate the proposed approach, demonstrating superior convergence precision, resource efficiency, and robustness across diverse adversarial scenarios.

title: Momentum-Kan: Traffic Flow Forecasting Via Frequency-Disentangled Modulator and Energy-Driven KANs
authors: Zhihong Wang, Zhuoxuan Liang, Wei Li, Xinyan Zhang, Nebojsa Bacanin, Moustafa Youssef
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Real-world traffic time series exhibit intricate temporal dynamics characterized by entangled high-frequency and low-frequency components, posing significant challenges for accurate forecasting. Decomposing high and low-frequency information (in both time and frequency domains) is a natural approach. However, time- and frequency-domain analyses emphasize different characteristics of time series, while high-frequency information is often progressively smoothed as network depth increases. Consequently, applying a homogeneous modeling strategy to all frequency components may produce inaccurate representations. While Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) offer promising function approximation capabilities, their inherent smoothing effect in deep layers often leads to critical loss of high-frequency details. To address this limitation, we propose <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Momentum</small>-<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Kan</small>, a novel architecture integrating frequency-disentangled modulation and energy-driven mechanisms. Our framework features three key components: (1) A Frequency-Disentangled Modulator (FDM) dynamically balances high-frequency (local) and low-frequency (global) components through adaptive convolutional kernels modulated by traffic fluctuation intensity; (2) An Energy-driven Mechanism (EDM) leverages the energy ratio between Fourier and wavelet basis functions to establish heterogeneous temporal pattern modeling; (3) A momentum-based gating system iteratively refines representations through residual frequency recombination blocks. Theoretical analysis demonstrates our approach's superiority in preserving fine-grained temporal structures while maintaining global trend consistency. Extensive experiments on six real-world traffic datasets show <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Momentum</small>-<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Kan</small> achieves state-of-the-art performance with 12.4% MAPE reduction on PEMS-BAY compared to existing methods.

title: A Hybrid Four-Switch Inductor-First Quadratic Step-Down Converter With Soft Charging Operation of Flying Capacitor
authors: Yufei Sun, Zhitong Chen, Qiwei Zhang, Xiaoya Fan, Yanzhao Ma
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents an inductor-first quadratic step-down (IFQSD) converter that achieves a voltage conversion ratio of D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and a five-fold extension in the duty cycle compared with a conventional half-bridge converter. The continuous input current effectively alleviates electromagnetic interference (EMI) while maintaining an input current substantially lower than the load current, thereby reducing direct current resistance (DCR) losses. Additionally, the proposed converter achieves soft charging of the flying capacitor to reduce the losses caused by hard charging. Furthermore, an internal ripple compensation circuit is implemented to reduce output voltage ripple. To ensure proper operation of the power transistors under negative voltage conditions, a negative-voltage bootstrap circuit is proposed. The proposed converter is fabricated in a 0.18-μm Bipolar-CMOS-DMOS (BCD) process with a die area of 6.615 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. The experimental results validate that the proposed converter achieves an output voltage of 1 V with duty cycles of approximately 0.289 and 0.204 at input voltages of 12 V and 24 V, respectively. The implemented design achieves a peak power conversion efficiency of 90.4%.
