title: Preparation and Investigation of Composite M-type Cathodes
authors: Xianmei Chen, Yunfei Yang, Anran Jiang, Junhao Sun, Fei Liu, Jinshu Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: In this paper, a cathode with a $\mathrm{Re}_{3} W$ phase barrier layer was prepared, and its emission performance and mechanism were investigated. The results show that S1 cathode exhibits the optimal service life and evaporation rate performance, with a service life of up to $12,000 \mathrm{~h}$ and an evaporation rate of $1.56282 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~g} \cdot \mathrm{~cm}^{-2} \cdot \mathrm{~s}^{-} { }^{1}$ at a temperature of $1050^{\circ} \mathrm{C}_{b}$.TEM analysis indicates that the phase composition of the cathode remains a single HCP solid solution phase and $\mathrm{Re}_{3} W$ phase during longterm operation, which may be the key reason for the cathode’s excellent service life.

title: Prescribed Performance and Predefined-Time Control of Spacecraft Rendezvous Based on Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Minjuan Huang, Weizhen Liu, Guangren Duan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Aiming at solving the problems of strong nonlinearity, external disturbances and obstacle constraints in the circular orbit dynamics of the target spacecraft, this paper proposes a comprehensive control strategy that integrates the fully actuated system approach, prescribed performance control, artificial potential field and sliding mode control. Firstly, the nonlinear relative dynamics model is transformed into a linear system with configurable characteristic structure by using the fully actuated system approach. Secondly, the prescribed performance control is introduced, which ensures that the tracking error evolves within the preset boundaries. Further, the active obstacle avoidance is achieved by combining the artificial potential field, and a disturbance observer is designed to compensate for external disturbances. Finally, a sliding mode control law based on a predefined time and combined with an artificial potential function was derived. The stability of the closed-loop control system was analyzed using the Lyapunov method, and the effectiveness of the proposed control scheme was verified through numerical simulations.

title: Validation of the 2.5D Large-signal Code TESLA by Its Application to the Modeling of the MPP 45 MW Relativistic Klystron
authors: Igor A. Chernyavskiy, Alexander N. Vlasov, Rasheda Begum, Takuji Kimura, Brad Stockwell, Peter Kolda
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: We present results of our extensive validation study of the NRL 2.5D large-signal klystron code TESLA. Recently, an improved version of the code was applied to the modeling of an existing mildly relativistic klystron, which was manufactured and tested at MPP (formerly CPI, Palo Alto). The device produces 45 MW of peak output power at S-band and consists of five single-gap cavities. Due to its compactness, this advanced klystron design is characterized by a significant role of the 2D effects, including active radial motion of particles. Therefore, accurate modeling of this klystron is not possible with simple 1D or 1.5D algorithms, but requires the use of more advanced, fully 2D, computational tools. That is why this case provides a good validation test for the recently improved 2.5D algorithm of the code TESLA. In close collaboration with MPP, NRL has conducted a detailed study of this klystron design, utilizing extensive TESLA simulations to predict its performance and analyze sensitivity of output characteristics to a wide range of design parameters. The results of TESLA simulations have shown good agreement with the predictions of the Particle-in-Cell (PIC) code MAGIC-2D and actual test data.

title: Extremely Large-Scale Dynamic Metasurface Antennas for 6G Near-Field Networks: Opportunities and Challenges
authors: Haiyang Zhang, Nir Shlezinger, Giulia Torcolacci, Francesco Guidi, Anna Guerra, Qianyu Yang, Mohammadreza F. Imani, Davide Dardari, Yonina C. Eldar
publish: IEEE Vehicular Technology Magazine
abstract: Near-field technologies based on extremely large-scale (XL) arrays are a key enabler for future 6G networks. However, implementing XL-array systems using traditional fully digital or hybrid analog/digital architectures poses significant challenges due to high power consumption and implementation costs. Emerging XL-dynamic metasurface antennas (XL-DMAs) provide a promising alternative, enabling ultra-low power and cost-efficient solutions, making them ideal candidates for 6G near-field networks. In this article, we discuss the opportunities and challenges of XL-DMAs employed in 6G near-field networks. We first outline the fundamental principles of XL-DMAs and present the specifics of the near-field model of XL-DMAs. We then highlight several promising applications that might benefit from XL-DMAs, including near-field communication, localization, and imaging. Finally, we discuss several open problems and potential future directions that should be addressed to fully exploit the capabilities of XL-DMAs in the next 6G near-field networks.

title: Consensus-Based State Estimation for Stochastic Discrete-Time Systems With Poisson-Distributed Disturbances Under Directed Graphs
authors: Weiqiang Chen, Yu-Tian Xu, Jie Mei, Guangfu Ma
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this paper, distributed state estimation is investigated for discrete-time linear systems with stochastic disturbances and noise. Different from existing works, the disturbance is modeled as a Poisson-distributed process, which effectively captures the impulsive and stochastic characteristics of practical disturbances. Moreover, system and measurement noises are described as general stochastic processes without relying on Gaussian assumptions. Under these considerations, a consensus-based distributed observer is designed for joint state and disturbance estimation under directed topologies. Sufficient conditions are derived to ensure uniform mean-square boundedness of augmented estimation error.

title: DeepONet-Enabled Backstepping Output-Feedback Control for 1-D Reaction-Diffusion PDEs with Neumann Boundaries
authors: Chenyang Yu, Runsheng Guo
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This work develops a DeepONet-assisted output-feedback control framework for one-dimensional reaction-diffusion systems under Neumann boundary actuation. We derive the kernel equation via backstepping and use DeepONet to learn the associated operator mapping with quantified approximation error. A Lyapunov-based argument shows that exponential stability of the closed-loop dynamics is preserved when the exact kernel is replaced by its approximation, for both the full-state feedback design and the collocated boundary-observer output-feedback scheme. Simulations further confirm the effectiveness and computational efficiency of the proposed method.

title: Design of a Multistage Stage Depressed Collector for Q-band Traveling-Wave Tube
authors: Yuxin Wang, Shaomeng Wang, Duo Xu, Zhanliang Wang, Yuan Zheng, Yubin Gong
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: A three-stage depressed collector integrated with cooling fins is designed and optimized to improve the overall efficiency of a Q-band dielectric-supported symmetric dual meander-line (DS-SDML) traveling-wave tube (TWT) The design is based on the energy distribution of the spent electron beam after beam-wave interaction. The electrode geometry and potentials were optimized using electrostatic simulation software, with particular emphasis on suppressing secondary electron emission. Simulation results demonstrate that under the operating conditions of 39 GHz frequency, 9.3 kV beam voltage, 0.08 A beam current, and 84.9 W output power, the collector achieves an efficiency of 91.5%. This raises the total tube efficiency from approximately 10.2% without a depressed collector to 55.8%. The proposed design significantly improves the energy utilization of the tube and holds considerable value for practical engineering applications.

title: Online Learning-Based Distributionally Robust Nonlinear Predictive Control for High-Order Fully Actuated Systems
authors: Wenxuan Wang, Han Wang, Chao Ning
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel Distributionally Robust Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (DR-NMPC) framework for discrete-time High-Order Fully Actuated (HOFA) systems subject to additive stochastic disturbances and state constraints. Leveraging the inherent feedback linearizability of HOFA systems, its nonlinear dynamics is transformed into a linear representation, thereby circumventing computational burden. The proposed framework features the exploitation of online Bayesian learning via a Dirichlet Process Mixture Model (DPMM) to construct a data-stream-driven ambiguity set that captures the time-varying disturbance distribution without prior parametric assumptions. Within this framework, a constraint-tightening method based on distributionally robust Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) is developed to ensure robust satisfaction of chance constraints. Theoretical analysis rigorously establishes the recursive feasibility of the proposed online learning DR-NMPC and the closed-loop stability of the HOFA system, guaranteeing convergence to a robust positively invariant set. The computational results demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the DR-NMPC method for HOFA systems.

title: Preparation of Ni-Mo-Ru alloy solder and impact on cathode
authors: Zheng Liu, Yutong Zhang, Junhao Sun, Xianmei Chen, Yunfei Yang, Jinshu Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Ni-Mo-Ru ternary alloy solder were prepared, and brazed the dispenser cathode and Mo (molybdenum support barrel), and the evaporation performance of the cathode after using this solder were investigated. The results showed that the high-temperature evaporation rate of the cathode welding assembly using this alloy solder was 67% of that using pure Ni solder, which is $6.31 \times 10-8 \mathrm{~g} \cdot \mathrm{~cm}-2 \cdot \mathrm{~s}-1$. This effectively reduced the evaporation rate, ensuring the vacuum requirements during the application of vacuum electronic devices, and improving the stability of the components.

title: Research on Thermo-Electro-Structural Coupling Effects in Sheet Beam Electron Guns
authors: Pucheng Wang, Wei Jiang, Qianqian Chen, Chaoxuan Lu, Yong Luo, Jianxun Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: The sheet beam electron gun, serving as a core component of millimeter-wave/terahertz vacuum devices, faces a critical challenge where thermal expansion induces relative positional shifts among the cathode, focus electrode, and anode. This shift is a primary factor limiting the electron beam transmission efficiency ($\geqslant 95 \%$). This paper establishes a multi-physics coupled model to quantify the disturbance of thermal deformation on the beam waist position, proposes a co-simulation scheme, and provides theoretical support for the design of high-power electron guns.

title: IVEC 2026 Author Index
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)

title: Detection and Estimation in Power System via Robust Unknown Input Observer
authors: Chenxiang Xi, Xinyu Wang, Mingyue Zhan, Yuee Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: The growing digitalization of power grids has heightened their vulnerability to data-driven cyber-attacks, such as false data injection. Conventional detection methods are often challenged by model uncertainties, and unknown disturbances. This paper presents a novel framework for attack detection and estimation by integrating Fully Actuated System (FAS) theory with a robust unknown input observer. The approach first employs operational data to derive a dynamic model in FAS form, whose structure simplifies the observer design by directly relating state derivatives to current states and inputs. Based on this model, a novel robust unknown input observer is designed to decouple bounded unknown disturbances and accurately reconstruct attack signals. Theoretical stability and convergence of the estimation error are established. Simulations on IEEE test systems show that the proposed method outperforms conventional observers in detection sensitivity, false alarm rate, and real-time attack waveform estimation, offering a reliable solution for enhancing cyber-physical security in power systems.

title: A Field of View Extension Method Based on Programmable Metasurface for One-Dimensional Mirrored Aperture Synthesis
authors: Wentao Liu, Jian Dong, Chengwang Xiao, Xiaoyang Du, Haoyang Zhou, Wenjing Wang, Haofeng Dou
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Mirrored aperture synthesis (MAS) is an effective technique for high-resolution passive microwave remote sensing. However, it still faces problems such as a limited field of view (FOV) and degraded image quality due to missing reflected signals. To address this issue, this paper proposes an FOV extension method based on a programmable metasurface. By replacing the reflector with metasurface, incident waves can be redirected toward a desired direction. Thus, the angle range of the array received signal is improved, allowing the FOV to be extended without increasing the reflector size. To realize the above method, a 1-bit programmable metasurface operating around 6.6 GHz is designed. It can stably steer the reflected wave to approximately 30° over incident angles from 10° to 60°. This method provides a feasible new approach for extending the FOV of MAS radiometer under limited-space conditions.

title: Self-learning Digital Twin for Offshore Floating Platform Dynamics and Productivity
authors: Xiaoling Liang, Yueyi Chen, Bernard Voon Ee How, Shuzhi Sam Ge
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems
abstract: As offshore energy transitions to greener operations, turret-moored floating platforms increasingly function as advanced cyber-physical systems (CPSs) exposed to harsh seas and cyber-physical risks. This paper targets productivity and safety for turret-moored platforms with thruster-assisted heading control under time-varying environmental loads and potential cyber-physical anomalies. We develop an operational and control oriented self-learning digital twin that fuses physics based hydrodynamic modeling with online neural approximation to capture modeling errors and slow drifts during platform operation. Prediction residuals from the twin are used for lightweight anomaly monitoring, which provides early warning of abnormal sensor or actuator behavior and supports supervisory mitigation. Building on these predictions, an autonomous decision-support module optimizes the platform heading via a multi-factor performance index while adaptive controller maintains robust heading stabilization under bounded prediction residuals and advisory mismatch. Comprehensive simulations across three extreme multi-directional sea states and scaled basin experiments demonstrate substantial reductions in roll motion and improved heading regulation; the monitoring layer further provides actionable early warning for abnormal operation. The results show a practical pathway to monitoring, decision-making, and control for maritime energy CPSs.

title: An Integrated Architecture for Robust End-to-End Radar Multi-Target Detection in Jamming Environments
authors: Haoying Zhu, Kuo Liao, Jiaye Wang, Wanyou Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radar Systems
abstract: When the radar suffers from the repeater deception jamming, the echoes of each target and the jamming signals will produce serious aliasing in time, frequency and spatial domains, the real targets will be masked and the detection performance is extremely limited. To address this issue and ensure the target detection capability of the radar system in this complex jamming scenario, we propose an integrated end-to-end anti-jamming target detection architecture based on Multi-scale Refinement and Adaptive Modulation (MRAM-net), which realizes the end-to-end processing from the input of the original radar echo to the output of multi-target position information when the type of jamming is unknown and the number of targets is uncertain. This integrated architecture departs from the conventional multi-stage serial processing paradigm, thereby mitigating error accumulation inherent in sequential processing and significantly reducing the complexity and implementation overhead of the radar signal processing system. Firstly, we design the Selective Multi-scale Refinement Block (SMRB), which effectively improves the target feature representation capability and suppresses redundant information. Furthermore, we propose a Spectrogram-Adaptive-Modulated Global Aggregation (SAMGA) module, which utilizes time-frequency features as global prior information to guide and modulate the feature extraction process. Finally, we also propose a model training optimization strategy termed Bidirectional Spreading Learning (BSL), which avoids the model falling into local optima and further improves the accuracy of the model by bidirectional spreading training. Simulation results demonstrate that MRAM-net achieves outstanding end-to-end detection performance and generalization ability across diverse jamming scenarios and varying numbers of targets.

title: Low Order Approximation of Flexible structures
authors: S. Fatemeh Sharifi, Daniël W.M. Veldman, Hans Zwart
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: Control design for linear, time-invariant mechanical systems generally requires an accurate low-order approximation in the low frequency range. For example a series expansion of the transfer function around zero consisting of a mass, velocity, and compliance term. Because computing such a series expansion of the transfer function can be cumbersome, a new (time-domain) method to compute low-order approximations is developed. The method does not require an explicit expression of the transfer function, and its advantages are demonstrated by examples.

title: A Neural Network Direct Control of STATCOM: Design and Experimental Validation
authors: Gaoxiang Li, Taihua Huang, Xiao Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: With the development of artificial intelligence, neural network technology has been applied in the power system. In this letter, a neural network direct control of static synchronous compensator (STATCOM) is proposed, which can improve the dynamic and steady-state performance. Firstly, a simulation model of STATCOM under direct reactive power control is established, and the simulation data are collected as the training dataset for the neural network. Then, the neural network architecture and training algorithm are designed according to the input and output variables, and the neural network direct controller of STATCOM is obtained through training. Compared with direct reactive power control, the proposed neural network direct control can significantly improve the dynamic and steady-state performance of STATCOM due to the direct and fast input-output characteristics of the neural network. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed control strategy is verified through the experiments based on TMS320F28377 chip.

title: Shared-Kernel Wavelet Networks for Near-Sensor Poisson Image Reconstruction
authors: Yuanhao Gong, Tan Tang, Qianyan Liu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Near-sensor image processing can significantly reduce the signal redundancy. One typical and classical operator is the Laplacian, which leads to a sparse Laplacian field. Meanwhile, the original image can be uniquely reconstructed from its Laplacian field via solving a Poisson equation. Such uniqueness is mathematically guaranteed in theory. As a result, the sparse Laplacian field can represent the image. In this paper, we first numerically confirm that the Laplacian fields of hundreds images are sparse and satisfy a stable distribution. Then, we show that one image can be accurately reconstructed from its Laplacian field. For the reconstruction task, we propose a wavelet-guided convolution neural network, which has three advantages. First, thanks to the shared kernels, the network is compact. It has less than 0.0002M and 0.021M parameters for the noise-free and noisy input, respectively. Second, it has high performance. It has linear computational complexity, leading to a real-time reconstruction, even for high-resolution images. Third, it achieves a higher accuracy than previous methods. The proposed network can be enhanced with higher channel numbers, improving its accuracy and robustness. Several numerical experiments are conducted to show the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed network. The proposed method can be applied in a large range of applications such as image compression, low light enhancement, object tracking, etc. The code is available at http://github.com/gyh8/swnet with possible updates in the future.

title: Multimodal Motion Tracking: A Review
authors: Evans Aidoo, Oliver Custance, Edwin Kwadwo Tenagyei, Chuan Dai, Minsi Chen, Zhijie Xu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Motion tracking underpins applications in healthcare, sports, surveillance, and smart environments, yet most existing surveys cover only two or three sensing modalities at a time. This paper reviews multimodal motion tracking across all four major modalities (radio frequency, optical, inertial, and magnetic), together with their preprocessing pipelines and fusion strategies. Drawing on more than 300 publications, we analyze fusion at the data, feature, and decision levels and link each choice to deployment constraints such as latency, privacy, and energy use. Three findings stand out. First, a preprocessing impact analysis built from published ablation studies shows that removing calibration or geometric alignment increases tracking error by 103% to 1900%, depending on modality and task. Second, benchmark datasets exhibit consistent gaps in demographic representation, environmental diversity, and synchronization documentation. Third, preprocessing quality and fusion strategy should be co-designed rather than chosen separately, since errors in one propagate into the other. We close with recommendations for next-generation dataset curation and outline open problems in privacy preservation, real-time processing, and cross-domain generalization.

title: Missingness-Aware Graph Learning for Collusion Detection in Public Procurement
authors: Zhangzhi Xu, Yanli Xu, Pengfei Huang, Delong Li, Yixiang Jiang, Qianhui Shen, Yunfei Cheng, Haicheng Tao
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Collusion in public procurement auctions can lead to sustained overpricing and create systemic distortions within the market. Although there exist studies on the effectiveness of detection methods using classifiers on the ``screens'' data, their practical deployment still faces two significant challenges. First, there is often incompleteness in the procurement data. Key features or even the features designed as the screens may not always be available. Second,the detection models are not transferable from one market to another. To address these challenges, we propossM2DG, aMissingness-aware andMulti-objective Domain-Generalizable graph learning framework built on a relation-aware graph Transformer. Specifically, M2DG constructs a hierarchical heterogeneous graph to facilitate missingness- and uncertainty-aware propagation. In this way, missingness patterns can dynamically modulate attention biases and message weights. Moreover, M2DG learns node-level message reliability gates to suppress unreliable messages during graph aggregation. It then uses an evidential head to output calibrated risk scores together with uncertainty estimates. Finally, it reduces over-reliance on market-specific shortcuts by incorporating domain generalization objectives for superior zero-shot and few-shot transfer across different jurisdictions. Extensive experiments on multi-market procurement benchmarks show that M2DG consistently improves in-domain detection, robustness to generic and procurement-aware missingness, calibration quality, and cross-market transferability.

title: A Polarization Modulation-Based Method for Generating High-Quality Ultra-Wideband Signals
authors: Chengyu Wang, Jinhang Li, Jingran Zhou
publish: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters
abstract: We propose a novel method for generating and transmitting ultra-wideband (UWB) pulse signals using polarization modulation. The combination of polarization controller(PC), polarization beam splitter(PBS), Mach-Zehnder Modulator(MZM), and polarizer(POL) form a carrier-canceling UWB signal generator. The generated centimeter-wave and millimeter-wave ultra-wideband signals have bandwidths up to 7.2GHz and 6.2GHz respectively, meeting the regulatory requirements of the Federal Communications Commission (FCC). At the receiver side, the system has a receiver sensitivity of -23.3 dBm with a forward error correction threshold(FEC) of 3.8 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> for high-speed wireless communication applications.

title: Cascade Model Predictive Control for AUV Trajectory Tracking Based on Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Haodong Kang, Tailai Cao, Ziwen Yang, Wenbin Yu, Shanying Zhu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the trajectory tracking problem of Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) subject to input constraints. Based on the Fully Actuated System Approach (FASA), a cascade model predictive controller is proposed to accomplish the trajectory tracking control. First, a Higher-Order Fully Actuated (HOFA) system model is formulated by incorporating the kinematics and dynamics of the AUV. Subsequently, a model predictive controller featuring a two-layer optimization architecture is designed. The first layer addresses the complex nonlinear constraints introduced by nonlinear state transformations, while the second layer computes the optimal control sequence to drive the AUV along the reference trajectory. The proposed controller guarantees the asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system and significantly reduces the computational complexity of the optimization process. Finally, simulation experiments are conducted to verify the effectiveness of the proposed trajectory tracking control strategy.

title: Command Filter-Based Fixed-Time Tracking Control for Nonlinear Systems with Full-State Constraints
authors: Zhenlian Sun
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a fixed-time command-filtered control scheme for nonlinear systems with full-state constraints. In the controller design process, state constraints are addressed using a barrier Lyapunov function (BLF). A fixed-time controller is formulated by integrating fixed-time techniques with backstepping methodology. To circumvent the issue of “explosion of complexity” inherent in backstepping, the control scheme incorporates fixed-time command filters (FTCFs). By employing compensators, the filtering errors introduced by FTCFs can be mitigated. Rigorous analysis confirms fixed-time stability in the closed-loop system. Simulation results further confirm the efficacy of the proposed approach.

title: Dynamics of a Spin-Wave Active Ring Resonator Driven by Harmonic-Null Square-Wave and Unipolar 8-bit Walsh Code Modulations
authors: Anirban Mukhopadhyay, Kaustubh Narayan, Anil Prabhakar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Spin-wave active ring resonators (SWARRs) based on yttrium iron garnet (YIG) films exhibit rich nonlinear dynamics, making them promising platforms for physical reservoir computing. We present systematic and experimentally simple methods to characterize a SWARR’s nonlinear behavior and fading memory. First, a drive frequency fd is modulated by a square-wave pattern engineered to have a spectral null at 3/T; this modulated signal is then applied to the SWARR. The power spectra at the output of the YIG delay line allow us to identify five distinct drive frequency ranges where nonlinearity appears as frequency peaks at fd±3/T. The short-term memory (STM) duration of the SWARR is estimated to be approximately 300 ns using a modulation pattern derived from the sequency-ordered 8-bit unipolar Walsh family. The nonlinear dynamics of the SWARR are further quantified by decomposing its temporal response to analog Walsh pulses in terms of the input Walsh codewords. The proposed methods provide a practical and general framework for the design and optimization of tunable spin-wave reservoir computers.

title: Explainable Tensor-Enhanced Knowledge Graph Rule Learning With Inverse Tucker Decomposition for Robust Link Prediction
authors: Xiangli Yang, Ao Liu, Yan Lu, Jing Yang, Na Song, Runbo Zhang, Jieming Yang, Xiaokang Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Knowledge Graph (KG) reasoning is a pivotal technique in modern artificial intelligence for unlocking latent knowledge structures, such as inferring missing facts or supporting intelligent decision-making. However, real-world KGs frequently suffer from intrinsic sparsity and incompleteness. Restricted by the dichotomy between predictive power and transparency, embedding based methods often function as opaque black boxes, while symbolic approaches struggle to capture fine grained semantic interactions due to limited expressiveness. This deficiency hinders the deployment of reliable AI systems where interpretability is crucial. To address this, we propose Explainable Tensor enhanced Knowledge Graph Rule Learning (ETKRL), a unified and interpretable framework tailored for such complex reasoning scenarios. Our method, integrating a novel inverse tensor Tucker decomposition mechanism, leverages the inherent logic of symbolic rules to explicitly model deep level interactions, rather than relying solely on latent features. Furthermore, to ensure robust global inference, we introduce a joint training strategy that dynamically fuses triplet embeddings with rule representations based on their semantic validity. By jointly optimizing rule generation and embedding learning, ETKRL avoids the limitations of disjoint reasoning pipelines. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that ETKRL achieves state-of-the-art performance even under data scarcity, offering a trustworthy and explainable solution for high stakes knowledge discovery.

title: Multi-Task Learning for Subtropical Forest Canopy Height Mapping from Sentinel-2 Imagery and Airborne LiDAR
authors: Xindi Liu, Majid Nazeer, Xinyu Yu, Janet Elizabeth Nichol, Jin Wu, Amos P. K. Tai, Shao-Yuan Leu, Man Sing Wong
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate forest canopy height mapping over large areas is crucial for carbon stock estimations and ecosystem monitoring. However, optical imagery suffers from signal saturation in dense forests, while the high cost of airborne Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) restricts its availability. To address this challenge, we propose a multi-task learning (MTL) framework, evaluated across both the U-Net and the Swin Transformer U-Net (SwinUNet) backbone, that uses paired Sentinel-2 imagery and three airborne LiDAR-derived structural metrics (canopy cover, understory density, and height standard deviation) as inputs, and is trained to jointly predict canopy height alongside these structural metrics. By requiring the model to reconstruct these structural attributes, it is able to learn physical relationships between spectral reflectance and 3D forest structure. Through ablation experiments under both random-split (RS) and spatial block-split (BS) validation, we demonstrate that the transfer learning framework, which combines MTL pre-training with subsequent fine-tuning, consistently outperforms both the single-task baseline and the input-only alternative across both architectures. Under spatially independent BS validation, SwinUNet MTL achieves a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 1.411 m and Coefficient of Determination (R<sup>2</sup>) of 0.635 over subtropical forests in Hong Kong, a 30% error reduction relative to the spectral-only baseline, while UNet MTL achieves RMSE of 1.141 m and R<sup>2</sup> of 0.761, a 42% reduction. Of these gains, 27%/33% (SwinUNet/UNet) are attributable to LiDAR-derived input augmentation, and the remaining 3%/9% to the multi-task learning framework. To extend applicability when fresh LiDAR acquisition is unavailable, we further introduce a Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) based temporal weighting scheme that generates pseudo-fresh structural metrics from outdated Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS) data, achieving an RMSE of 2.23 m on an independent 2023 ground-truth dataset. Evaluated on identical validation patches, our locally trained model substantially outperforms the World Resources Institute (Meta/WRI) canopy height map (RMSE = 3.81 m), underscoring the value of locally calibrated models for precision forest monitoring.

title: Octopus-Inspired Embodied Tactile Intelligence: A Universally Calibrated Miniature Vision-Based Suction Cup_supp3-3694100.mp4
authors: Rob B.N. Scharff
publish: N/A
abstract: The integration of high-resolution tactile sensors in suction cups is a key requirement for the realization of octopus-inspired control strategies in soft continuum robotic arms. However, existing solutions lack simultaneous force and orientation detection and either provide insufficient resolution or are too bulky for integration into soft continuum robotic arms. Moreover, inconsistent manufacturing often necessitates labor-intensive individual calibration for each suction cup. This article introduces a miniature suction cup (22 mm × 40 mm) with integrated vision-based detection of pressing force and contact orientation. Through consistent multimaterial additive manufacturing and a robust model architecture trained on augmented, cross-device datasets, this predictive model can be universally applied across devices of identical design, thus eliminating the need for individual calibration of each suction cup. High-resolution and high-accuracy orientation [pitch/roll mean absolute error (MAE): 1.43/1.45 degrees] and force detection (Fx/Fy/Fz MAE: 0.254/0.256/0.763 N) enable closed-loop, perpendicular approaches for optimal sealing. Real-world tests confirm robust handling of small, delicate, misaligned, and curved objects. These results represent an important step toward scalable, high-resolution tactile sensing for octopus-inspired soft robotic manipulation.

title: Non-ideal Phase Error Compensation Based on Global-Aware 3-D U-Net for GNSS-InSAR
authors: Feifeng Liu, Jiayu Chen, Zhanze Wang, Zhixiang Xu, Guanqun Wang, Minghao Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: GNSS-InSAR employs ground-based fixed receivers to simultaneously capture scene reflection signals from multiple navigation satellites, enabling three-dimensional deformation monitoring. However, asymmetric multi-static configurations introduce various non-ideal interferometric phase errors, while the system’s relatively SNR and resolution further exacerbate the challenges of error compensation. This paper proposes a GNSS-InSAR non-ideal phase error compensation method based on a globally aware three-dimensional U-Net. To overcome the constraint of scarce training data, a simulated dataset was generated by modelling deformation processes through four temporal patterns and a six-parameter spatial model. Various non-ideal errors were incorporated into the dataset based on the constructed signal model. To achieve spatio-temporal coherence across the entire scene, the network incorporates a global-aware module, employs a 2D-3D hybrid encoding-decoding mechanism, and adopts an alternating training strategy that balances local extremum points with global accuracy. Experimental validation was conducted across natural slopes and highway bridges covered by eight BeiDou IGSO satellites. Measurement accuracy was assessed using differential GNSS positioning data, demonstrating the network’s exceptional performance within GNSS-InSAR systems. This study pioneers the application of a globally aware 3D U-Net for GNSS-InSAR phase error compensation, significantly enhancing three-dimensional deformation monitoring accuracy. It outperforms conventional methods in both landslide and bridge field data, demonstrating strong practical utility and scalability potential.

title: HSMMamba: Hierarchical Superpixel MoE with Mamba for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Cuiping Shi, Yiting Wang, Weiwei Sun, Chenyang Fu, Zhan Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral image classification aims to fully exploit spatial and spectral information for fine-grained land-cover recognition in complex scenes. However, existing methods still face two major challenges. On the one hand, traditional convolutional networks are ineffective at modeling long-range spatial–spectral dependencies, whereas self-attention-based models usually suffer from high computational complexity. On the other hand, although hierarchical superpixels provide effective multiscale structural priors, most existing methods rely on fixed cross-level mapping relationships and shared feature transformation strategies, making them inadequate for handling the pronounced heterogeneity among features at different scales. To address these issues, this article proposes a Hierarchical Superpixel Mixture-of-Experts Mamba network for hyperspectral image classification, termed HSMMamba. The proposed framework unifies hierarchical structural priors, adaptive mixture-of-experts enhancement, and global–local joint state-space modeling within a single architecture. Specifically, a learnable hierarchical superpixel mapping module is first developed to adaptively optimize cross-level downsampling and upsampling relationships by introducing learnable residuals on top of fixed hierarchical mapping priors, thereby enhancing the consistency of multilevel feature propagation and reconstruction. Then, a hierarchical superpixel mixture-of-experts module is introduced to perform differentiated modeling of pixel-level and multiscale superpixel-level features, and to adaptively fuse them through a gating mechanism, thus improving the representation capability of heterogeneous multilevel features. Finally, a forward–backward–differential Mamba module is constructed to preserve the strength of long-range dependency modeling while enhancing the characterization of local boundaries, fine-grained variations, and class transition regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HSMMamba achieves overall accuracies (OAs) of 98.98%, 99.30%, and 95.65% on the three public HSI datasets of Pavia University, Salinas, and HongHu, respectively, verifying its effectiveness and superiority in complex scenarios.

title: A Saturation-Adaptive Field-Weakening Strategy for Wide-Speed-Range SynRM Drives With Complex-Vector Flux-Linkage Control_supp1-3675105.mp4
authors: Bo Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: The performance of synchronous reluctance motors (SynRMs) degrades substantially when magnetic saturation is neglected in the control strategy. This omission leads to a distorted field-weakening (FW) trajectory, which deteriorates both steady-state and dynamic performance at high speeds. To address this issue, this article proposes a saturation-adaptive FW strategy integrated with flux-linkage control. An accurate flux-linkage model incorporating saturation effects is first established. Using this model, the FW strategy employs polar-coordinate lookup tables for precise maximum-torque-per-ampere and maximum-torque-per-voltage tracking across a wide speed range. Furthermore, a complex-vector flux-linkage controller is designed directly in the discrete domain. This controller achieves dynamic decoupling and provides inherent compensation for the time delay. Experimental results on a 7.5 kW SynRM drive validate the effectiveness of the integrated scheme. The proposed scheme extends the stable load range by more than 30 percentage points of the rated torque at 0.2 per-unit (p.u.) speed. It also reduces the acceleration time from standstill to 3 p.u. speed by 9.4%. Therefore, the proposed scheme offers a high-performance solution for industrial drives requiring wide-speed-range operation.

title: Design and Electromagnetic Analysis of an Input Cavity for 628MHz Inductive Output Tubes
authors: Hirwa Sakindi Alain, Jiaqiu Tian, Dingyu Gu, Shaomeng Wang, Zhigang Lu, Yubin Gong
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This paper presents electromagnetic and particle-in-cell simulation of a 628 MHz input cavity for inductive output tube applications. The re-entrant cavity design achieves excellent impedance matching with return loss below -39 dB through optimized coaxial loop coupling. Time-domain analysis establishes steady-state RF voltage distribution calibrated from frequency-domain characterization. Subsequent particle in cell simulations demonstrate effective electron beam bunching with fundamental frequency dominance in the spectral response. Results validate the cavity design methodology and identify key performance metrics for pre fabrication de risking. Future work includes mechanical optimization for thermal management and high-power experimental validation.

title: High-Order Finite-Time Adaptive Command Filtered Backstepping for Strict-Feedback Systems: A FAS Approach
authors: Xuedong Li, Weizhen Liu, Jindou Xu, Guangren Duan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes an adaptive finite-time command filtered backstepping control scheme for high-order fully actuated strict-feedback systems with parameter uncertainties. By fully exploiting the system's fully actuated and strict-feedback structure, the design process is simplified through the proposed high-order finite-time adaptive command filtered backstepping design. The introduction of a finite-time high-order Levant differentiator effectively avoids the “explosion of complexity” problem. Finally, by constructing the Lyapunov function, it is proven that all closed-loop system signals achieve uniform ultimate bounded stability within a finite time, with results further validated through simulation.
