title: Analysis of Spatial and Temporal Characterization of GNSS Tropospheric Parameters During the Extreme Rainfall on ‘ 9.7 ’ in Hong Kong
authors: Ming Shangguan, Xiaomei Ren, Shuguang Wu, Huaiqi Ye, Wenkui Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: On 7 September 2023, Hong Kong experienced a rare extreme rainfall due to the low-pressure trough associated with the remnants of Typhoon ‘Haikui’, which caused significant casualties and economic losses. Analyzing the relationship between Precipitable Water Vapor (PWV), atmospheric horizontal gradient and the rainfall is important for short-term forecasting of extreme rainfall events. Through atmospheric modeling and inversion algorithms, the PWV, sea level Zenith Wet Delay (ZWD) and Water Vapor Concentration (WVC) distributions were subsequently obtained. Based on the multi-source datasets, this study systematically analyzed the spatiotemporal evolution characteristics of water vapor distribution in Hong Kong during the extreme rainfall event. The results show that, temporally, tropospheric parameters continue to increase before the rainfall event, peak shortly before the maximum rainfall occurs within 1 to 3 hours, and then start to decrease to normal levels after the rainfall ends. Further analysis using cross-wavelet and wavelet coherence shows a positive correlation between tropospheric parameters and rainfall, with correlation coefficients exceeding 0.8, and tropospheric parameters leading rainfall changes. Spatially, the high-value areas of PWV, ZWD, and WVC are highly consistent with the heavy rainfall zones, showing obvious spatial congruence. Notably, when WVC and ZWD reach their peaks simultaneously, the probability of extreme rainfall events increases significantly. Based on these findings, ZWD and WVC can be key indicators for short-range weather forecasting of extreme rainstorm events.

title: Hardware-Efficient Compensation of Transmitter and Receiver IQ Imbalance for Coherent Optical Transmission
authors: Yukun Zhang, Xiaoxue Gong, Weigang Hou, Xu Zhang, Lei Guo
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: In this paper, a low-complexity scheme for dual-polarization 16 quadrature amplitude modulation (DP-16QAM) transceiver IQ imbalance compensation is proposed, which reduces the effects of in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) imbalance. This scheme enables the use of existing clock recovery loops and equalizers to probe the transceiver IQ skew. For transceiver IQ skew estimation, the receiver(RX) IQ skew is estimated by a clock recovery algorithm based on Gardner's timing error detection(GSMA), and the transmitter(TX) IQ skew is estimated by finding the value that yields the lowest equalizer error(Scanning delay algorithm, SDA). To address transmitter IQ amplitude/phase imbalance, we propose a low-complexity MIMO equalizer. It comprises a butterfly CV-MIMO and a non-butterfly two-layer real-valued MIMO based on a multimodulus algorithm (TMMA-RV-MIMO). For convenience, the new equalizer is called C-R AEQ. A 100 km transmission simulation and experiment with 36 Gbaud DP-16QAM signals showed that, with the TX/RX IQ skew estimation, the estimation error is less than 0.8/0.25 ps. Under IQ imbalance conditions, the C-R AEQ provides a 0.5 dB Q-factor gain compared with the 4×4 MIMO equalizer. In the absence of IQ imbalance, the C-R AEQ incurs a 0.3 dB Q penalty compared with the 4×4 MIMO equalizer. Furthermore, the C-R AEQ achieves a 47× reduction in real multiplications compared with the 4×4 MIMO equalizer.

title: Low-Complexity Opto-Electronic Collaborative Real-Time Laser Frequency Locking for Coherent Optical Communication Systems
authors: Yuheng Liu, Zuyu Li, Hongxia Xing, Yuyang Liu, Cuifeng Du, Fan Li
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: To reduce the power consumption and hardware complexity of coherent transceivers, enabling symbol-rate sampling is desirable but requires eliminate the local oscillator frequency offset (FO) prior to analog-to-digital converter (ADC) to avoid anti-aliasing-induced spectrum truncation. Conventional optical phase-locked loops (OPLL) require removing the modulation information of the received signal to achieve accurate frequency and phase locking. In this paper, we propose an opto electronic collaborative real-time frequency locking scheme for coherent receivers. The residual-carrier modulation technique is employed to facilitate accurate FO tracking. Meanwhile, by combining a low-point Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) with a chirp z-transform (CZT), the complexity can be reduced while maintaining accuracy. Frequency locking performance and transmission are experimentally validated in dual-polarization 32 GBaud QPSK/16QAM systems. The results show that the residual FO after locking fluctuates around 0 MHz, with a root mean square value of 4.2 MHz, which is within the tolerance of the blind phase search carrier recovery algorithm. In optical back-to-back and C-band 40 km transmission, the BER performance is essentially same with and without receiver-side DSP-based FO compensation, and the frequency locking module can operate properly even with very low received optical power. These results indicate that the proposed scheme offers an efficient and reliable solution toward low-cost and low-power coherent optical communication.

title: Path Planning and Tracking Control of Mining Articulated Loader Based on Map Skeleton Extraction and Dynamic Preview MPC
authors: Tong Yuan, Chang Liu, Jiannan Chen
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To meet the autonomous operation requirements of a mining articulated loader in tunnels, this paper first establishes a kinematic model of the vehicle. Then, a path planning method based on map skeleton extraction is proposed. This method employs the Zhang-Suen thinning algorithm to extract the tunnel centerline, performs topological key point identification and path smoothing, and ultimately generates a feasible desired path. Next, a model predictive control strategy incorporating a dynamic preview mechanism is introduced. This strategy adaptively adjusts the preview distance based on vehicle speed, curvature, and operating mode, and designs a reverse switching logic to address reverse tracking. Finally, simulation results demonstrate that the proposed planning method can generate a driving path satisfying operational requirements, and the dynamic preview MPC significantly reduces tracking errors under both forward and reverse conditions, outperforming the traditional MPC approach.

title: Curvature-Adaptive RRT*-Smart: An Intelligent Sampling and Optimization Framework for Narrow Passage Navigation
authors: Yueling Wang, Yinan Liu, Wei Zheng, Yingxu Li, Hongbin Wang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: An improved RRT*-smart algorithm was proposed to address inefficient path planning in narrow passages. It incorporates an adaptively adjusted sampling radius based on curvature-sensitive optimization, and a multi-stage node screening strategy that combines geometric filtering and discrete curvature analysis to prune redundant nodes. An exponential jump strategy within the greedy optimization phase that replaces sequential collision detection with exponential jumps, reducing computational load by compared to the baseline RRT*-smart. Finally, ED-RRT narrow passage identification is implemented to accelerate planning in narrow regions. The algorithm was validated in MATLAB simulations. Evaluation results demonstrate that, compared to other RRT*-based variants, this solution significantly shortens path length, improves computational efficiency, and enhances obstacle avoidance capability.

title: Phase-Aware ESO and PPO Residual Control for Trans-Medium Water Entry
authors: Hengyang Xu, Fuxiao Tan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To address the strong transient impact, abrupt medium transition, and difficulty of attitude recovery during high-speed air-to-water entry of trans-medium vehicles, this paper proposes a water-entry attitude control method based on a phase-aware Extended State Observer (ESO) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) residual compensation. First, a simulation model incorporating six-degree-of-freedom rigid-body dynamics, phase-dependent medium transition, and hydrodynamic/impact effects is established and validated through open-loop and closed-loop tests. Then, on the basis of a PID baseline controller, a phase-aware ESO is introduced for disturbance estimation, while PPO is used to learn bounded residual compensation, thereby improving stability, adaptability, and deployability. For the post-entry recovery stage, a composite reward function and a phase-gating mechanism are designed to regulate the residual action across different phases. Simulation results show that the proposed method outperforms the PID baseline in peak tilt angle, peak angular rate, recovery time, terminal tilt angle, and control smoothness. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework can effectively improve water-entry attitude control performance while preserving baseline stability.

title: A Folded Slot Waveguide SWS with Compact Coupler for W-band High-Power TWT
authors: Jingrui Duan, Zhigang Lu, Zhanliang Wang, Shaomeng Wang, Huarong Gong, Yubin Gong
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: In this paper, a novel folded slot waveguide (FSW) slow-wave structure (SWS) is proposed for a W-band high-power traveling wave tube (TWT). The electromagnetic properties of the FSW-SWS were analyzed, and a detailed model of the FSW-TWT high-frequency system was developed. The FSW-TWT has a compact and simple coupler design, which allows for effective mode conversion without significantly increasing the length of the TWT. Particle-in-Cell (PIC) simulations revealed a saturated output power of 924.5 W at 96 GHz, with an electronic efficiency of $6.6 \%$ and a $3-\mathrm{dB}$ bandwidth of 4 GHz.

title: Lateral Control of Unmanned Ground Vehicle on Uneven Terrain: A Variable Gain Fully-Actuated System Approach
authors: Yipeng Cao, Li Li, Xulei Wang, Qing Geng, Shaomeng Gu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To enhance the dynamic performance of uncertain underactuated unmanned ground vehicle (UGV), a lateral control method is proposed by utilizing the ideology of variable gain and fully-actuated system (FAS) approach. Firstly, the vehicle dynamics model and tire model are augmented into a lateral error dynamics model, which is converted into an FAS model with varying motion states and uncertainties by introducing an auxiliary variable. Secondly, a variable gain state and disturbance observer (VGSDO) is proposed to estimate the uncertainties arising from external disturbances and unmodeled tire dynamics. The dynamic adjustment capability of the VGSDO is further enhanced by incorporating estimation error into the design of the gain function. Then, the estimated uncertainties are added to a designed variable gain fully-actuated controller (VGFAC) to achieve accurate suppression of complex uncertainties. System stability is proved by theoretical analysis, and the accuracy and robustness for the lateral control of the UGV on uneven terrain are enhanced by the above designed method. Finally, experimental results are given to show the effectiveness of the proposed control method.

title: Optimization of RF Parameters in a 6-Cell Superconducting Cavity for FEL Applications
authors: Tasir Ullah, Wang Shaomeng, Anilkumar Patibandla, Bilawal Ali, Ain Qurat Ul
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: The design and simulation of a 6-cell superconducting cavity has been carried out to provide the initial acceleration of the electron beam for a linear accelerator (linac). Achieving a high accelerating electric field gradient is crucial for effective beam acceleration inside the cavity. Key cavity parameters are optimized, including an Q-factor of $9.8 \times 1 \mathrm{e} 9$, geometry factor of $469 \Omega$, and cell-to-cell coupling of $1.87 \%$ tailored for Free Electron Laser (FEL) applications. In this study, the electron beam reaches an energy of 3.93 MeV at the cavity exit, with an accelerating gradient of $17.21 \mathrm{MV} / \mathrm{m}$. The 1.3 GHz cavity includes 6 cells. The design and RF properties of the superconducting cavity are investigated using advanced 3D simulation tools such as CST Microwave Studio and CST Particle Studio. These tools were also used to optimize the cavity geometry for maximizing the accelerating gradient.

title: HOFA-Based Predefined-Time Stabilization of High-Order Nonlinear Systems Under Matched Disturbances
authors: Alison Garza-Alonso, Pablo Cesar Rodriguez-Ramirez, Michael Basin
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates predefined-time stabilization of high-order nonlinear systems within the High-Order Fully Actuated (HOFA) framework under matched disturbances. By preserving the intrinsic high-order structure of the dynamics, the proposed methodology avoids recursive and order-reduction design procedures typically required in conventional approaches. Two control laws are developed. The first is developed for the disturbance-free case and guarantees convergence to the origin within a predefined time independent of the initial conditions. The second incorporates a sliding surface construction and is applicable both in the absence and in the presence of deterministic matched disturbances satisfying a Lipschitz condition, ensuring robust predefined-time stabilization. The proposed designs also exhibit moderate control effort, as illustrated in the Permanent-Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) application. The effectiveness of the approach is validated through numerical simulations on a fourth-order permanent-magnet synchronous motor operating under disturbance-free and matched disturbance conditions.

title: Fixed-Time Fault-Tolerant Control for Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell Leakage via Super-Twisting Observer
authors: Zeqi Hu, Chouqin Pi, Jian Chen, Zhongliang Li, Daniel Hissel
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Hydrogen energy utilization plays a crucial role in promoting decarbonization in the transportation sector and achieving the dual carbon goals. As a core technology for hydrogen energy utilization, proton exchange membrane fuel cells are susceptible to gas supply manifold leakage faults during actual operation, which may trigger issues such as oxygen starvation and efficiency degradation. To address this fault, this paper establishes a fault model that reflects the leakage mechanism and proposes a fault-tolerant control scheme integrating a super-twisting sliding mode observer and a fixed-time fault-tolerant controller. This scheme enables real-time and accurate estimation of fault parameters and system states via the observer, and achieves dynamic fault compensation and robust tracking of the oxygen excess ratio through the controller. The super-twisting structure effectively suppresses control chattering, guaranteeing fixed-time restoration of system stability. Simulation studies demonstrated that, compared with traditional methods, the proposed strategy exhibited faster convergence speed, smaller overshoot and weaker oscillation of the oxygen excess ratio under different leakage intensities and load disturbances, which significantly reduced the risk of oxygen starvation. This work provides key technical support for the efficient and reliable operation of fuel cells under complex fault conditions.

title: DARCade-YOLOv8: An Insulator Defect Detection Algorithm Based on Dilated Adaptive Cascading Bottleneck
authors: Qiyu Zheng, Yankang Liu, Long Li, Chengkai Yang, Jian Sun, Zhao Wang, Yuxuan Song, Qihang Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes an insulator defect detection algorithm, DARCade-YOLOv8, based on a dilated adaptive cascading bottleneck, aimed at improving the detection performance of insulators and their defects in images captured by powerline inspection drones. The study first designs a Dilated Multi-Scale Adaptive Fusion (DMSAF) module to efficiently fuse fine-grained features with global context. It then introduces a Dynamic Adaptive Refinement Convolution (DARC) module to enhance the perception of small target details. Subsequently, a Cascaded Dilated Adaptive Refinement (DARCade) bottleneck structure is constructed to replace the C2f module, optimizing both detection accuracy and inference efficiency. Finally, baseline selection and detection experiments for insulators and their defects are conducted on the self-constructed LPLID dataset. The results show that the DARCade-YOLOv8 model achieves a mAP0.5 of 74.8% for detecting minor insulator defects, a 9.8% improvement over the original algorithm, significantly optimizing the detection accuracy of insulators and their small defects. This model can effectively meet the engineering application requirements for drone-based powerline inspections.

title: Highly Sensitive Microwave Pressure Sensor Based on Coupled Transmission Lines Integrating Cantilever Structure
authors: Zhuoyue Zheng, Zhuowei Zhang, Cheng Teng, Terry Tao Ye, Han-Xiong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article proposes a novel microwave pressure sensor based on a coupled transmission line integrating a cantilever (CTLIC). The proposed CTLIC structure is implemented by embedding a serpentine-spring modified cantilever into the central microstrip line of a three-line coupled microstrip configuration. Under applied pressure, mechanical deformation of the central transmission line within the CTLIC induces an asymmetric disturbance in the electromagnetic coupling coefficient between adjacent resonators. This asymmetric coupling modulates the response characteristics, specifically causing relative shifts in the resonance frequencies (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$f_{T\!P1},f_{T\!P3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and an amplitude variation at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$f_{T\!P2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Consequently, two distinct sensing parameters are generated: the frequency change (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta f_{T\!P}=f_{T\!P3} - f_{T\!P1}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and the amplitude change (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\vert S_{11}\vert $</tex-math> </inline-formula>). Both parameters exhibit relatively good linearity and high sensitivity. To validate the sensing mechanism experimentally, a prototype was simulated, fabricated (on a rigid PCB substrate RO4003C), and measured. Measured results demonstrate that the sensor achieves precise pressure measurement across a 0–10 kPa range, exhibiting an average sensitivity of 104 MHz/kPa (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta f_{T\!P}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and 1.34 dB/kPa (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\vert S_{11}\vert $</tex-math> </inline-formula> at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$f_{T\!P2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>).

title: The DaSiamRPN Object Tracking Method Optimized by PCA-EWMA and Shallow Neural Networks
authors: Qi Xu, Yue Zhang, Xin Zhao, Mengjiao Wang, Huanbo Sun, Haichao Peng, Yuhang Liu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To address issues such as tracking drift and target loss in Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) power-line inspection tasks under complex environments, this paper proposes an optimized Distractor-aware Siamese Region Proposal Network (DaSiamRPN) object tracking method based on Principal Component Analysis - Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (PCA-EWMA) and shallow neural networks. By utilizing Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for target feature dimensionality reduction and adaptive factor calculation, combined with Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) for dynamic template smoothing updates, and optimizing the tracking model with a shallow neural network, the method effectively suppresses noise interference and tracking drift. Performance validation experiments were conducted on a dataset comprising six typical scenarios. The results show that the tracking accuracy of the improved algorithm increases by up to 78% compared to traditional methods, with overall performance significantly outperforming conventional approaches. This method effectively enhances the stability and accuracy of UAV tracking in inspection tasks, providing reliable technical support for the automation of power system operations.

title: Adaptive Knowledge Transfer-Based Particle Swarm Optimization for Cascade Pumping Stations Operation
authors: Chao Liu, Xinying Xu, Xing Bai, Rong Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Cascade pumping stations are core power components of inter-basin water diversion projects. Their optimal operation directly affects both operational costs and economic benefits. Existing optimization models for cascade pumping stations involve high-dimensional decision variables. This often leads to premature convergence when conventional algorithms are applied. To address this issue, this paper proposes a method to optimize the daily operation cost model of cascade pumping stations based on adaptive knowledge transfer. This method decomposes the cascade pumping stations optimization problem (CPSOP) into multiple single-stage pumping station optimization sub-problems, using the particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm as the solver. By transferring knowledge generated during the optimization process between different pumping station tasks, it achieves collaborative optimization of multiple single-stage pumping stations. Furthermore, an adaptive knowledge transfer probability adjustment mechanism based on convergence state awareness is introduced to regulate the transfer intensity during the global exploration and local exploitation phases. The proposed method is validated at the three-stage pumping stations along the main line of the Wanjiazhai Yellow River Diversion Project. The results show a reduction in daily electricity costs of approximately 2.45 % compared to the current operational scheme of the project. The research findings offer a viable approach to improving energy efficiency and economic operation in cascade pumping stations.

title: High-Precision Simultaneous Measurement of Inter-Satellite Six Degree-of-Freedom Position and Attitude Based on Multi-Laser Tracking System
authors: Guicun Li, Fei Huang, Haobiao Yu, He Zhang, Fei Han
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To meet the increasingly higher requirements for measurements of inter-satellite six degree-of-freedom (6-DOF) position and attitude between precise formation flying satellites of complex space missions, a novel high-precision simultaneous 6-DOF measurement method based on multi-channel femtosecond laser tracking is proposed. A single-channel femtosecond laser tracking system combining femtosecond laser precise ranging and fast steering mirror (FSM) with position sensitive detector (PSD) tracking angle measurement is established. The accurate analytical form of the relative position is obtained based on ray tracing. Furthermore, based on the fixed installation relationship of the three-channel femtosecond laser relative position measurement system, the high-precision measurement model of the inter-satellite relative position and attitude is completed through the three-point position and pose solution method and the rotation matrix transformation. The Monte-Carlo numerical simulation is used to complete the error analysis of relative position and attitude with respect to distance, angle, and installation relationships. The simulation results demonstrate that within an inter-satellite range of 300 meters, sub-millimeter-level relative position measurement precision and 0.002° three-axis relative attitude measurement precision can be achieved, satisfying the requirements of future autonomous navigation tasks for precise formation flying, such as synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and virtual space telescopes.

title: Surface-Engineered Negative Electron Affinity High-Gain Electron Beam Amplifier
authors: Anika Tabassum Priyoti, Shafayeth Jamil, Zezhi Wu, Iliya Larimojarad, Rehan Kapadia
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Achieving an electron source that simultaneously offers high efficiency, high brightness, and long operational lifetime remains a fundamental challenge due to intrinsic trade-offs in existing cathode technologies. Here, we demonstrate a graphene-encapsulated, hydrogen-terminated diamond heterostructure that acts as an atomically engineered gain medium while maintaining a stable negative electron affinity (NEA) emission interface. This architecture allows efficient carrier amplification with continuous charge neutralization and thereby enables operation beyond pulsed mode for the first time.

title: Distributed Prescribed Performance Formation Control for Multi-Robot Systems with Unknown Control Coefficients
authors: Zhenfeng Wang, Tao Chen, Yafeng Li, Dianrui Mu, Zhe Guan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the distributed formation control problem for a class of multi-robot systems subject to unknown control coefficients and stringent prescribed performance constraints. To strictly guarantee the transient and steady-state behaviors of the formation tracking errors, a prescribed performance control (PPC) strategy is synthesized. Central to this strategy is the introduction of a double-end smooth transition function to characterize the performance envelope, which facilitates a smooth startup for the leader vehicle while enforcing strictly bounded tracking constraints for the followers. To resiliently accommodate the unknown control coefficients, Nussbaum-type gain functions are incorporated into the control protocol, without requiring prior knowledge of the signs of the control coefficients. Furthermore, by integrating the dynamic surface control (DSC) technique, the proposed framework can circumvent the problem of explosion of complexity inherently associated with traditional backstepping procedures. Rigorous Lyapunov-based stability analysis establishes that all closed-loop signals achieve uniform ultimate boundedness (UUB), with the formation tracking errors strictly confined within their respective time-varying performance funnels. Finally, comprehensive simulation results validate the effectiveness, robustness, and superior tracking performance of the proposed control framework.

title: Networked Control of High-Order Fully Actuated Nonlinear Systems Subject to Packet Dropouts and Measurement Noises
authors: Hailong Cui, Xiang Xu, Guanglei Zhao
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: The theory of high-order fully actuated (HOFA) systems provides a unified framework for the analysis and control of nonlinear systems by eliminating nonlinearities via direct compensation and assigning desired linear dynamics. However, when such systems are deployed over imperfect communication networks, successive packet losses and non-differentiable measurement noises brings new challenges for the design of reliable event-triggered control protocol. By introducing a local memory unit to store all possible values since last successful transmission, the actual measurement errors is reconstructed at sensor side despite in absence of acknowledgement signals. Event-triggered control are then developed upon a more conservative triggering condition, that designs a series of dynamic variables to capture the worst scenario by utilizing all these reconstructed measurement errors. While arbitrarily small noise may cause the triggering condition to be satisfied infinitely near the equilibrium, i.e., Zeno behavior occurs, a mandatory minimum inter-event time (MIET) for any adjacent events is imposed for the event-triggered mechanism (ETM). Sufficient condition is derived for the stability of closed-loop system, as well as the MIET and the maximum allowable number of successive packet losses (MANSPL). The proposed approach preserves the full-actuation advantage while being robust to unknown bounded noise and successive dropouts, and all design parameters can be determined offline without online optimisation. Finally, the simulation results validate the theoretical findings.

title: Cycle-to-failure Prediction of Power Semiconductor Modules based on Early Degradation Indicators
authors: Yichi Zhang, Yi Zhang, Frede Blaabjerg, Huai Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Power cycling tests are widely used to obtain cycle-to-failure data for qualifying the package reliability of power semiconductor modules. In conventional practice, each test must be run to failure, which makes lifetime characterization costly and time-consuming. This paper reveals that early-stage degradation already contains a compact and physically meaningful indicator of device lifetime. An early degradation indicator is identified from the resistance evolution during the initial wear-out stage. Based on this indicator, a data-driven approach is developed to infer cycle-to-failure using only the first 5% of power cycling data. Experimental validation is conducted on 65 power semiconductor modules tested under 11 distinct conditions. The results show a linear relationship, in logarithmic form, between the early-stage degradation rate and cycle-to-failure across all tested conditions. These findings demonstrate that early degradation rates can serve as a reliable lifetime indicator, enabling substantial reduction of test duration without relying on full degradation trajectory prediction.

title: U3A: Uncertainty-Aware Accident Anticipation for Reliable Autonomous Driving
authors: Xingcheng Liu, Bin Rao, Yanchen Guan, Wanping Liu, Chengyue Wang, Xuesong Wang, Dengbo He, Yang Liu, Zhenning Li, Keqiang Li, Chengzhong Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Accident anticipation is essential for autonomous driving, yet existing video-based models often exhibit unstable predictions because they insufficiently account for the uncertainty that accumulates over long temporal sequences. Such instability leads to fluctuating confidence and unreliable alert timing in complex traffic environments. We propose U3A, an uncertainty-aware framework that improves the reliability of accident anticipation by jointly stabilizing representations and regulating decision behavior. U3A introduces a dual diffusion refinement module that enforces temporal coherence at both image and object levels, reducing abrupt feature shifts caused by occlusion and scene variability. To quantify epistemic uncertainty, the framework applies Monte Carlo Dropout over historical states and incorporates these estimates into a time-sensitive actor–critic policy, which learns to delay alerts under unstable confidence and issue warnings only when risk is consistently supported. Experiments on DAD, CCD, A3D, and AoTA show that U3A achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and mean time-to-accident, while producing smoother and more dependable alert trajectories. The results demonstrate that integrating diffusion-based feature refinement with uncertainty-guided policy learning offers a practical path toward more reliable accident anticipation for autonomous vehicles.

title: Monocular Spatial Pose Estimation of the Overhead Contact System Dropper Clamp Based on Deep Learning
authors: Jiuru Lu, Yu Cai, Mai Zhang, Zhirui He, Chuxiong Hu, Ze Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: In automated maintenance of high-speed rail, estimating the spatial pose of the overhead contact system (OCS) dropper clamp is crucial. This estimation is essential for robot operations like disassembly and assembly. However, the complex outdoor environment has drastic lighting variations, background clutter, and frequent occlusions, which undermines the stability and robustness of detection systems. Moreover, traditional pose estimation algorithms have low real-time performance. This limits maintenance efficiency and hinders rapid response and continuous operation in automated systems. To address various interference issues in outdoor scenarios, this study enhances the model’s adaptability to complex outdoor backgrounds by replacing the backgrounds of catenary clamp images to generate datasets under different weather conditions, and applying data augmentation techniques, such as flipping, luminance adjustment, and noise injection, to improve adaptability to various environments. A dataset of 31,104 annotated images is constructed. The dataset includes eleven geometrically distinct keypoints. Finally, the pose of the dropper clamp relative to the camera is computed using the EPnP (Efficient Perspective-n-Point) algorithm. Experiments show that the proposed monocular method is efficient, stable, and accurate. It is suitable for real-time automated maintenance in high-speed rail environments.

title: Function-Level Adaptive Model Predictive Control Applied to Attitude Tracking of High-Speed Aircraft
authors: Yuan Li, Xuebo Yang, Zhiguang Feng, Wei Huang, Shuangxi Liu, Yi Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: External disturbances pose significant challenges to the attitude tracking and precision maintenance of high-speed flight vehicles. To enhance the tracking accuracy, this paper proposes a function-level adaptive model predictive control (FAMPC) approach. In contrast to conventional parameter-adaptive methods, the proposed functional adaptive law (FAL) estimates unknown disturbances directly without requiring auxiliary parameterized approximators. Furthermore, rather than relying on terminal invariant sets for stability guarantees, a Lyapunov-based model predictive control (MPC) framework is formulated. The core approach involves synthesizing an auxiliary controller, whose influence is incorporated as an additional constraint to enforce a specific decay rate for a properly selected Lyapunov function, thereby ensuring closed-loop stability and recursive feasibility. Rigorous theoretical analysis formally establishes the recursive feasibility and closed-loop stability under the proposed Lyapunov-based framework, while extensive simulation studies validate the effectiveness of the proposed control strategy.

title: A Multi-Granularity Approach Via Image and Feature Diversification for Fine-Grained Recognition
authors: Jing Li, Wenhao Wang, Yanbo Wang, Yang Wang
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Fine-grained visual recognition (FGVR) remains challenging due to large intra-class variation, small inter-class differences, and limited annotated data. Despite progress, dataset scarcity leads to weak generalization and overreliance on limited visual cues, while conventional image- or feature-level augmentation often disrupts or overlooks critical fine-grained details. To address this, we propose IFD (Image- and Feature-level dataset Diversification), a novel framework that integrates two complementary augmentation strategies. At the image level, Discriminative Amplitude Perturbation (DAP) uses Fourier-based token perturbations to diversify samples while preserving structural details. At the feature level, EFR mitigates feature magnitude imbalance in Vision Transformers, encouraging more balanced learning of fine-grained cues. Together, these methods improve generalization and discriminative power, achieving competitive accuracy in data-scarce FGVR settings.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Multi-authority Secure Data Sharing with Traceability and Attribute Revocation for IoT
authors: Haojie Zhou, Zhaofeng Ma, Zhiquan Liu, Tiezheng Wu, Jiyuan Song, Pengfei Duan
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: To address the demand for fine-grained access control in Internet of Things (IoT) data sharing, attribute-based encryption (ABE) has emerged as a critical solution. Nevertheless, the computational overhead inherent in ABE poses a major challenge for its direct deployment on resource-constrained IoT devices. Data storage and sharing through centralized cloud may be interrupted due to cloud server failures. Additionally, if user attributes cannot be revoked, some users who should have had their data access rights canceled will still be capable of accessing the data. To solve these problems, we propose a multi-authority attribute-based data sharing scheme that combines blockchain to construct a secure and trusted data sharing environment, while supporting efficient attribute revocation. We reduce the computational burden during the online encryption phase through online/offline encryption, while introducing outsourced decryption to lessen the computational overhead of user decryption. Our scheme supports the tracing of key abusers and attribute revocation for users. Furthermore, by interplanetary file system (IPFS), we reduce on-chain storage burden, and by interplanetary name system (IPNS), we address the difficulty of logically deleting old ciphertexts when updating ciphertexts. Security analysis shows that our scheme exhibits static security, and experimental results demonstrate that our data sharing scheme exhibits excellent efficiency and practicality.

title: Investigations on EMC-Thermal Co-Design for Shielding Enclosures with Apertures
authors: Kaushik Patra, Bibhu Prasad Nayak, Sayantan Dhar
publish: IEEE Letters on Electromagnetic Compatibility Practice and Applications
abstract: Metallic enclosures are widely used for electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) shielding to protect electronic systems from external and internal electromagnetic interference. However, the inclusion of metal shielding often leads to an increase in the internal temperature of the enclosure, which can adversely affect the performance and reliability of electronic components. Traditionally, EMC and thermal management are optimized separately, but in practice, these two aspects are interdependent. In this work, we investigate a simultaneous approach for EMC shielding and thermal performance improvement using simulation-based studies. This article's goal is to develop design guidelines for enclosure apertures and temperature gradients to achieve an optimal balance between shielding effectiveness and thermal management. The study intends to provide practical design rules for the placement, size and distribution of apertures to ensure adequate cooling without compromising EMC performance.

title: Secure ISAC With Fluid Antenna Systems: Joint Precoding and Port Selection
authors: Abdelhamid Salem, Hao Xu, Kai-Kit Wong, Chan-Byoung Chae, Yangyang Zhang, Salma Elkawafi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: This paper presents a novel framework for enhancing physical-layer security in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems by leveraging the reconfigurability of fluid antenna systems (FAS). We propose a joint precoding and port selection (JPPS) strategy that maximizes the sum secrecy rate while simultaneously ensuring reliable radar sensing. The problem is formulated using fractional programming (FP) and solved through an iterative algorithm that integrates FP transformations with successive convex approximation (SCA). To reduce computational complexity, we further develop low-complexity schemes based on zero-forcing (ZF) precoding, combined with greedy port selection and trace-inverse minimization. Simulation results show substantial improvements in both secrecy performance and sensing accuracy compared to conventional baselines, across a wide range of FAS port number, user loads, and sensing targets. These findings highlight the critical importance of FAS geometry optimization in enabling secure and efficient joint communication-sensing for next-generation wireless networks.

title: Experimental Study on Thermal Stability of High-Temperature Superconducting Reactor Coil Based on Distributed Optical Fiber Sensing Technology
authors: Jiajun Huang, Mengda Li, Yanan Wang, Jie Sheng, Junjie Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: High-temperature superconducting (HTS) reactors represent a novel class of substation equipment offering substantial advantages, notably large power capacity, reduced energy losses, and minimal acoustic noise, which significantly enhance the transmission efficiency and economic viability of the power grid. Their stable operation strictly depends on a reliable refrigeration system to maintain a cryogenic environment. Thus, the integrity of the sealing system is a core element for ensuring operational stability. Compromised cryostat sealing can lead to a slow leakage of liquid nitrogen (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{LN}\_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>), directly resulting in insufficient cooling capacity. This induces localized overheating in key regions, such as the current terminals and the coil apex, ultimately triggering a coil quench, thereby severely threatening the security and stable operation of the power grid. To address the thermal instability caused by compromised cryostat airtightness, this study developed a Rayleigh scattering-based distributed fiber optic sensing (DFOS) system to monitor a prepared HTS reactor coil. The main results show that with the liquid level 6cm below the coil's upper surface, increasing the applied current causes a broader spatial temperature rise in the outer turns, whereas the inner turns exhibit a higher peak temperature magnitude. Furthermore, under the rated current, the outer turns exhibit a significant temperature rise only after the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{LN}\_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> level drops by more than 5.5 cm.

title: Bio-Inspired Event-Based Visual Servoing for Ground Robots
authors: Maral Mordad, Kian Behzad, Debojyoti Biswas, Noah J. Cowan, Milad Siami
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: Biological sensory systems are inherently adaptive, filtering out constant stimuli and prioritizing relative changes, likely enhancing computational and metabolic efficiency. Inspired by active sensing behaviors across a wide range of animals, this paper introduces a principled 1D event-based visual servoing framework for ground robots operating in structured environments. Utilizing a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS), we demonstrate that by applying a fixed spatial kernel to the asynchronous event stream generated from structured logarithmic intensity-change patterns, the resulting net event flux analytically isolates specific combinations of kinematic states. We establish a generalized theoretical bound for this event rate estimator and show that linear and quadratic spatial profiles isolate the robot’s velocity and position-velocity product, respectively. Leveraging these properties, we employ a multi-pattern stimulus to directly synthesize a nonlinear state feedback term entirely without traditional state estimation. To overcome the inescapable loss of linear observability at equilibrium inherent in event sensing, we propose a bio-inspired active sensing limit-cycle controller. Experimental validation on a 1/10-scale autonomous ground vehicle confirms the efficacy, extreme low-latency, and computational efficiency of the proposed direct-sensing approach.

title: Design and Control of Battery-Buffered Charging Infrastructure Utilizing a Modular Simulation Framework
authors: Malte Neef, Leo Strobel, Jonas Schiller, Francesco Montanari, Marco Pruckner
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Large-scale battery-supported charging station clusters, often referred to as charging hubs, have emerged as a promising solution to meet the growing demand for electric vehicle fast charging. Integrating stationary energy storage systems enables these hubs to mitigate peak power demand and reduce the strain on the local power grid. However, designing and operating such systems remains challenging, as it requires coordinated decisions on battery configuration and control strategies to ensure reliable and cost-effective operation. This work develops a modular simulation framework that enables flexible modeling of charging hubs through extensive parameterization, including adjustments to the number of charging units, the maximum grid power, the buffer storage capacities, and the control strategy.We validate the framework based on real-world data from Audi charging hubs and conduct a case study showing that a cost-optimal charging strategy can achieve cost savings of up to 70 % compared to immediate charging, while an emission-optimal strategy can save up to 17.5 % in CO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> emissions. However, both types of savings reduce significantly with increased charging demand at the charging hub.We demonstrate the framework’s ability to inform planning decisions by identifying the maximum grid power as the limiting factor in supplying even higher charging demands through a series of scenario evaluations.

title: Artificial Intelligence of Things as a Foundation for Agentic AI Systems: Architectures, Applications, and Challenges
authors: Thippa Reddy Gadekallu, Yongkang Zhao, Zhihao Wen, Pronaya Bhattacharya, Yuchao Xia, Jijing Cai, Engin Zeydan, Kai Fang, Hailin Feng
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The evolution of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has reached a critical point, where agentic AI systems demonstrate strong capabilities in goal formulation and planning but remain difficult to deploy in real-world settings due to their limited grounding in physical environments. These limitations arise from the challenges of partial observability, actuation uncertainty, and strict resource constraints that characterize the physical world. This survey argues that the Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) provides the necessary foundation to embed agentic intelligence into such environments by enabling continuous interaction between sensing, reasoning, and action. We analyze the synergy between goal-driven agentic AI and distributed AIoT infrastructures and present a unified taxonomy of AIoT-enabled agentic architectures, highlighting trade-offs across centralized, edge-native, and hybrid deployment models. The survey further examines key enabling technologies, including edge intelligence, semantic communication, digital twins, and trust mechanisms, and discusses how they integrate into cognitive control loops. Through representative applications in smart cities, industrial automation, healthcare, and energy systems, we show how this convergence moves automation beyond rule-based behavior toward context-aware autonomy. Finally, we identify open challenges related to long-horizon safety, resource-aware intelligence, and ethical governance, and outline research directions toward robust, trustworthy, and socially embedded autonomous systems.

title: $\chi$-Entropy: A Distributional Measure of Multiscale Topological Organization
authors: Cong Shen, Guancen Lin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Many real-world systems exhibit multiscale organization, where structural signals emerge across a continuum of resolution parameters. However, widely used topological summaries along filtrations (e.g., curves or collections of features) often lack a simple, principled way to compare how structural responses are distributed across scales. We propose <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\chi$</tex-math></inline-formula>-Entropy, a distributional descriptor that interprets the Euler characteristic curve as a scalewise response profile and quantifies its dispersion via an entropy formulation. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\chi$</tex-math></inline-formula>-Entropy is designed to be robust to nuisance factors, such as global rescaling and sign changes of the response, enabling direct comparison across heterogeneous datasets. We evaluate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\chi$</tex-math></inline-formula>-Entropy on a diverse suite of benchmarks—including geometric shapes, spatial transportation networks, signed trust networks, canonical nonlinear dynamical systems, and epileptic electroencephalography—and show that it consistently reveals nontrivial multiscale organization that is missed by degree/weight-based statistics, spectral descriptors, and persistence-based summaries. These results suggest <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\chi$</tex-math></inline-formula>-Entropy as a simple and domain-agnostic tool for quantifying multiscale topological organization in complex systems.

title: Efficient and Deterministic E2E Transmission for Avionics Networks Based on IEEE 802.1DP
authors: Xuan Wei, Qimin Xu, Lei Xu, Cailian Chen, Xinping Guan, Le Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: IEEE 802.1DP indicates that with the explosive growth of equipment and services in next-generation aircraft, time-sensitive networking (TSN) is a promising technology to support deterministic backbone communication. Nonetheless, remote data concentrators, data concentration points bridged to the TSN backbone, are essential for deterministic end-to-end (E2E) communication. Because its aggregation methods directly affect network link efficiency amid increasingly scarce link resources. Most research focuses on fixed-aggregation methods for single-type data buses, resulting in bandwidth waste and further limiting deterministic communication. Therefore, we propose a flexible time-sensitive aggregation architecture in TSN avionics networks. In this architecture, a flexible time-sensitive aggregation mechanism is designed to classify heterogeneous flows into flexible aggregation slots and forward aggregated flows according to global TSN scheduling. Then, a frame-level aggregation model precisely represents generated flow attributes, reducing bandwidth reservations and expanding the feasible scheduling space. A coordinated mapping and scheduling (CMS) algorithm based on the model is proposed to reduce bandwidth consumption while ensuring deterministic E2E transmission. In CMS, a minimum-time-margin model and congestion detection and reclassification are developed for bidirectional adjustment, boosting computational efficiency while eliminating unavoidable scheduling collisions. Compared with the State-of-the-Art algorithm, our method obtains the Pareto front in three metrics and achieves less than 1/20 time cost for over 4000 flows.

title: Cooperative Multiobjective Optimization of Continuous Crystallization via COBC Under Process Uncertainty: From Startup to Steady-State Operation
authors: Mingyan Zhao, Tao Liu, Bo Song, Lek Wantha, Zhixing Cao, Sohrab Rohani, Rolf Findeisen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: To deal with complex dynamics and operational constraints for optimizing the startup and steady-state operation of continuous crystallization via continuous oscillatory baffled crystallizer under process uncertainty, this article proposes a cooperative multiobjective optimization (MOO) method for implementation. By considering the target crystal length, concentration and aspect ratio of the two-dimensional crystal size distribution (2-D CSD), a robust MOO program is established for the steady-state operation of continuous crystallization under process constraints and uncertainties. The resulting operating conditions provide a reference for the startup optimization. By introducing the Earth mover's distance to evaluate the deviation between the outlet 2-D CSD and the target 2-D CSD determined from the robust steady-state MOO, a constrained startup optimization scheme is subsequently proposed, along with a coupled wavelet-based control vector parameterization approach and a decision variable updating strategy named SHapley Additive exPlanations based on the model interpretability. Experimental results on the continuous crystallization of L-glutamic acid demonstrate that the variances of the above three objectives are significantly reduced over 70% compared to a recently developed MOO method. Moreover, the proposed startup optimization shortens the startup time over 30% compared with the standard operation based on steady-state optimization.

title: Design of a Self-Aligning Knee Exoskeleton for Minimizing Parasitic Forces During Assisted Ambulation_supp3-3693674.mp4
authors: Sheng Guo
publish: N/A
abstract: One major challenge in the application of exoskeletons is the misalignment between the biological and mechanical joint axes during use, which arises from human–robot kinematic mismatches and variability in wearing conditions. As a result, parasitic forces are generated at the physical human–robot interface and lead to significant relative sliding distance (RSD) between the exoskeleton and the human leg due to constrained fit. To address this issue, we propose a novel kinematically adaptive, self-aligning knee exoskeleton that eliminates sagittal-plane misalignment to reduce parasitic forces and removes the need for prealignment before use. Both platform and human–subject experiments were performed to evaluate its performance. The platform tests demonstrate that the exoskeleton can transmit effective pure assistive torque without introducing additional parasitic forces at the pHRI. Human–subject tests show that the exoskeleton maintains low RSD and parasitic forces across walking speeds up to 1.8 m/s and assistance levels up to 16 N<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot$</tex-math></inline-formula>m; although both increased with speed and assistance level, peak differences remained below 6 mm and 8 N, respectively. During stand-to-sit transitions under constant assistive torques (up to 8 Nm) at large knee flexion angles, similarly low RSD and parasitic forces were observed, with torque transmission efficiency exceeding 87%.

title: Data-Driven PID Adaptive Control for Robotic Manipulators with Guaranteed Asymptotic Tracking
authors: Zongyang Hu, Zhe Guan, Hao Yu, Changchun Hua
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a Data-Driven PID Adaptive Control (DDPID-AC) strategy to address the control problem of robotic manipulators subject to parametric uncertainties and constant external disturbances. Unlike conventional PD-based adaptive methods, this scheme incorporates an integral action into the filtered error vector, improving the control law while retaining the structure of the standard data-driven parameter update law. Theoretical analysis utilizing Lyapunov theory demonstrates the global asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system, ensuring the elimination of steady-state errors induced by constant disturbances. Simulation results on a two-degree-of-freedom manipulator validate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme: DDPID-AC not only achieves high-precision tracking with zero steady-state error under disturbances but also guarantees that the estimated parameters converge to their true values under the combined conditions of interval excitation and disturbance-free operation.

title: Variable Gain Hierarchical Sliding Mode Control for Flexible Spacecraft Attitude
authors: Chen Shijia, Sun Xinzhu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To meet the demand for high-precision attitude control during Earth service in China's low-Earth orbit (LEO) Internet plus constellation, and considering the issue that traditional rigid body models may generate false high-frequency oscillations or overshoots due to the neglect of flexible vibrations, leading to deviations in angular velocity predictions from reality, this paper designs a variable gain hierarchical sliding mode control (HSMC) method based on an inertia-adaptive observer (IAO) and a first-order approximate rigid-flexible coupling dynamics model of flexible spacecraft. The designed inertia-adaptive observer estimates system states and time-varying rotational inertia online. The outer loop of the hierarchical sliding mode control achieves attitude tracking, while the inner loop suppresses flexible vibrations. Combined with variable gain adjustment (VGA), it achieves adaptive adjustments of errors, sliding mode surfaces, and vibration energy. Based on observer error boundedness analysis and numerical simulation comparisons with adaptive robust controllers and continuous smooth controllers, the results show that the designed variable gain hierarchical sliding mode attitude controller can more quickly suppress flexible panel vibrations and achieve control effects.

title: Reinforcement Learning Based Disturbance Rejection Control for High-Order Discrete-Time Nonlinear System
authors: Jianwei Ren, Yanzheng Zhu, Lin Liu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this paper, a reinforcement learning (RL) based disturbance rejection control method is developed in this paper for a class of high-order discrete-time nonlinear system. To address the noncausal problem, a system transformation approach is established by transforming the systems into an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n$</tex>-step-ahead predictor. Additionally, an extended state observer is presented for monitoring latent information and disturbances within the system. This observer integrates radial basis function (RBF) neural networks to approximate unknown functions, while leveraging actor-critic neural networks to minimize the strategic utility function and estimate errors for unknown nonlinear dynamic predictions. The proposed output feedback controller is designed by considering the approximation of the controller update rate through RL-based RBF neural networks to achieve enhanced control effectiveness. Based on the Lyapunov theory, all the signals of the resulting closed-loop system are assured to be semiglobal uniformly ultimately bounded. Finally, the effectiveness and advantage of the theoretical results are illustrated via numerical simulations.

title: Prescribed-Time Consensus Control for Nonlinear Second-Order MASs with Quantization and Actuator Attacks
authors: Shixin Xia, Jiewen Yin, Huifang Min
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper addresses adaptive prescribed-time consensus tracking for second-order nonlinear multi-agent systems (MASs) under actuator deception attacks, unmeasurable states, and quantization constraints. A novel time-varying constraint function ensures initial-condition-independent prescribed-time convergence to a predefined precision. Radial basis function neural networks (RBF NN) approximate unknown nonlinearities. An adaptive controller is designed via backstepping. Lyapunov analysis proves boundedness and prescribed-time convergence. Simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed control scheme.

title: A Data-Driven Prediction Model for Deformation Resistance Correction Coefficient of Cold Rolling Mill
authors: Yongwei Wang, Shuzong Cheng, Cheng Qian, Le Liu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: The deformation resistance of steel strip, as a critical parameter in cold rolling force prediction, significantly impacts the accuracy of rolling force estimation. However, since deformation resistance cannot be directly measured through sensors during production, it remains challenging to establish a universal deformation resistance model applicable to various rolling conditions. To address this issue, this study first analyzes the mechanism models of rolling force and deformation resistance, derives a soft sensing model for deformation resistance, and further develops a correction coefficient model. Using a large dataset of high-quality samples, the deformation resistance correction coefficient was fitted using the least squares method. A deep neural network (DNN) prediction model for deformation resistance correction coefficients was established, with the optimal topology structure determined. Comparative experiments analyzing the prediction errors of the DNN model and other models demonstrate that the DNN model exhibits superior generalization capability and high prediction accuracy in data-driven modeling.

title: Command-Filter-Based Fixed-Time Prescribed Tracking Switching Control for Time Delay Nonlinear Systems With External Disturbances
authors: Ning-Sen Sun, Cui-Hua Zhang, Chang-Chun Hud
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper studies fixed-time prescribed performance tracking control utilizing a command filter for a category of nonlinear systems featuring unknown control gains, exogenous disturbances, and state delays. A novel triple-parameter switching control strategy is introduced to tackle the issue of unknown control gains, incorporating online parameter tuning based on designed criteria to enable flexible gain scheduling and a finite number of switching instances. To overcome the drawback of conventional prescribed performance functions that depend on system initial states, a new class of fixed-time prescribed performance functions with automatic parameter tuning is developed, removing this reliance. A command-filter-based backstepping approach with adaptive parameters (bandwidth and damping factor) effectively circumvents the computational burden associated with high-order derivatives in conventional backstepping and enhances filtering adaptability. Furthermore, the problem of nondifferentiability of virtual controllers at switching instants in existing switching control methods is addressed, and a composite adaptive law is formulated to concurrently estimate unknown parameters, external disturbances, and time-delay terms. A reformulated Lyapunov function framework is employed to rigorously establish the boundedness of all signals within the closed-loop system and the convergence of the tracking error to the predefined bound within a pre-specified fixed time. Moreover, simulation outcomes confirm the efficacy of the proposed algorithm, demonstrating superior tracking performance, parameter estimation precision, and robust disturbance rejection capabilities.

title: Adaptive Fuzzy Control Based on Fully Actuated System Approach for Active Suspension Systems with Input Dead Zones
authors: Yongshuo Zhang, Xianbo Wang, Tenglong Huang, Menghua Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes an adaptive fuzzy control method for quarter vehicle active suspension systems, which is designed to specifically address the problems of system uncertainties and input dead zone constraints. The fuzzy control are adopted to approximate the system uncertainties and input dead zone characteristics. Meanwhile, drawing on the inherent characteristics and technical advantages of the fully-actuated system approach, an innovative adaptive fuzzy control method for active suspension systems based on the fully-actuated system is proposed. Compared with existing fully-actuated system control methods, the designed control method can ensure that the system states converge to zero, rather than being confined to a small neighborhood. Simulation results verify the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed control method.

title: Approximate Dynamic Programming Cooperative Game-Based Modular Robot Tracking Control via Error Cost Function
authors: Zebin Ji, Yi Qin, Bing Ma, Hucheng Jiang, Tianjiao An
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper presents a tracking control strategy for modular robots based on symmetric error cost function, aiming to address the limitations inherent in conventional positive-definite quadratic cost formulations. In such traditional formulations, the simultaneous presence of state and control variables in the cost functional gives rise to unbounded accumulation over an infinite time horizon, thereby precluding the attainment of a well-defined optimization solution. The dynamical model of the modular robotic system is derived from joint torque feedback measurements. By employing the principles of approximate dynamic programming, the trajectory tracking task is reformulated as an optimal control problem wherein each modular unit is regarded as a cooperating agent within a multi-player game framework. The cost function is approximated using a critic fuzzy network, from which an optimal tracking control law is subsequently synthesized. Experimental evaluations conducted on a physical prototype validate the effectiveness of the proposed methodology.

title: Enhanced Field Emission Performances of Single-Crystal SmB6 Nanowire Films by Gd Doping
authors: ChuQiao Hu, HaoJian Lin, JingShi Li, CaiJian Zhang, Yu Zhang, Shuai Tang, Jun Chen, Shaozhi Deng, Fei Liu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: SmB6 is a promising cold-cathode material due to their abundant surface electron states, low electron affinity and high electrical conductivity. To improve the field emission (FE) performances of SmB<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf> for practical applications, gadolinium-doped samarium hexaboride (Gd-doped SmB ${ }_{6}$) nanowire films as successfully synthesized on silicon (Si) substrates sing chemical vapor deposition (CVD) way. FE measurements demonstrate that the $\mathbf{1. 6} \mathbf{a t. \%}$ Gd-doped SmB. nanowire film has a low turn-on field of $2.53 \mathrm{~V} / \mu \mathrm{m}$ (at $10 \mu \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$) and achieves a maximum emission current density of up to $271.67 \mu \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$, which is much better than the undoped $\operatorname{SmB}_{6}$ nanowire film. These experimental results reveal that the Gd dopants can effectively improve the FE characteristics of SmB6 nanowire film, originating from the augment of the electrical conductivity, the increase of the aspect ratio and the decrease of surface work function. This work may give some helpful references to enhance the FE performances of cold-cathode nanodevices.

title: Emission Performance of a ZrO2-Doped Aluminate Dispenser Cathode
authors: Shixian Ding, Xiaoxia Wang, Ruoqi Zhang, Ziming Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Enhancing the performance of thermionic cathodes remains a persistent research objective. This work investigates a dispenser cathode impregnated with ZrO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>-doped aluminate. Pulsed emission tests revealed a current density inflection point of $18.61 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$ at $1100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, with a slope of 1.4, indicating both high emission capability and good emission uniformity. Furthermore, the Miram curve and the Practical Work Function Distribution (PWFD) curve demonstrate that the cathode exhibits a work function of $1.86-1.88 \mathrm{eV}$, which is lower than that of conventional barium-tungsten dispenser cathodes.

title: Fokker–Planck Soft Actor–Critic
authors: Hyo-Seok Hwang, Jaewon Kim, Junhee Seok
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Learning expressive and multimodal policies is essential for solving complex continuous control tasks. However, most reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms rely on unimodal or factorized Gaussian policies, limiting their representational flexibility. While soft actor–critic (SAC) encourages stochasticity through the maximum entropy RL framework, its reverse KL-based update biases the policy toward dominant modes, often resulting in mode collapse. In this work, we propose Fokker–Planck SAC (FP-SAC), a principled policy optimization algorithm that formulates soft policy improvement through stochastic differential equations and derives a distribution-level objective from the corresponding Fokker–Planck (FP) equation. By minimizing the residual of this partial differential equationusing physics-informed learning and leveraging the expressiveness of normalizing flows, FP-SAC enables stable and accurate learning of multimodal policies. Experiments on multigoal environments, MuJoCo and Meta-World benchmarks, demonstrate that FP-SAC captures multimodal behaviors more effectively and achieves higher task performance with greater stability compared to existing approaches.
