title: ESCORT: Enriched SAR Change detection via Copula Regression Technique
authors: Ahidjo Abdoulaye, Alejandro C. Frery, Mingyang Ma, Shuangxi Zhang, Shaohui Mei
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) change detection is a key tool for monitoring land-use and land-cover dynamics under all-weather and day/night conditions, yet its performance is often hindered by speckle noise, weak exploitation of spatial context, and limited modeling of nonlinear temporal dependencies. These issues make it difficult to reliably separate change and no-change regions, particularly in heterogeneous and transitional areas. This paper presents an Enriched SAR Change detection via Copula Regression Technique (ESCORT), which adopts a nonparametric framework designed to address these challenges through dependency-aware and spatially consistent modeling. Particularly, the core contribution lies in the use of local copula density (LCD) features, which capture nonlinear temporal dependencies between bi-titemporal SAR observations in a distribution-free manner and provide strong discriminative power under noise. To further stabilize these dependencies, spatial weighting kernels (SWK) are employed to enhance both the difference image and the input images while preserving spatial structure. Finally, an enriched feature construction strategy is proposed to explicitly encode neighborhood-level information, by which coherent change maps can be produced using a regularized kernel logistic regression (KLR) model with spatial consistency constraints. Experimental results on three diverse SAR datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework, achieving the highest Kappa scores on two datasets with relative Kappa improvement of up to 1.85% (up to 13.77% statistical gain) over the best baseline on San Francisco and/or Sulzberger datasets, while remaining competitive with recent deep learning approaches on the more challenging Ottawa dataset.

title: Online Stochastic Optimization for Coordinating EV Charging Stations with PV-Battery Systems
authors: Dafeng Zhu, Bo Yang, Zhao Yang Dong, Rui Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid
abstract: The surge in electric vehicle adoption strains charging stations (CSs) and the grid, calling for distributed resource integration and CS coordination to manage random supply and demand. This letter introduces a novel online stochastic optimization method that improves operation revenue and facilitates CS collaboration by ensuring the economic profitability of battery operations through a deterministic guarantee and setting battery-based prices. Simulations demonstrate near-optimal performance while upholding service quality and stability.

title: Improving Device Localization Accuracy Based on IEEE 802.11 FTM Protocol Through Deep Neural Networks
authors: Kacper Handzlik, Marek Natkaniec
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The goal of this paper is to enhance and combine existing methods of localization, such as the Fine Timing Measurement protocol and the Pedestrian Dead Reckoning algorithm, in small-scale indoor environments. Moreover, this paper proves that replacing most simple mathematical equations with Deep Neural Networks in positioning algorithms can provide better localization accuracy in small and domestic environments. The research addresses the critical challenge of achieving sub-meter accuracy in GPS-denied environments where traditional satellite-based positioning systems fail to operate effectively. Through comprehensive experimentation and validation, the paper demonstrates how machine learning techniques can compensate for the inherent limitations and noise present in sensor data from accelerometers, gyroscopes, and wireless signal measurements. The proposed hybrid approach leverages the complementary strengths of deterministic algorithms and adaptive neural networks to create a positioning system that can operate reliably across diverse indoor scenarios. Finally, the work establishes a framework for future research in AI-enhanced indoor positioning systems, providing both theoretical foundations and practical implementation guidelines for real-world deployment.

title: A Hierarchical Learning Framework for Microelectrode Manipulation Under Microscopic Vision_supp1-3696526.mp4
authors: Yao Guo
publish: N/A
abstract: Micromanipulation is a common task in a range of precision assembly and minimally invasive medical applications. Whilst handling rigid micronano targets is reaching maturity, the manipulation of thin, flexible objects–such as implanted neuro-electrodes for brain–machine interfacing–remains challenging due to their deformable nature and microscale characteristics. These challenges are further hampered by in vivo motion dynamics and limited focal depth of microscopic vision, hindering the precise execution of the task. Existing approaches relying on visual servoing have limited generalizability and robustness due to incomplete visual observation, including occlusion and defocus. To address these problems, we propose a hierarchical learning framework that endows the system with high-level decision-making and low-level motion control to automate micromanipulation. This framework, coupled with the hierarchical operational strategy, enables 3-D perception using a monocular microscope by exploiting implicit defocus cues while improving positional tracking accuracy under deformation. Experimental results using microneural-electrode manipulation demonstrate that our proposed framework surpasses conventional state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin.

title: Achieving Socially-Aware Car-Following in Mixed Traffic: A Safe Multi-Task Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Xiao Wen, Jingyuan Zhou, Sisi Jian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Current research on autonomous vehicle (AV) car-following control primarily emphasizes optimizing AV performance without considering interactions between AVs and human-driven vehicles (HDVs). This oversight can lead to socially non-compliant AV behaviors that confuse following HDVs, destabilize traffic flow, and increase congestion and crash risks. Moreover, while most studies employ safe deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods focused solely on AV safety, we argue that AVs, which are equipped with superior sensing and decision-making capabilities, should proactively contribute to the safety of following HDVs. To address these limitations, we propose a socially aware AV car-following controller based on social value orientation (SVO). Given the broad spectrum of SVO, we design a safe multi-task DRL (SMTDRL) framework that includes a single-task DRL backbone, a curriculum-based multi-task learning strategy for efficient cross-SVO knowledge transfer, and a differentiable safety module using control barrier functions (CBFs) to ensure safety for both the AV and its HDV followers. Comprehensive simulations demonstrate that SMTDRL improves traffic flow stability, ride comfort, and fuel economy while enforcing strict safety constraints at the platoon level, outperforming both single-task DRL and baseline multi-task DRL approaches. For example, in safety-critical scenarios, SMTDRL achieves a 100% collision-free rate, significantly outperforming baseline multi-task DRL approaches. In terms of traffic performance, it reduces the average cumulative damping ratio by approximately 7% compared to single-task DRL models. Furthermore, SMTDRL exhibits strong generalizability to unseen SVO values, highlighting its robust adaptability in mixed traffic environments.

title: Constrained Hybrid AI for Smart Classroom Audiovisual Diagnosis
authors: Pan Chang, Gang Xu
publish: IEEE Consumer Electronics Magazine
abstract: Smart classrooms are consumer-scale cyber-physical systems in which heterogeneous audiovisual (AV) failures must be diagnosed and resolved within a strict pre-class window. Conventional rule-based self-checking is fast but brittle under closed-world assumptions, while purely data-driven AI is difficult to deploy safely due to limited training data and the risk of unsafe actions on physical devices. This article presents a constrained hybrid diagnostic architecture, studied as an early deployment and integration effort, that combines deterministic rule-based self-checking, non-intrusive physical verification, and a large language model (LLM) used only as a constrained diagnostic planner. The system separates diagnostic reasoning from physical execution: rules and LLM proposals are represented as auditable check actions, all actions are routed through a policy enforcement point, and unsafe or high-impact requests are rejected before reaching devices. Controlled fault-injection experiments on a representative smart classroom testbed covered ten predefined fault categories and 100 repeated runs, while the architecture was deployed in 70 real classrooms. In this controlled setting, the constrained LLM planner achieved 99% accuracy of root-cause identification, generated safe diagnostic paths when rule priors were incomplete, reduced a representative symptom-driven diagnosis from 38 static checks to 7 checks, and produced 14 unsafe or out-of-scope requests, all of which were successfully blocked by the policy layer.

title: Dynamic-n-Static Multiplex Graph Representation Learning for Improved Link Prediction
authors: Jia Yu, Mengjun Ding, Weiqiang Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Social systems often involve multiple types of relations, each exhibiting distinct temporal characteristics. Such systems can be modeled as temporal multiplex graphs in which each graph layer represents one type of relation. In this article, we study the link prediction problem in a particular yet common class of multiplex graphs, namely dynamic-n-static multiplex graphs, which consist of both evolving dynamic layers and long-term static layers. We propose DS-MGN, a representation learning model for dynamic-n-static multiplex graphs. DS-MGN introduces a cross-layer neighbor encoding (CLNE) scheme, which injects stable topological priors from static layers as contextual information, thereby enhancing the representations of the more complex dynamic layers by encoding nodes’ structural connectivity patterns in the static layer. The CLNE scheme is architecture-compatible and can be seamlessly integrated into other models with similar neural network architectures, offering substantial performance improvements. Furthermore, to support research on such complex graph structures, we present a high-quality dynamic-n-static multiplex graph dataset, which includes a dynamic sports social network as well as a static network capturing long-term stable collegial relationships between members, meeting the need for complex and comprehensive multiplex graph datasets under practical data collection challenges. Extensive experiments on both a public benchmark and the collected dataset demonstrate that DS-MGN achieves state-of-the-art performance against 12 strong baselines. Moreover, our analyses highlight its scalability and potential for real-world applications, including evolving social network analysis, professional collaboration tracking, and dynamic recommendation systems.

title: Task-Driven Generative Prompt Learning for Vision-Language Pretrained Model
authors: Qirui Wu, Shizhou Zhang, De Cheng, Yinghui Xing, Guoqiang Liang, Kairui Dang, Yanning Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: With the emergence of vision-language pre-trained models(VLM) like CLIP and ALIGN, transferable representations can be adapted to a wide range of downstream tasks via prompt tunning. While existing learnable prompt tuning approaches like CoOp and VPT have proved effective across varied tasks, their reliance on fixed parameters limits adaptability to new tasks without additional offline retraining. To adapt the pre-trained model to various tasks without the necessity for iterative offline training, we seek a task-driven prompt generator to map the tasks into prompts with a single feed-word process. In this paper, we propose a novel Generative Prompt (GPrompt) for vision-language pre-trained models, which is produced through Task-driven Prompt Generator (TDPG) driven by flexible and accurate task descriptors extracted from downstream samples. A flexible and simple inference pipeline is designed for efficient adaptation of our proposed method. Our method is able to surpass backpropagating-free prompt-based methods by +9.3%. Additionally, combined with existing training-free metric-based methods such as Tip-Adapter further improves the performance. Extensive experimental results on 11 datasets demonstrated the effectiveness of our proposed methods.

title: A Novel Hybrid-Transverse-Flux Multi-Stator Dual-Electrical-Port Skew-Teeth Machine for Mild Hybrid Vehicles
authors: Ziqi Huang, Mingyuan Jiang, Shuangxia Niu, Zhenghao Li, Feifan Ni
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: This paper presents a novel hybrid-flux dual-electrical-port machine that ingeniously combines an axial-flux machine and a transverse-flux machine via a shared common rotor to achieve efficient energy distribution for mild hybrid vehicles. The proposed integrated topology features dual electrical ports and adapts well to diverse operating conditions in mild hybrid vehicles. To evaluate the performance of the proposed machine, 3-D finite element analysis (FEA) was carried out to investigate its back electromotive force (EMF), cogging torque, output torque, and torque ripple under motor/generator modes. The results confirm that the skew-teeth design effectively reduces torque ripple and enhances output torque. The machine can switch between motor and generator modes to meet various work scenarios. The proposed machine has high torque density, low cogging torque, and excellent fault tolerance, making it very suitable for mild hybrid vehicle applications.

title: Complex Frequency-Domain Finite Element Analysis for Magneto-Mechanical Coupling in Amorphous Wound Cores
authors: Shengze Gao, Ruihang Li, Xiaojun Zhao, Fangyu Gao, Haoda Li, Lanrong Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: To accurately simulate the vibration characteristics of soft magnetic wound cores under square-wave excitation, a magneto‑mechanical coupling finite element method (FEM) in the complex frequency domain is proposed. Based on harmonic balance theory, the Laplace transform is employed to model the nonlinear magnetic field, while parallel computing is incorporated to improve computational efficiency. The equivalent mechanical parameters of the wound core, the homogeneous Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio, are determined and used to construct a 2D magneto‑mechanical coupling model. The simulation results, including magnetic field distributions and vibrational displacements, are validated against experimental measurements of an amorphous wound core, and the factors affecting computational efficiency are analyzed.

title: Modal Analysis of Open Gyrotropic Waveguides
authors: Konstantinos Delimaris, Georgios D. Kolezas, Carsten Rockstuhl, Grigorios P. Zouros
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: We report on the modal analysis of open gyrotropic waveguides (GWs). The GWs consist of a noncircular gyrotropic (i.e., gyroelectric and gyromagnetic) core and an infinitely extending isotropic cladding. To solve this problem, we develop two independent full-wave methods. The first technique is an extended integral equation (EIE) method, an extension of a previously developed EIE used to calculate the propagation constants in composite dielectric-isotropic waveguides. The second technique is a Chebyshev expansion method (CEM). In both implementations, the electric and magnetic fields in the gyrotropic core are expanded in superpotential-based cylindrical vector wave functions (SUPER-CVWFs), recently developed for the problem of oblique multiple scattering by gyrotropic cylinders. Both techniques allow us to calculate the propagation constants in the general case, without approximations. Various noncircular GWs are considered. The EIE and CEM results are validated against a commercial finite element solver, and the accuracy and computational performance of both methods are benchmarked. A microwave application is presented where the complex propagation constants of ferrite rods are computed in the presence of an external magnetic flux density bias. Our work advances the theory of propagation in open waveguides, from dielectric/isotropic to gyrotropic ones, and enables the design of contemporary waveguiding structures.

title: Generalized Kullback-Leibler Divergence Loss
authors: Jiequan Cui, Beier Zhu, Qingshan Xu, Zhuotao Tian, Xiaojuan Qi, Bei Yu, Hanwang Zhang, Richang Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: In this paper, we delve deeper into the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence loss and mathematically prove that it is equivalent to the Decoupled Kullback-Leibler (DKL) Divergence loss that consists of 1) a weighted Mean Square Error ($\mathbf {w}$MSE) loss and 2) a Cross-Entropy loss incorporating soft labels. Thanks to the decoupled structure of DKL loss, we have identified two areas for improvement. Firstly, we address the limitation of KL loss in scenarios like knowledge distillation by breaking its asymmetric optimization property along with a smoother weight function. This modification effectively alleviates convergence challenges in optimization, particularly for classes with high teacher-predicted scores. Secondly, we introduce class-wise global information into KL/DKL to reduce bias arising from individual samples. With these two enhancements, we derive the Generalized Kullback-Leibler (GKL) Divergence loss and evaluate its effectiveness by conducting experiments on CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet, and vision-language datasets, focusing on adversarial training and knowledge distillation tasks. Specifically, we achieve new state-of-the-art adversarial robustness on the public leaderboard — RobustBench and competitive knowledge distillation performance across CIFAR/ImageNet models and CLIP models, demonstrating the substantial practical merits.

title: A Fault Detection Scheme for UAV Swarm Networks Based on Dynamic Clustering and Hybrid Trust Mechanism
authors: Zhunyuan Ye, Zhihao Xu, Ke Zhang, Bin Jiang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a fault detection scheme based on dynamic clustering and a hybrid trust mechanism for largescale UAV swarms, addressing the issues of high detection latency and high false alarm rates caused by unstable communication links in flight environments. First, an adaptive clustering algorithm is proposed to construct a stable clustered network topologyto facilitate efficient intra-cluster fault detection. Second, a hybrid trust evaluation model integrating personal trust and interaction trust is designed to enable cluster head nodes to effectively distinguish node faults from link interference. Finally, the proposed scheme is evaluated through multiple sets of simulation experiments.

authors: Ana Margarida Ferreira, Inês Camaño Garcia, Hernâni Alves, Inês Lopes, Natacha Ribeiro Pinto, Estrela Nunes, Tabata Aviles Parra

publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 3: Workshops, Situated Actions, and PDC Places

title: Practical Fixed-Time Tracking Control for Nonlinear High-Order Fully Actuated System with Unknown Control Coefficients
authors: Ping Wang, Liang Feng, Weizhen Liu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper addresses the practical fixed-time (FxT) tracking control for nonlinear high-order fully actuated (HOFA) systems with unknown control coefficients and nonlinear dynamics. First, an adaptive compensation law is designed by integrating neural network approximation with adaptive techniques, incorporating both negative low-order and high-order terms to effectively compensate for the system's unknown nonlinearities within a fixed time. Based on fully actuated system (FAS) theory, an adaptive FxT tracking controller is then developed, incorporating the Nussbaum gain function and intermediate control variables to manage the impact of unknown control coefficients on system stability. Specifically, the intermediate control variables include negative fractional-order terms of the tracking error to promote FxT convergence of the closed-loop system. Using FxT stability analysis theory, it is rigorously proven that the controller ensures practical fixed-time convergence of the closed-loop system. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed control scheme is validated through simulations of a pendulum system model.

title: Remotely Operated Vehicle Path Planning Based on Reward Gradient Prioritized Experience Replay
authors: Chunhui Liu, Mingyang Li, Peng Xu, Zhixue Li, Peiyu Qiu, Zonglei Mou
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has great potential in 3D path planning for Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs). However, crucial experiences of safely reaching the goal are often sparse in complex underwater environments. This leads to training difficulties and low sample efficiency, resulting in a low navigation success rate. Therefore, a Reward Gradient Prioritized Experience Replay (RGPER) mechanism is proposed based on the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) framework. The RGPER mechanism uses the temporal difference error as the priority base and designs the reward gradient of adjacent control steps as a dynamic enhancement factor. By filtering small reward changes through a smooth truncation function, the proposed method can recognize crucial experiences and increase their sampling weights. The experimental results demonstrate great improvements. Compared with PER, the RGPER increases navigation success rate by 2.8%, while the average path length decreases by 7.8%, and energy consumption by 18.7%. The results show that RGPER effectively enhances the reliability and energy efficiency of ROVs' path planning.

title: Advancing Environmental Sustainability: Exploring Pathways of SMEs' Green Innovation in Different Green Performance Levels within Sustainable Supply Chain Management
authors: Min Zhou, Kannan Govindan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management
abstract: Existing literature on small and medium-sized enterprises' (SMEs) green innovation within sustainable supply chains often assumes a uniform adoption path, overlooking the critical role of green performance heterogeneity. This study addresses this gap by investigating how green performance levels fundamentally alter the configurational pathways to SMEs' green innovation. Grounded in a context-specific technology organization-environment (TOE) framework—integrating green technology bricolage (GTB), top management's green cognition (TMGC), and network embeddedness (NE), we triangulate data from 263 Chinese SMEs using fuzzy-set qualitative comparative analysis and necessary condition analysis, supported by extensive robustness checks to ensure a high level of research rigor. The results reveal causal asymmetry and distinct innovation logics. For high-green performance SMEs, green innovation is primarily driven by the synergy of green technology bricolage and network embeddedness, often without high managerial cognition. Conversely, low-green performance SMEs necessitate high top management's green cognition and network embeddedness to overcome green innovation inertia. By demonstrating that SMEs' green innovation pathways are contingent on past green performance, this study resolves fragmentation in the literature and offers tailored guidance for fostering sustainability in diverse SME contexts.

title:
Broadband double-layer electromagnetic wave absorber based on Nd
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Fe
                    <sub>17</sub>
                    N
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    with planar magnetocrystalline anisotropy
authors: Naoki Mitsui, Jun Akamatsu, Satoshi Abe, Ryoya Okazaki, Nobuyoshi Imaoka
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: We designed and prepared a double-layer electromagnetic wave absorber using Nd<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">17</sub>N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> as the absorbing material, which exhibits a positive imaginary part of complex relative permeability over a wide frequency range from the GHz to sub-THz band owing to its high planar magnetocrystalline anisotropy and high saturation magnetization. Consequently, we fabricated a prototype of a double-layer absorber that achieved −20 dB reflection loss over a 24.7 GHz bandwidth spanning 68.0–92.7 GHz at a thickness of 1.16 mm, excluding the reflective backing layer. Comparison with a double-layer absorber employing Sr-ferrite as the absorbing material revealed that the Nd<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">17</sub>N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>-based absorber can achieve more than twice the −20 dB absorption bandwidth. This broad absorption bandwidth can be attributed to the combined effects of broadband magnetic loss in Nd<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">17</sub>N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> and two independent phase-matching conditions owing to the presence of two distinct layers. These results contribute to the development of broadband electromagnetic wave absorbers and provide an effective means of exploiting the distinctive material properties of Nd<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">17</sub>N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>.

title: Examining the Dynamics of Vertical Integration Strategies: Implications for Inventory Performance and Supply Chain Positioning_supp1-3699371.pdf
authors: LI Qi
publish: N/A
abstract: This study examines how vertical integration (VI)—specifically forward integration (FI) and backward integration (BI)—affects inventory performance (IP) and how this relationship depends on a firm's position in the supply chain. Using panel data from Chinese manufacturing firms listed on the Shanghai and Shenzhen stock exchanges from 2007 to 2018, we analyze the effects of VI strategies on different types of inventory. The results show that FI improves the performance of finished goods inventory, whereas BI enhances the management of raw materials inventory. Further analysis indicates that downstream firms benefit more from BI, while upstream firms gain greater advantages from FI. Drawing on Transaction Cost Economics and the Resource-Based View, we explain how VI helps firms reduce coordination costs and leverage internal capabilities to improve inventory efficiency. To ensure the robustness of our findings, we conduct several additional tests, including instrumental variable estimation, regression analyses by inventory type, subgroup analyses of upstream and downstream industries, and alternative measures of inventory performance. The results remain consistent across these tests. These findings contribute to the literature on vertical integration and supply chain management and provide practical guidance for firms seeking to align VI strategies with their supply chain position to improve inventory efficiency.

title: Recent Improvements to the Overmoded X-band Magnetically Insulated Line Oscillator
authors: Adam Brusstar, Ryan Revolinsky, Emma Guerin, Zachary Rabine, Vincent Vaughn, Nicholas M. Jordan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Mode competition in magnetically insulated line oscillators is greatly affected by the symmetry of the slow-wave structure and feedback from the extractor region. Analysis of mode competition exhibited in experiment and simulation of the University of Michigan’s overmoded X-band magnetically insulated line oscillator led to a new design to mitigate these issues. A precision alignment system for the MILO cathode based on exact constraint principles was designed and fabricated, and the support posts of the beam dump and extractor were carefully optimized. Simulations of the new design show improved output mode purity and the complete elimination of mode competition. Experimental results are forthcoming.

title: On the Feasibility Boundaries of LLM Prefill Inference on Unified-Memory Edge SoCs
authors: Juyeon Lee, Jiayue Wang, Soojung Ryu, Hyuk-Jae Lee
publish: IEEE Computer Architecture Letters
abstract: LLM inference on unified-memory (UM) edge SoCs is commonly assumed to follow a GPU-centric execution model once nominal memory capacity appears sufficient. Our experiments on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano 8GB reveal a prefill feasibility boundary in the evaluated modified FlexGen-based runtime, where execution stability becomes sensitive to runtime memory-management behavior. Under GPU-centric execution, collapse is first observed at batch size 8 with zram enabled, and the boundary shifts to batch size 16 under a modified memory configuration. At these boundary points, completion becomes probabilistic, with only 23%–33% of runs succeeding; all failures occur before decode. Notably, collapse can occur despite apparent physical-memory headroom, showing that nominal capacity alone does not determine execution stability. These findings show that, in the evaluated setup, prefill completion acts as an effective feasibility gate: latency and throughput become meaningful only after execution feasibility is established.

title: 2026 IEEE RAS Award Recipients Announced [Society News]
authors: John Leonard, Henrik Christensen, Yi Guo, Somil Bansal, Chuchu Fan, Robert Katzschmann, Cristina Piazza, Xiaolong Wang, Jur van den Berg, Ming Lin, Dinesh Manocha
publish: IEEE Robotics & Automation Magazine
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: Safe Reinforcement Learning Control for Unmanned Surface Vessel
authors: Yana Yang, Chunyu Liu, Tengfei Zhang, Changchun Hua, Jianxiong Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Unmanned surface vessel (USV) faces three core challenges in achieving safe, efficient, and adaptive autonomous navigation in complex dynamic waters. Reinforcement Learning (RL) policies are prone to violating physical constraints during exploration, leading to hazardous behaviors. Existing safety mechanisms struggle to ensure dynamic safety at the fundamental level. The differences in safety constraints between source and target domains during transfer learning result in unstable policy transfer and low sample efficiency. To address these issues, this paper proposes a safety reinforcement learning framework that integrates geometric mapping and control theory. First, a structured transfer paradigm based on predefined coordinate transformations is constructed to map unconstrained source domain policies into the safe subspace of the target domain, eliminating inter-domain safety boundary differences at the geometric level. This enables zero-risk initialization and deployment of policies, resolving the issue of safety instability during transfer. Second, a safety correction module embedded with a Control Lyapunov Function (CLF) is designed to project the raw actions of the policy in real time into a safe set that satisfies Lyapunov stability conditions. This constrains action feasibility from the perspective of control theory at its root, avoids reliance on reward design, and ensures hard safety boundaries under dynamic uncertainties. Furthermore, by decoupling policy capability learning from target domain safety constraints and leveraging pre trained knowledge from the source domain, the framework accelerates convergence in the target domain, significantly improves sample efficiency, and circumvents the trade-off between safety and efficiency. Finally, simulation demonstrate that the proposed method achieves significantly faster training convergence in tasks such as obstacle avoidance and target tracking, while maintaining strong robustness under environmental disturbances.

title: Trajectory tracking of Wind Turbine Blade Mountain Transporter based on High-order Fully Actuated MPC
authors: Yuhao Jiang, Yahui Zhang, Tenglong Huang, Boqiang Zhang, Yinshu Wang, Xin Jin, Xiaohong Jiao, Guilin Wen
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the trajectory tracking control problem of a wind turbine blade mountain transporter (WTBMT) operating in complex mountainous environments, where strong kinematic coupling and external disturbances impose stringent requirements on tracking accuracy and real-time control. A high-order fully actuated (HOFA) based model predictive control (MPC) framework is developed to transform the original nonlinear and underactuated vehicle kinematics into a linear predictive model suitable for real-time optimization. On this basis, the MPC optimizes a virtual control input to achieve high-precision trajectory tracking, while a disturbance observer is incorporated to compensate for matched disturbances at the actuator level. Simulation results under realistic mountain-road conditions verify the effectiveness and real-time performance of the proposed HOFA-DOMPC strategy.

title: Improved Algorithm for Asymptotically Optimal Path Planning of Mobile Robots
authors: Wei Zheng, Yingxu Li, Yinan Liu, Weiyuan Yan, Lianbin Duan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: The bidirectional Rapidly-exploring Random Tree star (BI-RRT*) algorithm is widely used for robot path planning due to its ability to generate collision-free and asymptotically optimal solutions. However, it still suffers from several limitations, including inefficient goal-directed sampling, redundant path nodes, and insufficient path smoothness. This paper proposes an improved BI-RRT* algorithm with two key enhancements. First, a goal-biased sampling strategy is introduced to guide tree expansion toward the goal region, thereby accelerating feasible-path discovery and improving exploration efficiency. Second, a node optimization mechanism is applied after initial path generation to eliminate redundant nodes, refine the path structure, and shorten the path length. Simulation results in multiple environments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Compared to the traditional RRT-Connect algorithm, the Improved BI-RRT* algorithm reduces the average path length by 11.9 % (from 1054.2 m to 928.6 m), reduces the number of path nodes by 68.9 % (from 24.7 to 7.7), and shortens the planning time by 13.0 % (from 7.24 s to 6.30 s), based on the three experimental map models. These results indicate that the improved algorithm achieves faster convergence and generates smoother and more efficient paths.

title: The Transition between Electron Emission Mechanisms in a Nonplanar Vacuum Crossed-Field Gap
authors: Lorin I. Breen, Jack K. Wright, Allen L. Garner
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Crossed-field devices are used in numerous applications, such as high-power microwaves and Hall thrusters. Previous studies have characterized the transition from thermal-field emission to the limiting current in a vacuum crossedfield gap, which is important for characterizing and improving device efficiency and operation. These studies considered planar devices; however, practical devices are nonplanar, such as cylindrical. Recent studies have applied variational calculus to determine the limiting current for nonplanar crossed-field devices and point transformations to assess the transitions between electron emission mechanisms for nonplanar geometries. This study applies these results to identify the transitions between individual electron emission mechanisms and the limiting current to determine the underlying physics dominating the emission behavior in relevant regimes. Specifically, we characterize the transitions between thermal emission, field emission, and the limiting current in vacuum for a crossed-field gap in spherical, cylindrical, and prolate spheroidal coordinate systems.

title: Adaptive Consensus Control for Nonlinear Multi-Agent Systems With Edge-Event-Triggered Communication Under Dos Attacks
authors: Weishang Sun, Changchun Hua, Ruixue Cui
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper addresses the consensus control problem for nonlinear multi-agent systems under DoS attacks. A distributed edge-triggered adaptive control protocol is proposed, which employs an edge-triggered mechanism to achieve asynchronous communication scheduling for resource efficiency, and incorporates an adaptive controller to counteract attack-induced effects. Theoretical analysis derives sufficient conditions for system stability and quantifies the impact of attacks on performance. Finally, simulation results on a single-link robotic manipulator demonstrate that the proposed protocol effectively conserves communication resources while maintaining resilience against DoS attacks.

title: Non-Relativistic Crossed-Field Devices for HPM
authors: Michael S. Worthington, Alan Palevsky
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This abstract discusses the possible use of non-relativistic high power crossed-field devices for HPM. Empirical results from several magnetrons and CFAs that achieved powers up to 20 MW will be presented as well as plan for a $20+\mathrm{MW}$ magnetron.

title: Influence of Reflector Transparency and Axial Magnetic Field on Efficiency and Mode Purity of a Multi-reflector Axial Vircator
authors: Nani Medicherla, Abhishek Kumar Jha, R. Seshadri, Subrata Kumar Datta, M. V. Kartikeyan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: A multistage axial virtual cathode oscillator (vircator) employing finite-transparency reflector disks is investigated using self-consistent particle-in-cell simulations. The combined influence of reflector transparency and axial magnetic field on RF power, spectral purity, and mode selectivity is examined. Reflector transparency is varied from 80% to 100% using PEC, copper, and stainless-steel reflectors. Results show that high reflector transparency ($\geq 90 {\%}$) is the dominant factor enabling stable, spectrally pure ${TM}_{01}$ oscillations, while suppressing competing TE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</inf> modes. A weak axial magnetic field ($0.005-0.01 \mathrm{~T}$) further enhances TM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">01</inf> stability but cannot compensate for low transparency. The study establishes practical design guidelines for compact, magnet-free or weakly magnetized multistage vircators.

title: Short-Term Photovoltaic Forecasting Based on Transformer-BiLSTM
authors: Xiao Yuan Luo, He Xiang Liu, Xin Yu Wang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Addressing the issue of insufficient accuracy in traditional single-model photovoltaic power prediction under complex weather conditions such as cloudy and rainy weather, this paper proposes a combined prediction model that integrates Transformer and Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory(BiLSTM). The model first utilizes the self-attention mechanism of Transformer to globally model historical Photovoltaic(PV) power sequences and multi-source meteorological features such as irradiance and temperature, effectively capturing long-range dependencies and complex interactions among multiple variables. Subsequently, it employs the BiLSTM network to perform bidirectional fine temporal learning on the extracted high-level features, enhancing the model's ability to depict local dynamic processes such as power fluctuations and climbs. Experiments based on measured data from PV power stations in Ningxia region demonstrate that the proposed model significantly outperforms comparative models such as Long Short-Term Memory(LSTM), Graph Convolutional Neural Networks(GCN), and Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) in terms of MAE, RMSE, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{2}$</tex> under various meteorological conditions, including sunny, cloudy, and rainy days. Ablation experiments confirm the functional complementarity and synergistic effectiveness of the Transformer and BiLSTM modules, and test results across different seasons further verify the model's robustness and generalization ability, providing an effective solution for improving short-term to ultra-short-term PV power prediction accuracy.

title: Stability Limit and Extinction Mechanism of Continuous-Wave Magnetrons under Load Mismatch
authors: Kaicheng Wang, Yuzhe Xue, Man Zhang, Pan He, Yun Zhang, Shibin Xu, Licun Wang, Yong Yin
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This paper investigates the operational stability limits of a continuous-wave ($C W$) magnetron under mismatched load conditions using Particle-in-Cell (PIC) simulations. A 2.5 GHz ten-vane magnetron model with voltage standing wave ratio (VSWR) of 2.0 was analyzed by scanning load phases and ramping cathode voltage. Simulation results reveal that the system is most vulnerable to collapse at the phase of maximum frequency pulling, rather than the power sink region. Time-frequency spectral analysis confirms that the operational failure results from fundamental $\pi$-mode extinction rather than mode jumping. Based on Slater’s theory, this phenomenon is attributed to excessive circuit conductance caused by severe detuning $\left(G_{\text {circuit }}(\omega)\gt g_{e}\right)$, which exceeds the electronic power generation capability.

title: Inertia-Matching FAS Conversion and Stability Analysis for Underactuated Lagrangian Systems
authors: Xiaoshuang Zhou, Yana Yang, Tengfei Zhang, Changchun Hua, Junpeng Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Fully actuated system (FAS) theory provides a systematic framework for control design, but its direct application to underactuated Lagrangian systems is hindered by inertial coupling, which introduces unactuated accelerations into the output dynamics. This paper proposes an inertia-matching-based conversion framework for a class of underactuated Lagrangian systems with symmetric inertia structures. By constructing an output that satisfies a precise inertia-matching condition, the unactuated accelerations are exactly eliminated from the output dynamics. The solvability and integrability conditions of this matching are established, and the corresponding zero dynamics are explicitly derived. For the regulation case, a conditional energy-based internal-stability analysis is provided. Simulations on a double-pendulum crane illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed conversion and the resulting nominal control performance.

title: Simulation Study of Nanoscale Air-Channel Transistors Enabled by Ion Implantation
authors: Junxiang Yang, Ruihan Huang, Yuting Zhou, Feiliang Chen, Jian Zhang, Mo Li
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Nanoscale air-channel transistors (NACTs) have attracted considerable attention because of their highspeed, high-frequency potential and radiation hardness. In previous work, NACTs with saturation characteristics were realized based on ion implantation technique. In this work, the influence of gate width and gate distance on the electrical characteristics of NACTs is investigated. The findings provide practical design guidelines for strengthening gate control and facilitate the deployment of NACTs in high-frequency amplification applications.

title: An Empirical Investigation on Source Code Comprehension Performance Between Naming and Structural Issues with and without Generative AI Assistance_supp1-3702933.pdf
authors: Shotaro Inoue
publish: N/A
abstract: Context: Software developers frequently comprehend source code with code smells that reduce readability. A previous study conducted without generative AI assistance reported that source code with naming issues required greater cognitive loads than that with structural issues. Objective: This study investigated whether accuracy and time differed when comprehending source code with naming or structural issues with and without generative AI assistance. Method: We conducted two experiments with 106 software development practitioners. In the first experiment, the subjects comprehended the source code with either naming or structural issues without generative AI assistance and answered nine questions about the source code. In the second experiment, the subjects performed the same task but with generative AI assistance. Additionally, we performed propensity score matching and compared the subjects in both experiments. Results: In the first experiment, unpaired ttest results did not show a statistically significant difference in the percentage of correct answers between naming (mean 70.5%) and structural (mean 79.8%) issues after applying Holm’s correction (adjusted p = 0.0652, p = 0.0326) and no significant difference in the comprehension time (adjusted p = 0.329, p = 0.329). In the second experiment, no significant differences in the percentage of correct answers or comprehension time between naming and structural issues were observed. In the propensity score matching comparison, 25 pairs were matched. The results of the unpaired t-test did not show a statistically significant difference in comprehension time between those without generative AI assistance (56.2 min) and those with assistance (45.1 min) when comprehending source code with naming issues (adjusted p = 0.0622, p = 0.0311). There was a significant difference in the percentage of correct answers between those without generative AI assistance (78.2%) and those with assistance (66.7%) when comprehending source code with structural issues (adjusted p = 0.0354, p = 0.0177). Conclusion: This study indicates that the effectiveness of generative AI assistance can be influenced by differences in the types of code smells in the source code and suggests why the effectiveness of generative AI assistance in source code comprehension has varied across previous studies.

title: Dataset Pruning: Reducing Training Data by Examining SGD-Influence
authors: Shuo Yang, Yucheng Huang, Zeke Xie, Ping Li, Min Xu, Liqiang Nie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: The great success of deep learning relies heavily on ever-increasing amounts of training data, which incurs enormous computational and infrastructural costs. This raises crucial questions: Does all training data contribute equally to a model's performance? How much does each individual training sample or sub-training set affect the model's generalization, and how can we construct the smallest proxy training set without significantly sacrificing performance? To address these questions, we propose dataset pruning, an optimization-based sample selection method that (1) examines the influence of removing particular training samples on the model's generalization ability with high computational efficiency and theoretical guarantees, and (2) constructs the smallest subset of training data that yields a strictly constrained generalization gap. The empirically observed generalization gap achieved by dataset pruning is largely consistent with our theoretical expectations. To estimate each sample's influence, we develop an SGD-Influence method that tracks parameter changes during stochastic gradient descent, effectively overcoming the convexity and optimality assumptions required by traditional influence function approaches. Complementing this, our distributed discrete optimization partitions the dataset into manageable buckets, allowing for efficient sample selection without sacrificing quality. Extensive experiments on three datasets of varying scale and complexity demonstrate that our approach not only aligns well with theoretical expectations but also outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Notably, compared to our previous work, the proposed method achieves a 61.26% reduction in computational cost while delivering higher accuracy.

title: Haptic Tracing: A New Paradigm for Spatialized Vibrotactile Haptic Rendering_supp1-3702732.pdf
authors: Tom Roy
publish: N/A
abstract: Haptic technology enhances interactive experiences by providing force and tactile feedback, improving user performance and immersion. However, despite advancements, creating tactile experiences still remains challenging due to device diversity and complexity. Most available haptic frameworks rely on trigger-based or event-based systems, and disregard the 3D scene information to render haptic information. This paper introduces Haptic Tracing, a novel method for spatial tactile haptic rendering that simplifies the creation of interactive haptic experiences without relying on physical simulations. It uses concepts from visual and audio rendering to model and propagate haptic information through a 3D scene. The paper also describes how our proposed haptic rendering method can be used to create a vibrotactile rendering system, enabling the creation of perceptually coherent and dynamic haptic interactions. Finally, the paper discusses a user study comparing haptic rendering techniques that combine different propagation strategies and modulation functions. The results show that our approach significantly enhances the realism and expressivity of the haptic feedback, showcasing its potential for developing more complex and realistic haptic experiences.

title: Collaborative Orthogonal Learning for Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization_supp1-3700184.pdf
authors: Chengyu Hu
publish: N/A
abstract: Balancing feasibility, convergence, and diversity is a fundamental challenge in constrained multi-objective optimization, particularly in landscapes with irregular or disconnected boundaries. Existing methods primarily exchange solution positions but neglect valuable convergence direction information contained within the evolutionary process, which may lead to inefficient and redundant searches for the constrained Pareto front (CPF). To address this, we propose a dual-swarm competitive optimizer with collaborative orthogonal learning (COL) strategy, which effectively decouples global exploration and diversity exploitation. Specifically, the main swarm performs trend learning to identify convergence directions from boundary and winner-loser interaction information, enabling global exploration through infeasible regions toward the CPF. Guided by the learned trends, the auxiliary swarm executes orthogonal learning to search complementary subspaces, which broadens the solution distribution while avoiding redundant searches, ensuring diversity exploitation capability. Additionally, a niche-guided subset selection strategy is introduced to maintain uniform distribution within the objective space through a three-level subset division mechanism based on local niche capacity. Extensive experiments on standard and extended benchmark instances demonstrate the robustness and superiority of our approach over nine state-of-the-art methods.

title: Design Method of Third-Order Intermodulation for Helix Traveling-Wave Tubes
authors: Wenkai Deng, Yang Ji, Xinna Zhang, Baodong Zhou, Li Qiu, Dapeng Gong, Shenglong Guo, Wenjing Cai, Xiaolin Jin, Tao Huang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a design method for the third-order intermodulation distortion (IM3) of traveling wave tubes (TWTs) based on the field relative phase. By analyzing the relative phase between the fundamental wave and intermodulation signals, this method enables the suppression of IM3 values under different output back-offs $(\mathrm{O B O})$ conditions, while maintaining the output power essentially unchanged. The interaction circuit of a C-band helix TWT is designed using this method. The results demonstrate that the IM3 values are reduced across the range from $\mathrm{OBO}-3 \mathrm{~dB}$ to $\mathrm{OBO}-20 \mathrm{~dB}$, which verifies the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Neural-Network-Based Predefined-Time Formation Control of Multiple Unmanned Surface Vessels With Switching Topologies
authors: Zuo Wang, Hui Wang, Wenhui Dong, Yanzheng Zhu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this paper, the predefined-time formation control issue is studied for multiple unmanned surface vessels (USVs) subject to switching topologies and uncertain nonlinear dynamics. Unknown nonlinearities inherent in vessel dynamics are compensated by neural network approximation techniques, and the conservatism associated with fixed topology is eliminated by introducing the switching topologies. A distributed adaptive formation controller is structured via backstepping to accomplish the formation control objective for multiple USVs with switching topologies. The efficacy of the proposed control scheme is validated by simulation results.

title: Event-Triggered Control of Helicopter Systems Using the High-Order Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Zifan Liu, Chengxiang Li, Lantao Xing
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this paper, the high-order fully actuated (HOFA) system approach is employed to investigate the event-triggered control problem for a two-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) helicopter system. First, the 2-DOF helicopter dynamics are transformed into an HOFA system model using the “ascending dimension and descending order” method. On this basis, an event-triggered control scheme driven by variations of system outputs is developed. The proposed method not only simplifies the controller design but also significantly reduces communication resource consumption. It is shown that, under the proposed control strategy, all signals in the closed-loop system are globally bounded and Zeno behavior is excluded. Finally, simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the HOFA-based event-triggered control approach.

title: FinSAA: Sparse-Aware CNN Accelerator With Fast Irregular Index Decoding and Adaptive Dense-Sparse Convolution Architecture
authors: Qikang Li, Lixun Wang, Huihong Zhang, Pengjun Wang, Mengfan Xu, Yong Ding, Yuejun Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Fine-grained sparsity offers significant potential for accelerating convolutional neural networks (CNNs) by enabling effective model compression. However, the irregularity in data distribution presents challenges for hardware, including low processing element (PE) utilization and high overhead in sparse index addressing. Compared with dense computation, these issues hinder the efficiency of sparse computation. To address these challenges, this work proposes a CNN accelerator architecture that integrates both sparse and dense computation modes, aiming to enhance hardware resource utilization and inference performance. First, a row-merging optimization strategy is applied to alleviate workload imbalance in fine-grained sparse convolutions. A spatially aware sparse decoding module is then designed to enable fast and accurate mapping of sparse indices. Additionally, an outer product-based Winograd transform optimization is introduced, combined with unified sparse-dense processing units, reducing transformation overhead by 35%–51%. The proposed architecture is implemented on an Xilinx XC7Z100 platform. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed accelerator achieves peak performance of 862, 420, and 314 GOPS on AlexNet, ResNet, and MobileNetV2, respectively. Compared with prior work, it delivers <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.8\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.1\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> performance improvement and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.47\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.41\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> higher DSP efficiency, clearly demonstrating its versatility and effectiveness.

title: FISHER: A Foundation Model for Multimodal Industrial Signal Comprehensive Representation
authors: Pingyi Fan, Anbai Jiang, Shuwei Zhang, Xinhu Zheng, Zhiqiang Lv, Bing Han, Wenrui Liang, Junjie Li, Wei-Qiang Zhang, Yanmin Qian, Xie Chen, Jia Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Industrial signal analysis is hindered by severe data heterogeneity, which we characterize as the M5 problem. Existing solutions rely on specialized models that lack robustness and scalability, while large-scale pretraining has rarely been investigated in this area. In this work, we derive a prioritized roadmap for the M5 problem and propose FISHER, a <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">F</u>oundation model for multimodal <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">I</u>ndustrial <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">S</u>ignal compre<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">HE</u>nsive <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</u>epresentation. To address the foremost multisampling-rate problem, FISHER utilizes a novel sub-band modeling approach that treats sampling rate increments as concatenated sub-band information, enabling the adaptive usage of full signal bandwidth without resampling. FISHER is pretrained by teacher–student self-distillation over external audio and music data. We also establish the RMIS benchmark, comprising 19 datasets across four modalities. In the experiment, FISHER outperforms 24 state-of-the-art series encoders (up to 2B) with much smaller sizes (up to 16×), showcasing groundbreaking diagnostic accuracy and remarkable versatility. We further demonstrate that 1) seamless adaptation to variable sampling rates is the key to generalization 2) audio and music data provide better temporal variability, which is essential for pretraining. Both FISHER and RMIS are open-sourced.

title: A Staged Lean–Industry 4.0 Framework for Progressive Automation of Industrial Internal Transport
authors: Paula Lamo, Lucía Alonso Virgós, Luis Alfonso Menéndez-García, J. Javier Rainer Granados
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Industrial internal transport is a persistent source of non-value-added time in manufacturing, yet is rarely addressed as a dedicated scope in Lean–Industry 4.0 integration frameworks. This paper proposes a five-stage framework governing the transition from process stabilization to phased automation in internal transport environments, connecting Lean diagnostics, digital readiness assessment, multi-criteria prioritization, and governed deployment through explicit stage gates and ordinal readiness thresholds. The framework is applied retrospectively to operational data from a prior Lean intervention at a Spanish automotive SME. The intervention yielded a process cycle efficiency increase from 14.58% to 42.42 %, a takt-time reduction from 200 to 80 min per project, and elimination of overtime; retrospective analysis attributes these gains to the first three stages, while advanced automation candidates remain at the analytical planning stage. The principal limitations are the single-site scope, the retrospective validation design, and the absence of deployment data for the advanced automation stages.

title: Partitioned Orthotropic Equivalent Thermal Modeling and Validation for Distributed Hairpin Windings in Permanent Magnet Motors
authors: Weiwei Geng, Xiao Zhang, Lijiang Zhou, Yu Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Energy Conversion
abstract: Distributed hairpin windings in permanent magnet (PM) motors feature complex three-dimensional (3-D) structures, which bring great challenges to efficient thermal modeling and temperature prediction. High-fidelity thermal simulations of such windings suffer from excessive computational costs, hindering rapid thermal design optimization. To address this issue, a partitioned equivalent thermal modeling method is proposed for hairpin windings, with a focus on the structurally intricate end-winding regions. Based on heat transfer theory and orthotropic material properties, the equivalent orthotropic thermal conductivities of in-slot and end-winding regions are rigorously derived, and the tensor transformation of orthotropic thermal conductivity is employed to account for the non-orthogonal arrangement of end-windings. Comparative simulations of the detailed hairpin model and the proposed equivalent model demonstrate that the latter achieves high predictive accuracy while reducing computational resources by over 95%. Experimental tests on a distributed hairpin winding PM motor prototype further validate the accuracy of the detailed model and the reliability of the equivalent model (relative error < 5%).

title: Lightweight Food Localization and Recognition via Multi-Branch Feature Learning and Enhanced Aggregation
authors: Xiangyi Zhu, Yancun Yang, Pindan Cao, Guorui Sheng, Bruce Denby
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Food image localization and recognition on edge devices is a core task in food computing, enabling convenient dietary monitoring and efficient health management. However, food localization and recognition presents significant challenges due to inherent intra-class variability, inter-class similarity, and non-rigid characteristics. To address these challenges, we propose YOLO-Multi Feature Fusion, a novel multi-feature fusion model for food image localization and recognition. Building upon the YOLOv5 framework, YOLO-Multi Feature Fusion integrates several key components: the Ghost Bottleneck from the lightweight GhostNet, a newly designed Multi-Scale Feature Bottleneck, a Bidirectional Vision Transformer, and an Information Cross-Exchange module. These modules enable the model to comprehensively capture and fuse complex feature information from food images while simultaneously reducing both model parameters and computational load. Extensive evaluations on benchmark datasets (UEC Food100, UEC Food256, and ZSFooD) demonstrate that YOLO-Multi Feature Fusion outperforms existing lightweight detectors. Compared to YOLOv5, YOLO-Multi Feature Fusion achieves mAP improvements of 3.0%, 3.0%, and 0.3% on these datasets, respectively, with parameter reductions of 5.7M, 4.7M, and 4.9M, and computational load reductions of 44.6 GFLOPs, 42.0 GFLOPs, and 42.0 GFLOPs. The source code will be released upon the formal publication of the paper.

title: Cooperative Coverage Control for Multiple Stratospheric Airships in Emergency Communication Missions
authors: Weicheng Gong, Yifei Zhang, Tian Chen, Ming Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Emerging emergency communication scenarios demand robust, efficient, and fair wireless coverage in environments where terrestrial infrastructure is unavailable. Multiple stratospheric airship (MSA) systems offer significant advantages in coverage and deployment flexibility, but also pose substantial challenges for autonomous cooperative control under uncertainty. This paper addresses the multi-agent cooperative exploration and coverage control problem for MSA in disaster-area emergency communication missions. The problem is formulated as a constrained maximization problem, where the deployment and motion of the airships are coordinated to maximize exploration efficiency, communication quality, and service fairness. To this end, we propose a distributed cooperative control framework for MSA systems. A virtual guiding force (VGF) method is developed to unify the exploration and coverage phases, while a frontier-based cooperative exploration algorithm with auction-based task allocation enables efficient discovery of unknown regions. In addition, a fairness-aware communication control scheme (FCCS) is integrated to improve resource allocation among ground users. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing methods in exploration efficiency, coverage effectiveness, fairness, and computational cost, validating its potential for practical emergency communication applications.

title: Sequential Task Assignment and Resource Allocation in V2X-Enabled Mobile Edge Computing
authors: Yufei Ye, Shijian Gao, Xinhu Zheng, Liuqing Yang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Nowadays, the convergence of mobile edge computing (MEC) and vehicular networks has emerged as a vital enabler for the ever-increasing intelligent onboard applications. This paper proposes a multi-tier task offloading mechanism for MEC-enabled vehicular networks leveraging vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communications. The study focuses on applications with sequential subtasks and explores the collaboration of two tiers. In the Vehicle Tier, the requesting vehicle (RV)-service vehicle (SV) matching scheme and the inter-vehicle collaborative computation are studied, with joint optimization of task offloading decision, communication, and computing resource allocation to minimize energy consumption while satisfying delay requirements. In the Roadside Unit (RSU) Tier, collaboration among RSUs is investigated to further address multi-access issues of uplink subchannels and computing resources for serving unmatched RVs. To tackle this intricate problem, a layered optimization framework is first proposed to obtain task offloading decisions and optimal continuous resource allocation, after which a subchannel allocation scheme is designed to recover the discrete solution with low complexity. Extensive experiments are conducted to demonstrate that the proposed method reduces average energy consumption by at least 15% compared with recent utility maximization and energy cost minimization benchmarks under varying task delay requirements and vehicle scales.
