title: A Novel Constraint-Aware Scheduling Algorithm for Computing and Communication Satellite Constellation Resources
authors: Lili Zhao, Xueqian Wang, Yongge Ma, Jun Du, Gang Li, Yongpan Liu
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Computing and communication satellite (CCS) constellation resource scheduling is crucial for efficient onboard processing of remote sensing data. In this letter, we innovatively propose a constraint-aware scheduling (CAS) algorithm for fast resource scheduling of CCS constellation. CAS utilizes the perception of constraint violations in the elite solution at each iteration, specifically for connectivity and memory constraints, to heuristically clear and refill subtask assignments, thereby exploring the solution space efficiently. Experimental results show that the proposed CAS algorithm reduces the runtime of CCS constellation resource scheduling while maintaining the solution quality in comparison with existing state-of-the-art algorithms.

title: Modulating Oxygen Vacancy Concentration for High-Performance Undoped HfO₂ Ferroelectric Capacitors
authors: Xuanxi Liu, Yang Yang, Guanlin Li, Zhiquan He, Xiuyi Wang, Boping Wang, Shibo Wang, Xiangli Zhou, Pengfei Jiang, Tiancheng Gong, Wei Wei, Xiao Long, Yuan Wang, Qing Luo
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: Ferroelectric HfO₂ thin film has attracted considerable attention due to its ability to induce ferroelectricity without doping. However, its performance is still far inferior to that of doped HfO₂-based ferroelectric films, which limits its further development. In this study, the ferroelectric performance of TiN/HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/TiN devices was significantly enhanced by modulating the oxygen vacancy concentration in undoped HfO₂ films. TEM and EELS analysis demonstrate that the increase in oxygen vacancy concentration within the undoped HfO₂ film promotes the crystallization of the ferroelectric o-phase, accompanied by an increase in dielectric constant, leading to a substantial enhancement in device performance. Ultimately, the TiN/HfO₂/TiN ferroelectric capacitor achieved a high remanent polarization (2Pr ~ 40 μC/cm²). Under an electric field of 2.5 MV/cm, the device sustained over 4 × 10⁹ switching cycles before hard breakdown.

title: Asymptotic Observability of Boolean Networks With Markovian Data Loss
authors: Shuyu Tan, Chi Huang, Yong Wang, Jianquan Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: Interactions in practical networked systems are subject to temporally correlated data loss caused by stochastic disturbances. In contrast to conventional models that assume independent Bernoulli data loss, this article investigates the asymptotic observability of Boolean networks (BNs) under Markovian loss dynamics. Within a newly developed algebraic framework, the evolution of the data loss switching signal is modeled as a Markov chain, and a column similarity matrix is constructed to characterize the set of states that are distinguishable with probability one in finite time. By establishing an equivalence between the asymptotic reachability of the distinguishable state set and the asymptotic observability of the system, necessary and sufficient conditions for asymptotic observability are derived with reduced computational complexity. Practical numerical examples are provided to illustrate the proposed results.

title: Efficient Hyperspectral Multiclass Change Detection Based on Spectral-Spatial Harmonization
authors: Hongyu Zhao, Shuai Hao, Zhijie An, Yanqing Yao, Yue Wu, Maoguo Gong, Huchuan Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The rich spectral information in hyperspectral images (HSIs) provides a significant advantage for fine-grained, multiclass change detection (CD), yet a robust and practical framework remains elusive. Existing deep learning methods are often fragmented, failing to simultaneously address a triad of critical challenges: i) spectral inconsistency between HSI pairs acquired from different sensors or times; ii) inefficient static fusion of hierarchical spectral-spatial features, which struggles to model complex changes; and iii) severe label scarcity for multiclass supervision, which limits model training. To systematically solve these interconnected problems, we propose the Deep Differential Feature Fusion (D2F2) framework, a holistic, end-to-end architecture. D2F2 introduces two novel components: a Spectral Adaptive Sampling (SAS) module that adaptively remaps inputs with arbitrary spectral bands to a unified feature space, thereby alleviating spectral inconsistency caused by missing spectral channels and band-number mismatch. Concurrently, a Spatial Hierarchical Aggregation (SHA) module replaces static fusion with a dynamic, attention-based mechanism to generate highly discriminative, change-aware features. To address label scarcity, the entire framework is trained using an efficient semi-supervised strategy. This fully convolutional design also enables ultra-efficient map-to-map inference, which eliminates the massive computational redundancy of patch-based methods. Extensive experiments on three challenging HSI datasets demonstrate that D2F2 not only achieves new state-of-the-art accuracy but is also orders of magnitude more computationally efficient than competing deep learning models. The code will be available at: https://github.com/dmhz/HSCD.

title: Output Feedback Concurrent Learning Control for Fully Actuated System
authors: Jiaming Zhang, Yulin Duan, Changping Yu, Yang Liu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel concurrent-learning-based adaptive control scheme for second-order fully actuated systems with unknown parameters. Unlike conventional adaptive control approaches that depend on velocity measurements or state observers, the proposed method employs a systematic filtering structure to achieve velocity-independent parameter adaptation. A key innovation is the construction of history-stack matrices, which enable consistent parameter estimation without requiring persistent excitation in the instantaneous sense. Theoretical analysis establishes the global asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system under the interval-excitation condition. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness and performance of the proposed approach.

title: Guest Editorial Special Issue on Brazilian DTV+ (TV 3.0): Transforming Broadcasting for the Future
authors: Cristiano Akamine, Yiyan Wu, Liang Zhang, Sung-Ik Park, Débora C. Muchaluat-Saade, Marcelo F. Moreno, Jeongchang Kim, Sungjun Ahn, Jon Montalban, Rafael Sotelo, Mylene Farias, Guido Lemos, Yin Xu, Luiz Fausto
publish: IEEE Transactions on Broadcasting

title: Trajectory Planning for Fixed-Wing UAV Swarms Based on Exact Penalty Function
authors: Yangzhou Cao, Bin Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Multi-unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) formation cooperation is essential for complex missions where single-UAV systems fall short. To address the inherent challenges of free-final-time optimization and continuous-time state constraints in fixed-wing UAV swarm reconfiguration, this paper proposes a trajectory optimization framework based on an exact penalty function. First, a 3-degree-of-freedom (3-DOF) dynamic model is established, incorporating strict constraints for collision avoidance, communication connectivity, obstacle avoidance, and terminal formation geometries. By integrating control parameterization with time-scale transformation, the infinite-dimensional optimal control problem is transcribed into a finite-dimensional nonlinear programming (NLP) problem. This approach eliminates heuristic weight tuning and efficiently resolves free-final-time variables. Numerical simulations in symmetric and asymmetric obstacle environments demonstrate that the proposed method consistently generates smooth, safe, and time-optimal trajectories while maintaining formation stability, thereby advancing constrained optimal control theory for UAV swarms.

title: Toward Fully Driven Tactile Exploration: HDT-Net for Efficient Textile Material Recognition from Cyclic Sliding Sequences
authors: Yangqianhui Zhang, Dong Han, Minghao Wang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Tactile material recognition is an important capability for intelligent robots to perceive surface properties during active contact. In particular, cyclic sliding acquisition-in which a sensor is moved back and forth across a surface-provides rich sequential cues that resemble human touch behavior, but it also introduces challenging multivariate temporal patterns, repetitive motion cycles, and subtle inter-class differences among materials with similar surface properties. To address these issues, we propose HDT-Net, a lightweight fully convolutional architecture for textile material recognition from cyclic sliding tactile sequences. The model combines a dual-branch large-small kernel convolution with structural re-parameterization for efficient long-range temporal modeling, and decoupled transformations that explicitly separate intra-variable feature refinement from inter-variable interaction. To evaluate the proposed method, we construct the CSTT-14 dataset, containing 14 material categories with diverse surface characteristics collected under a repeated back-and-forth sliding protocol. Experimental results show that HDT-Net consistently outperforms representative baselines across different input lengths while maintaining the lowest computational cost among all compared models, demonstrating its suitability as a lightweight perception module for tactile material recognition systems.

title: Finite-Time Estimation for Autonomous Nonlinear Systems Against Sensor Attacks Based on Nonlinear Multi-Observer
authors: Cui-Hua Zhang, Xin-Rui Kong, Ying Zhang, Chang-Chun Hua
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper focuses on the finite-time secure state estimation for autonomous nonlinear systems with intense nonlinear coupling under malicious sensor attacksthese systems are core to new energy vehicles, mobile robots and industrial servo systems, whose sensor measurement links are highly attack-vulnerable and thus risk operational safety. Based on the systems' structural characteristics and local differential observability, a set of nonlinear multi-observers is designed for each observable sensor subset to underpin finite-time secure estimation, and a min-max secure estimation strategy is further proposed to search for uncompromised sensor combinations within a preset finite time window. Rigorous theoretical proof verifies that the method ensures exponential convergence of estimation errors (with arbitrarily adjustable convergence rates) even against potentially unbounded attacks. Compared with existing methods for general nonlinear systems, it shortens the search time for uncompromised sensors and achieves accurate secure state estimation within the expected time.

title: Fixed/Prescribed-Time Synchronization Control for Multi-Layer Complex Chaotic Networks
authors: Shixiang Sun, Xinjiang Wei, Lewei Dong
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates fixed-time (FT) and prescribed-time (PT) intra-layer synchronization for multi-layer chaotic networks. By comparison principle and network structure, a quantized FT/PT controllers that achieve synchronization within a fixed time or prescribed time is designed. Simulations on a two-layer network verify effectiveness.

title: CSI Feedback via Joint Source-Channel Coding and Modulation: A Comprehensive Study for 6G Realization
authors: Nan Jiang, Younsun Kim, Bowen Yang, Hang Qi, Yu Xiao, Yupeng Cui, Feifei Sun, Chen Qian, Bin Yu, Chengjun Sun, Hyoung-ju Ji, Min Jang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In modern wireless systems, the process of acquiring channel state information (CSI) has become increasingly critical yet challenging, driven by the conflicting needs of expanding antenna arrays and the pursuit of high spectral efficiency. Recent research particularly leveraging joint source-channel coding and modulation (JSCM) via autoencoder architectures, has demonstrated initial promise in significantly enhancing CSI feedback effectiveness. However, a substantial gap persists between algorithm research and practical realization, particularly concerning real-world validation and systematic design methodologies. This article introduces a comprehensive framework enabling JSCM-based CSI feedback, which encompasses a learning methodology robust against wireless channel imperfection and protocol designs tailored for seamless integration into practical cellular systems. A hardware proof-of-concept (PoC) platform is developed and deployed, where experimental results substantiate the proposed method's superiority in realistic wireless environments. Furthermore, by synergizing technical and protocol innovations, this article resolves three critical challenges impeding the deployment of JSCM-based CSI feedback in future 6G networks: variant resource allocation, low peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) waveforms design, and accurate channel quality indication (CQI) estimation. Equipped with these enabling techniques, the proposed JSCM-based CSI feedback solution is positioned for 6G adoption, demonstrating substantial throughput improvements over conventional separate source and channel coding (SSCC) implementations.

title: A Linear Optimal State-Feedback Control Strategy with Capacity-Independent State-of-Charge Balancing for Modular Second-Life Battery Systems
authors: Dibyendu Khan, Liyan Qu, Wei Qiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: This article proposes a linear quadratic regulator (LQR)-based double-loop optimal state-feedback control strategy with a capacity-independent active power sharing method for the optimal power management and state of charge (SoC) balance of a three-phase modular second-life battery (SLB) system. Given the charging/discharging power demand, the proposed power sharing method determines the active power reference for each SLB module to achieve SoC balance among the SLB modules within each phase of the system, without the need for the capacity information of the SLB modules that is commonly required in the existing SoC balancing methods. In the proposed LQR-based double-loop control strategy, the outer control loop minimizes the reference power tracking error of the modular SLB system, and the inner control loop regulates the active power distribution among the SLB modules using the proposed power sharing method while satisfying the power constraints of the SLB modules. The convergence of the SoC balancing and the stability of the proposed control strategy are proven via Lyapunov-based analysis. Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop test results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed control strategy for power reference tracking and its superiority over a state-of-the art model predictive control strategy and a proportional and integral-based self-adaptation control strategy for power constrained SoC and power balance among the SLB modules of a modular SLB system.

title: Rubbing Interaction with Two-Handed Virtual Reality Controllers
authors: Huawei Tu, BoYu Gao, Weiqiang Xin, Guanyu Ye, and Hai-Ning Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: As a common action in our daily lives, rubbing has been a viable technique for various devices, including touchscreens, digital pens, and skin surfaces. However, there has been little research on the design of rubbing in virtual reality (VR), particularly with VR controllers. This study explores the design space of rubbing with two-handed VR controllers. We started with a one-on-one interview with 10 participants to understand four components of rubbing with VR controllers: hand use, rubbing direction, device type and rubbing contact. In Experiment 2, we proposed a method to classify rubbing with three hand uses and six rubbing directions. Results showed that the overall classification accuracy reached 96%. Experiment 3 revealed that our method was able to successfully reject all seven tested non-rubbing actions. Results from Experiment 4 reflected user preference for using our technique to perform five typical VR tasks. Our study contributes to VR interaction design with controllers.

title: Characterizing Interbrain Synchrony in Student Dyads during Collaborative Problem Solving via Hyperscanning EEG
authors: Chun-Hsiang Chuang, Ting-Yu Hsu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Learning Technologies
abstract: Effective collaboration is essential for learning, especially when performing tasks that demand logical reasoning and problem-solving. Understanding how knowledge is co-constructed between individuals can enable valuable insights into the underlying cognitive processes. As teamwork increasingly shifts to online environments, there is a pressing need to investigate the dynamics of participant interaction beyond observable behaviors in depth. This study used a hyperscanning electroencephalography (EEG) approach, one of the most commonly used methods in social interaction research, to investigate interbrain synchrony (IBS) between dyads performing high school-level mathematics and computational thinking tasks of varying difficulty in a setting designed to simulate remote communication. IBS was predominantly observed in the frontal, temporal, and parietal regions and varied significantly across most frequency bands with respect to both task difficulty and completion time. Interestingly, an inverted U-shaped relationship emerged between IBS and task difficulty, with the highest synchrony observed during tasks of moderate complexity, suggesting that a balanced cognitive challenge may be optimal for promoting neural alignment. For the most demanding tasks, however, IBS was positively associated with longer completion times, implying that extended engagement with high-difficulty tasks may encourage sustained collaborative effort and neural coupling. These findings highlight IBS as a potential neural marker of effective joint problem-solving and shared cognitive engagement during collaborative tasks. This study has implications for educational design, team-based learning, and the development of neuroadaptive tools to enhance remote collaboration.

title: Front Cover
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Nanotechnology Magazine

title: A Semiconductor Photocathode with Electrostatically Tunable Electron Affinity Surface
authors: Anika Tabassum Priyoti, Rehan Kapadia
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Electrostatically tunable negative electron affinity (NEA) semiconductor photocathode can be a stable alternative to chemically activated NEA surfaces, which are highly sensitive to atmospheric exposure. Here we present a planar silicon-insulator-graphene heterostructure-based Hot-Electron Light-Assisted Cathode (HELAC) [1, 2], that enables reversible, air-stable NEA operation. Unlike conventional cathodes, HELAC offers independent electronic control over emission parameters, eliminating its dependence on surface chemistry. Together with its structural simplicity and scalability, these features establish HELAC as a transformative platform for the development of nextgeneration compact, optically modulated, and highfrequency electron source.

title: High Data Rate Communication Through the Atmosphere at Sub-Terahertz Frequencies for Future Low Earth Orbit Satellite Systems
authors: Frédéric André
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) telecom satellite systems ambition to become an alternative to ground fiber optics internet, thus requiring extremely high data rates to be transmitted from ground to satellite, and back. Presently laser communication at wavelength around $1.55 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ are foreseen. But strong atmospheric attenuation caused by clouds and fog is an issue without satisfactory solution yet. In this presentation we study the benefit of using sub-terahertz frequencies 50-220 GHz, for this purpose, and the available vacuum electronics technologies available for power amplification.

title: Intermediate Variable Estimator Based Fault-Tolerant Formation Control of Multi-Agent Hofa Systems
authors: Shiyu Han, Guangren Duan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper presents a fault-tolerant formation control framework for multi-agent high-order fully actuated (HOFA) systems by incorporating an intermediate variable estimator (IVE). The proposed IVE avoids the classical observer matching conditions and enables simultaneous estimation of the system states and actuator faults with provable convergence. By combining the IVE with the HOFA framework, an adaptive formation-tracking control protocol is developed such that nonlinear followers asymptotically converge to a prescribed formation with respect to the leaders within an arbitrarily small error bound. The controller parameters are determined according to the HOFA methodology, which facilitates the construction of an ideal closedloop linear system. Numerical simulations are provided to verify the effectiveness of the proposed method under different fault scenarios.

title: Connectivity-Preserving and Collision-Free Prescribed-Time Consensus Tracking for Second-Order Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Zhenyu Liu, Yafeng Li, Hao Li, Shuaigang Feng
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of connectivity-preserving and collision-free control for a class of second-order multi-agent systems (MAS) under switched prescribed-time sliding mode control. Firstly, an error transformation function is introduced to transform the connectivity-preservation and collision-avoidance requirements among agents into an errorstabilization problem. Then, a switched prescribed-time sliding mode surface is designed, to guarantee that the tracking errors and sliding mode variables converge to bounded compact sets in prescribed time based on the radial basis function neural networks and the correspondingly designed prescribed time control law. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified by the Lyapunov stability theory and numerical simulations.

title: Mitigating Multimodal Hallucination through Effective and Perception-aware Granularity Alignment_supp1-3703183.pdf
authors: Cangqi Zhou
publish: N/A
abstract: Hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have garnered great attention in recent studies. In our research, we observe a significant granularity mismatch between the data utilized across different training stages of LVLMs. However, the impact of this granularity mismatch on model performance remains underexplored. Such incongruence may lead to inconsistencies between the mapped coarse-grained visual information and the introduced fine-grained textual information. Trained on these misaligned data, the model will fabricate hallucinatory content through ungrounded extrapolation. In this paper, we systematically investigate how data granularity differences across different training stages affect model behavior, through defining a granularity metric and varying it in these stages. Our analysis confirms the detrimental impact of granularity discrepancies on model performance, underscoring the importance of identifying an appropriate alignment granularity and the challenge of achieving effective alignment across training stages. Based on these findings, we propose EPAlign, an Effective and Perception-aware granularity Alignment framework that aligns different stages to an appropriate granularity level to mitigate hallucinations. Extensive experiments show that EPAlign significantly reduces hallucinations in LVLMs.

title: Finite-Time Control for the Speed and Tension System of the Reversible Cold Strip Rolling Mill Based on Fully-Actuated System Approach
authors: Le Liu, Zhanming Liu, Nuan Shao, Yiming Fang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To address the high-performance control challenges arising from the multivariable, strongly coupled, and nonlinear dynamics of the speed and tension systems in reversible cold strip rolling mills, this paper proposes a finite-time decoupling control method based on a high-order fully-actuated (HOFA) model. By converting the physical mechanistic model into a HOFA form, the inherent “differential explosion” issue of traditional backstepping methods is fundamentally avoided, while system nonlinearities and coupling terms are explicitly separated, establishing a clear framework for structured control design. Parameterized finite-time controllers are then designed through appropriate linear feedback gains and homogeneous power nonlinearities, ensuring practical finite-time stability of the closed-loop system and achieving complete dynamic decoupling between speed and tension channels. This overcomes the limitations of conventional disturbance-compensation-based passive decoupling approaches. Theoretical analysis rigorously proves the finite-time convergence and provides an explicit upper bound for convergence time. Simulations with actual mill parameters demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms non-fully-actuated finite-time strategies in convergence speed and control accuracy.

title: Suppression of Dual-Frequency Microwave Dielectric Multipactor by External Magnetic Field
authors: Xinyi Huang, Huihui Wang, Dagang Liu, Laqun Liu, Zhijie Li
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This paper presents a method for suppressing the multipactor discharge on a dielectric window with a dualfrequency high-power microwave: An external magnetic field B is applied to the vacuum side of the dielectric window, parallel to the dielectric window and simultaneously perpendicular to the deposition electric field $E_{d c}$ and the high-power microwave electric field $E_{r f}$, the gyrofrequency frequency is the average of the frequencies of the two components of the dual-frequency high-power microwave. The results of theoretical and numerical analysis show that this method can significantly reduce the upper and lower boundaries of the susceptibility diagram, and electrons can obtain higher energy through cyclotron resonance, making it easier for the energy to exceed the second crossover energy in secondary electron yield, which can stably suppress multipactor discharge with a dual-frequency highpower microwave.

title: MTS-Pose: A Lightweight Stereo Multi-Task Framework for Underwater 6D Pose Estimation
authors: Shenghui Li, Xin Wang, Rui Wang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Accurate 6D pose estimation is widely required for autonomous underwater robotic manipulation. However, reliable depth perception is often compromised in underwater environments. Furthermore, real-time multi-target perception is severely restricted by the computational constraints of subsea edge devices. To bridge these gaps, we propose a lightweight Multi-Task Stereo framework for Pose estimation (MTS-Pose). In this framework, segmentation, recognition, and pose estimation are jointly performed by a single shared backbone. To resolve scale ambiguity in passive vision, stereo features are fused under geometric constraints by a novel Epipolar Cross-Attention module (ECAM). Additionally, gradient conflicts between 2D perception tasks and 3D pose estimation are alleviated by a multi-stage feature recalibration strategy. The proposed MTS-Pose is evaluated on a synthetic underwater dataset. Stable multi-task perception performance is demonstrated by the results. Real-time inference at 28.8 FPS and centimeter-level translation accuracy are achieved. Ultimately, a practical solution for coarse pose estimation is provided for underwater industrial manipulation tasks.

title: THALES TH1511 105 GHz 1 MW CW Industrial Gyrotron: A New ECRH Power Source for the WEST Fusion Reactor
authors: Alberto Leggieri, Konstantinos A. Avramidis, Francis Bouquey, Ioannis Chelis, Lena Delpech, Benjamin Ell, Lukas Feuerstein, Gerd Gantenbein, Stefan Illy, John Jelonnek, Jianbo Jin, François Legrand, Christophe Lievin, Gérald Lietaer, Rodolphe Marchesin, Moritz Misko, Ijaze M. Oumar, David Quetel, Tomasz Rzesnicki, Sebastian Stanculovic, Etienne Vallée, Manfred Thumm
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This paper reports the design, manufacturing, and testing of the THALES TH1511 $105 \mathrm{GHz}, 1 \mathrm{MW} \mathrm{CW}$ gyrotron for WEST. The prototype was tested in short pulses at KIT, demonstrating 1.2 MW power and $46 \%$ efficiency capabilities with readiness for long pulse operation.

title: Global Prescribed-Time Prescribed Performance Control for Nonlinear Interconnected Systems: A High-Order Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Zhijie Li, Zelong Zhang, Jian Zhang, Jiaqiang Chen
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Based on the higher-order fully actuated system approach, this paper solves the prescribed-time prescribed performance control problem for nonlinear interconnected systems. By integrating a parameterized Lyapunov function and a prescribed-time regulation function, we propose a prescribed-time control strategy for nonlinear interconnected systems, which enables the system output to converge to zero within the preset time. Moreover, based on a novel prescribed performance function with an infinite initial value, we propose a relaxed-condition prescribed performance control strategy. This strategy enhances the system's transient and steady-state performance while eliminating the influence of initial conditions inherent in conventional prescribed performance methods. Finally, asimulation example is given to substantiate the effectiveness ofproposed algorithm.

title: Standardized FMU Modeling Framework for Traveling Wave Tube Simulation
authors: Honghui Su, Haiying Yuan, Zheng Tan, Shilong Zhu, Wanli Shi, Luanfeng Gao, Yulu Hu, Bin Li
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Traveling wave tube (TWT) simulation tools commonly use proprietary formats, making cross-tool co-simulation difficult. To address this issue, this paper designs a standardized encapsulation method for TWT solvers based on the Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI) 2.0 Co-Simulation standard. A layered architecture separates FMI interface implementation from solver logic, and an automated packaging workflow is designed. Using a beam-wave interaction solver as an example, verification shows that simulation results are consistent with the original solver. This method provides a feasible solution for vacuum electronic device simulation standardization.

title: Cart Pendulum Control: a Fas Approach
authors: Guang-Ren Duan, Wei-Jie Ren, Hao-Wen Liu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper presents a switching control strategy for the cart-pendulum system subject to symmetric constraints on the pendulum angle. Based on the fully actuated system (FAS) approach, the proposed framework addresses the underactuated dynamics and input coupling through a three-stage design. First, a sub-FAS controller is developed for the pendulum subsystem to ensure stabilization for large-angle deviations and high-velocity conditions. By analyzing the system dynamics in reverse time, we analytically characterize the region of exponential attraction (RoEA) boundaries for underdamped, critically damped, and overdamped damping regimes, providing an explicit description of the RoEA. Second, to achieve stabilization of both the cart position and pendulum angle near the upright equilibrium, a local linearized FAS model is derived. Through a state transformation involving the cart and pendulum coordinates, the dynamics are converted into a fourth-order linear fully actuated form, enabling exponential stabilization via parametric design. Furthermore, a smooth switching mechanism based on statedependent sigmoid functions is introduced to seamlessly blend the sub-FAS controller with the local linear controller, mitigating control discontinuities. Numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in achieving stabilization while strictly satisfying state constraints.

title: Precision Force Control for a Flexible-Base Space Manipulator in Asteroid Surface Excavation Under Unknown Contact Forces
authors: Shiyu Li, Xianglong Kong, Ning Zhang, Jianqiao Zhang, Maoguo Li, Zichun Ma, Ziyi Xu, Shengxin Sun, Jianrong Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: The weak gravity and unknown mechanical characteristics of the shallow asteroid regolith pose significant challenges for sampling missions. To address these issues, enable compliant excavation, and suppress the compound disturbances induced by unknown end-effector contact forces and flexible solar arrays, a cooperative control framework for a hovering sampling robot is proposed in this paper. First, a dynamic model of the rigid-flexible coupled system is established. Second, a cooperative control method is developed to handle the dynamic interactions. This method integrates a nonlinear active disturbance rejection control strategy to suppress compound disturbances caused by manipulator reaction forces and flexible structural vibrations, alongside an adaptive impedance controller based on a radial basis function neural network to achieve compliant excavation. Finally, a virtual simulation prototype is constructed to validate the proposed framework. The simulation results demonstrate that the architecture effectively suppresses complex compound disturbances. Throughout the entire sampling process, the manipulator consistently maintains stable and disturbance-tolerant joint trajectory tracking with errors bounded within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.3$</tex> rad. Concurrently, the hovering base achieves stable hovering with a steady-state position error of approximately 0.025 m and an equivalent attitude rotation error of less than 0.127 rad. This research lays a solid foundation for the safe and autonomous execution of future asteroid sampling missions.

title: Study of a High Efficiency 80W Q-Band Space TWT
authors: Shuli Zhao, Yi Zhang, Xiao Liang, Jingxin Xia, Sihan Yin, Wenjie Yu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: This paper presents the development of a Q band continuous wave (CW) space traveling wave tube (TWT) for communication. This tube was designed using multi-stage depressed collectors (MDC), helix slow-wave structure (SWS) and phase velocity tapering circuit. The test results show that the prototype is capable of providing output power up to 84 W, gain of $53 d B$ and overall efficiency of 55.8%, at $37.5-42.5 \mathrm{GHz}$. It is also found within the 5 GHz bandwidth, the electronic efficiency is over 19\%, while small-signal gain flatness is below 1.3 dB.

title: Tunable Predefined-Time Attitude Stabilization for Spacecraft Based on Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Yu-Yao Wu, Zhichao Sha, Xueying Wang, Xin Long, Jiming Liu, Yaoyuan Zeng
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: The attitude stabilization of spacecraft is investigated in this paper using a fully actuated system approach, with consideration given to initial parameter uncertainty, external disturbances, and actuator faults. A predefined-time extended state observer is introduced based on the fully actuated system approach to estimate the lumped disturbance in spacecraft system. In the following, a tunable predefined-time control scheme is proposed to guarantee practically tunable predefined-time stability of the resultant closed-loop system. Simulation results show the effectiveness of the proposed controller.

title: Installation and Commissioning of the Gyrotron for the ST40 Tokamak
authors: Emma Wooldridge, Colin Abraham, Aleksanda Alieva, Trevor Blackman, Bhavya Kenia, Nicolas Lopez, Arthur Magill, Ruth Peacock, Vladimir Shevchenko
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Tokamak Energy has procured a gyrotron as part of an electron cyclotron resonance heating (ECRH) and current drive (CD) system for the ST40 spherical tokamak. It is a multi-frequency system operating at 104 GHz or 137 GHz employing a gyrotron purchased from Kyoto Fusioneering capable of generating 1 MW for 2 s. First oscillation of the gyrotron was achieved at the end of 2025 with commissioning of the gyrotron due to complete in early 2026 and full integrated commissioning on ST40 in the second half of the year. The present status of the project will be discussed along with the next steps for commissioning and operation on ST40. In addition to the gyrotron commissioning, this project includes the waveguide routing and installation, and the ECR launcher - designed to enable the demonstration of conventional ECR heating and current drive during the first phases of operation before moving on to scenarios for future operations. It also includes the design and commission of the control system for both the gyrotron and launcher.

title: Optimal Parameter Selection of Linear Graph Filter for Denoising Based on Distributed Gradient Descent
authors: Dan Xiong
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Signal denoising is one of the most fundamental and important technologies in the fields of science, engineering, and medicine. Thanks to the flourishing development of graph signal processing theory, graph Laplacian regularization has been widely applied in signal denoising. However, the denoising performance depends on the selection of regularization parameter. Parameter tuning is a common and critical challenge in machine learning and deep learning. This paper proposes a parameter selection method based on distributed optimization for a novel class of low-pass graph filters designed for denoising. By establishing a mean squared error (MSE) minimization criterion, the parameter optimization is transformed into a problem solvable in a distributed manner across graph nodes. This method fully leverages graph structural information, enabling data-driven parameter tuning and supporting large-scale graph signal processing. The simulation experiment results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: A X-band Compact High-power Sheet Beam Extended Interaction Klystron
authors: Wei Yuan, Jianxun Wang, Yixin Wan, Xinjie Li, Guo Liu, Yong Luo
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: To meet the requirements for compact X-band high-power microwave sources, this paper proposes a 3-cavity Sheet Beam Extended Interaction Klystron (SBEIK) operating at 9.3 GHz. Following 1D optimization of the cavity positions, a low external quality factor $\left(\mathrm{Q}_{\text {ext }}\right)$ output cavity was implemented for 3D particle-in-cell (PIC) simulation. Results indicate that for a highfrequency circuit with a length of 135 mm, operating at 80 kV and 200 A yields an output power of 5 MW and an efficiency of $31.25 \%$. These results demonstrate the potential of the SBEIK for the miniaturization of high-power microwave devices.

title: A Soft-Charging 17-Level High Voltage-Gain SC-based Inverter with Reduced Switch Count and Low TSV
authors: Majid Hosseinpour, Hossein Mansourizadeh, Ali Seifi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a 17-level switched-capacitor-based (SC-based) multilevel inverter featuring an eightfold voltage gain. The proposed topology consists of 10 power switches, 5 diodes, and 5 capacitors. The reduced number of switches significantly decreases the required gate drivers and control complexity, while also minimizing the number of heat sinks. In this structure, only five independent control signals are sufficient to drive five pairs of switches. The topology is designed so that, by employing an appropriate charging sequence and a small inductor in the common charging path of the capacitors, the inrush current is effectively limited and the capacitors are charged under soft-charging conditions. This is accomplished without the need for inductor bypass diodes, multiple inductors, or auxiliary external circuits. These features not only reduce voltage and current stresses on components but also enhance their lifetimes and decrease overall system cost. The proposed inverter does not require pre-charged capacitors, and voltage balancing among the capacitors is achieved automatically and rapidly. Moreover, the total standing voltage (TSV) of the proposed topology is limited to 5.8V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</sub>, indicating a low-voltage rating requirement for the switches and diodes. Notably, only two components in the entire structure are subjected to the maximum output voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</sub>). The circuit's operating principle, capacitor sizing procedure, soft-charging analysis, and power loss calculations are thoroughly discussed. In addition, a comprehensive comparison with existing topologies is provided for voltage gain, component count, voltage stress, and inrush current. The results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed structure in reducing losses, improving power density, and simplifying control. To validate the feasibility of the proposed inverter, a 530 W laboratory prototype is implemented, and both steady-state and dynamic experimental tests are conducted, confirming correct operation under various load conditions and modulation indices.

title: A Low Input Current Ripple Symmetric Bipolar Output Converter for Bipolar DC Distribution Systems
authors: Zhongbao Lin, Desheng Rong, Xuanjin Sun, Yadong Han, Changxuan Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: To address the challenges of bus voltage asymmetry due to load imbalance and high input current ripple in bipolar DC distribution systems, this paper proposes a low input current ripple converter with symmetrical bipolar output. The converter is formed by combining full bridge and three level structures with coupled inductors, flying capacitors, and an auxiliary inductor. The proposed converter achieves bipolar output voltage symmetry without extra balancing control, while all switches realize zero voltage switching (ZVS) over a wide range. Even under unbalanced loads, the efficiency does not degrade significantly owing to the modulation strategy designed to minimize conduction losses. The operation principle, power transmission characteristics, ZVS conditions, and magnetic integration design method are elaborated in detail. Finally, a 1.6 kW experimental prototype was built to validate the effectiveness of the proposed converter.

title: Investigating the Impact of Quantization Methods on the Safety and Reliability of Large Language Models
authors: Artem Kharinaev, Viktor Moskvoretskii, Egor Shvetsov, Kseniia Studenikina, Mikhail Bykov, Evgeny Burnaev
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are powerful tools for modern applications, but their computational demands limit accessibility. Quantization offers efficiency gains, yet its impact on safety and trustworthiness remains poorly understood. To address this, we introduce OpenSafetyMini, a human-curated safety dataset with 1,067 challenging questions to rigorously evaluate model behavior.We publicly release human safety evaluations for four LLMs (both quantized and full-precision), totaling 4,268 annotated question-answer pairs<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">a</sup>. By assessing 66 quantized variants of these models using four post-training quantization (PTQ) and two quantization-aware training (QAT) methods across four safety benchmarks including human centric evaluations, we uncover critical safety performance trade offs. Our results show both PTQ and QAT can degrade safety alignment, with QAT techniques like QLORA or STE performing less safely. No single method consistently outperforms others across benchmarks, precision settings, or models, highlighting the need for safety-aware compression strategies. Furthermore, precision-specialized methods (e.g., QUIK/AWQ for 4-bit, AQLM/QUIP# for 2-bit) excel at their target precision, meaning that these methods are not superior compression methods but rather distinct approaches.

title: Post-hoc Wasserstein Certificates for Autoencoder Reconstructions
authors: Joohong Rheey, Hyunggon Park
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Assessment of autoencoders is frequently constrained to average empirical losses, which lack principled and distributional guarantees. Existing methods incorporating optimal transport are training-centric and fail to provide post-hoc certificates for a trained model. In this paper, we derive a high-probability, data-dependent upper bound on the Wasserstein-1 distance between the input and reconstruction distributions. The bound is computed directly from the empirical mean absolute error on a hold-out set, requiring neither retraining nor the use of an optimal transport solver. We provide an explicit finite-sample deviation radius scaling. Moreover, we introduce an analytic attribution framework based on a joint-Gaussian surrogate, which provides interpretable insight into the structure of the reconstruction error. This framework decomposes the expected reconstruction loss into bias, variance, and alignment components in closed form. This enables an auditable procedure for both certifying and diagnosing autoencoder reconstructions.

title: An Overview of Low-Rank Structures in the Training and Adaptation of Large Models: From Implicit Low-Dimensionality to Efficient Training and Fine-Tuning [Special Issue on the Mathematics of Deep Learning]_supp1-3666749.pdf
authors: Laura Balzano
publish: N/A
abstract: The advent of deep learning has immeasurably changed the ways we process data in signal processing and machine learning. However, training and deploying modern deep learning models demand substantial computational resources, raising concerns about exorbitant training costs, GPU shortages, and heightened energy consumption. Several lines of research over the last decade have explored the emergence of low-dimensional structures during the training process, where basic elements such as weight matrices and representations tend to be approximately low rank even though not explicitly trained to be. These low-dimensional structures arise in part due to the implicit bias of the methods used to train deep networks, providing the potential to partially explain why deep models need fewer samples than the number of model parameters. This implicit low dimensionality has inspired the exploration of low-rank structures in training and fine-tuning large-scale deep learning models more efficiently. In this article, we review recent exciting advances in using low-rank structure in deep learning and aim to clarify the mathematical foundations underlying their design. Specifically, we highlight key insights from a rich line of research focused on theoretically understanding and leveraging low-rank structures in deep learning both at every iteration of training as well as at the global minimum.

title: Visual Explanations for AI-Based Graphic Design Feedback: Impact of XAI on Professional Designers’ Task Performance and Perceptions
authors: Shoko Sawada, Tomoyuki Suzuki, Nami Ogawa, Kota Yamaguchi, Masashi Toyoda
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: AI-powered tools have transformed creative workflows, including graphic design assessment, where designers receive AI-generated scores to refine their work. In commercial design, these scores often reflect expected business outcomes rather than purely aesthetic quality. However, the black-box nature of AI-generated feedback often leaves designers uncertain about how to improve their work, leading to inefficient iterations and reduced satisfaction. To address this challenge, we explore how eXplainable AI (XAI) can make score-based feedback more interpretable and actionable. We developed two complementary forms of XAI feedback: highlight-based feedback, which identifies design elements contributing to the score, and adjustment feedback, which presents design variants to visualize how small changes in specific properties (e.g., size, position, or color) affect scores.We conducted a user study with 18 professional designersworking on a commercial advertisement design task, comparing three systems: 1) a baseline score-only system, 2) a prototype system with highlight-based feedback, and 3) a combined system integrating both feedback types. Results showthat both XAI-enhanced systems significantly improved designers’ perceived usefulness, explainability, actionability, and creativity, while task performance metrics (completion time, iteration count, and design diversity) remained comparable to the baseline. Only the combined system significantly improved overall satisfaction, as designers adopted an effective two-phase workflow balancing exploration and score-driven refinement. These findings demonstrate that integrating complementary XAI feedback can support informed and satisfying creative decision-making in commercial design workflows.

title: Finite-Horizon H ∞ Consensus Control for IoT-Enabled Multi-Agent Systems With Stochastic Scheduling and Relay-Assisted Communications
authors: Jie Ban, Liangdong Guo, Zidong Wang, Lei Zou
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) increasingly relies on large-scale networked agents that cooperate over bandwidth-limited and unreliable wireless links. This paper studies the finite-horizon H∞ consensus control problem for a class of discrete-time IoT-enabled multi-agent systems subject to random parameters, stochastic communication scheduling, and relay-assisted transmissions. To reflect practical IoT communication constraints, a novel transmission framework is considered that integrates a stochastic communication protocol with a decode-and-forward relay mechanism under random packet losses. Within this framework, only a subset of sensing information is scheduled for transmission, encoded, decoded, and forwarded by relay nodes to improve communication reliability and coverage. Based on the resulting networked system model, a distributed consensus controller is designed to attenuate the effects of disturbances and communication uncertainties over a finite time horizon. Sufficient conditions guaranteeing finite-horizon H∞ consensus are derived in terms of recursive linear matrix inequalities, and a corresponding controller synthesis algorithm is developed. Simulation results illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in achieving robust consensus for IoT-enabled multi-agent systems operating over stochastic relay-assisted communication networks.

title: RISimg: Wi-Fi Imaging based on Spatiotemporal Coding of Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces
authors: Ruinan Li, Miao Li, Jiakang Su, Wenjin Yu, Dixiang Yang, Zijun Wang, Xiaolong Zheng, Qiang Cheng, Liang Liu, Huadong Ma
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Wi-Fi computational imaging has emerged as a promising paradigm for non-intrusive sensing; however, its practical deployment is severely hindered by dense physical multipath clutter, hardware phase quantization errors, and the limited bandwidth of commercial Wi-Fi. Traditional beamforming-based algorithms often completely lose target focus in complex environments, resulting in severe ghosting artifacts. To overcome these fundamental limitations, we propose RISimg, a novel robust Wi-Fi imaging framework empowered by the spatiotemporal coding of Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface. We design a differential coding strategy to guarantee a well-conditioned and noise-robust sensing matrix, and propose a multi-frequency sparse reconstruction algorithm based on the Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator. By constructing a large-scale overdetermined system, this physics-driven approach effectively suppresses multipath interference and hardware errors, successfully recovering the basic morphological outlines of complex targets. To further enhance the imaging performance, we propose leveraging a Conditional Diffusion Model to refine the imaging results. By utilizing the reconstruction as a structural prior, a carefully designed Conditional U-Net progressively refines the image through a generative reverse sampling process, restoring high-fidelity continuous boundaries. Extensive evaluations using a hardware prototype built with commercial Wi-Fi devices and a low-cost metasurface demonstrate that the final cascaded CDM achieves an unprecedented Structural Similarity Index Measure of 0.9192 and a Spatial Correlation Coefficient of 0.7336, paving a robust new avenue for Wi-Fi imaging.

title: Distributed EMPC for Coupled Nonlinear Systems with Input Constraints and Disturbances
authors: Miaomiao Ma, Jing Cui, Ruoxin Hao, Hong Chen
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This paper presents a distributed economic model predictive control (DEMPC) approach for nonlinear subsystems that are dynamically coupled and subject to input constraints and bounded disturbances. In the proposed strategy, each subsystem computes its control action independently via local optimization problem, based on its nominal decoupled dynamics. To enhance robustness against disturbances and interconnections, the robustness constraint is involved in each local optimization problem. Given the non-convexity and lack of positive definiteness in economic stage costs, an auxiliary positive-definite function is introduced to bypass the strict dissipativity assumptionła condition notoriously difficult to verify for nonlinear coupled systems. This function is utilized to construct a tunable stability constraint within the local optimization problem, thereby guaranteeing closed-loop convergence without requiring reliance on system dissipativity. The recursive feasibility of the resulting control strategy and the stability of the closed-loop system are rigorously analyzed. Finally, the application of the proposed DEMPC scheme to the three-cart mass-spring-damper (MSD) system illustrates the possible improvement in control and economic performance while reducing the computational burden.

title: A Self-Adaptive Physics-Informed Gated Recurrent Unit Neural Networks Model for Estimating the Lifetime of Li-ion Batteries
authors: Mohammad AlShaikh Saleh, Alamera Nouran Alquennah, Ali Ghrayeb, Shady S. Refaat, Haitham Abu-Rub, Sunil P. Khatri, Sertac Bayhan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks have recently emerged as a promising approach for applying deep neural networks to solve partial differential equations. However, accurately addressing challenging regions in the solutions of stiff partial differential equations necessitates adaptive methods. Additionally, the inherent limitations of baseline Physics-Informed Neural Networks in handling sequential or time-series data significantly constrain their applicability. In light of this, this paper introduces a Self-Adaptive Physics-Informed Attention-based Gated Recurrent Unit model, which enhances the baseline Physics-Informed Neural Networks framework to address these critical issues. The proposed model represents a significant advancement in Physics-Informed Neural Networks by integrating an attention-based Gated Recurrent Unit layer, thereby enabling more effective modeling of sequential data than existing physics-informed models. Moreover, this paper jointly minimizes the prediction error between the estimated and measured outputs (data-fit loss) and the residual associated with the governing degradation physics (physics-residual loss), while learning adaptive weights to balance both objectives during training. The efficacy of the proposed model is demonstrated through an essential case study, which is to predict the state of health of lithium-ion batteries, utilizing four battery datasets from different sources, namely the NASA, MIT, XJTU, and HUST battery datasets. The proposed model is compared with the baseline Physics-Informed Neural Networks and other benchmark models to demonstrate improvements in predictive accuracy and network initialization capabilities.

title: Design of an Optically Transparent OAM Digital Coding Metasurface with Low RCS
authors: Zhihao Li, Sijia Li, Yuhao Wu, Liqiu Hu, Zhe Cheng
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: In this letter, an asymmetrically transmissive, optically transparent and multi-bit digital coding metasurface with low radar cross section (RCS) has been proposed based on the Pancharatnam -Berry (PB) principle and transmission phase theory. The metasurface provides high transmittance and a phase gradient from 17.0 to 20.5 GHz. Its 3-bit unit cells are divided into four groups and realize cross-polarization transmittance over 0.7 with phase difference of 90° between groups. In the band of 22.0-27.0 GHz, the unit cells are reorganized into two groups, which reflectance of co-polarization above 0.95 and the phase difference is 180° between them. Through a specific coding sequence based on these principles, the metasurface with the transmittance of 48.42% can transmit x-polarized waves into vortex beams with orbital angular momentum (OAM) in 17.0-20.5 GHz and reduce RCS of oppositely y-polarized incidences in 22.0-27.0 GHz. The metasurface holds significant application in transparent scenarios such as glass curtain walls, aircraft canopies, and windows in space station.

title: Edge Truncation Effect Suppression in Omnidirectional Endfire-Scanning Phased Arrays Based on Reconfigurable Technique
authors: Jing-Cheng Liang, Baiqi Liu, Ren Wang, Cong Luo, Siyuan Gao, Bing-Zhong Wang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: A reconfigurable edge-element technique is proposed to suppress the edge truncation effect in omnidirectional endfire-scanning phased arrays. By restoring the impedance matching of edge elements, the proposed method enables a compact and scalable planar array architecture. A 7-element two-layer circular array prototype is fabricated and measured, achieving an 18.8% impedance bandwidth (480–580 MHz) with all ports maintaining an active voltage standing wave ratio (VSWR) below 3. In addition, an approximately 1.5 dB gain improvement is obtained at lower frequencies. The measured results agree well with simulations, validating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Learning 3D Scene Reconstruction From Nighttime Driving Videos
authors: Andrea Ramazzina, Stefano Gasperini, Mario Bijelic, Felix Heide, Federico Tombari
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Neural Radiance Fields and Gaussian Splatting have emerged as powerful representations for reconstructing complex dynamic urban scenes from multi-view driving logs. By producing photorealistic and geometrically consistent renderings, these methods offer a foundation for closed-loop simulation as well as scalable data augmentation engine, enabling the synthesis of diverse viewpoints and conditions without the need for costly additional data collection in the real world. However, existing approaches are almost exclusively tailored to well-lit, daytime environments and struggle with the challenges of nighttime settings, where noise, strong flares, and moving light sources dominate the visual signal. To address these limitations, we propose NightSplat, a Gaussian Splatting framework specifically designed for high-fidelity reconstruction and novel view synthesis of nighttime driving scenes. Through dedicated components, our method explicitly models sensor noise, lens flare effects, and introduces a lightweight module to represent dynamic vehicle lights. Extensive evaluations on nuScenes and Waymo Open Dataset demonstrate that NightSplat significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art baselines, both quantitatively and qualitatively, thereby extending simulation and testing to nighttime scenes. Project page: night-splat.github.io

title: Bayesian-Based Co-Optimization of Spatial and Energy-Aware Clustering in Multi-Level HWSN
authors: Meenal Rajput, Ram Narayan Yadav
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The durability and stability of heterogeneous wireless sensor networks (HWSNs) are severely limited by spatial energy imbalance and static protocol parameter tuning, especially when event-driven traffic is present. Existing clustering approaches typically optimize either spatial partitioning or protocol parameters independently, leading to suboptimal energy utilization. This paper proposes B2ZOF (Bayesian Two-Stage Zone Optimization Framework), a unified co-optimization approach that uses Bayesian optimisation to jointly optimise protocol control parameters and spatial zone geometry.Initially, a hybrid design combining square and circular shapes is optimized through multiple iterations to achieve a balanced spatial energy usage. Subsequently, the protocol parameters that dictate the selection of cluster heads, the scheduling of sleep and awake cycles, and relay-assisted routing are fine-tuned using a Gaussian Process surrogate with an Expected Improvement acquisition function. The proposed framework incorporates multi-level energy diversity and event-driven communication to adjust to changing sensing conditions. Simulation results indicate that the network’s stability period is extended by two to three times compared to baseline protocols, demonstrating improvements in stability duration and enhanced energy lifespan. Ablation analysis further reveals that event-driven communication and optimized spatial partitioning are the primary contributors to performance gains.These findings validate that joint spatial protocol co-optimization offers a scalable and efficient solution for dependable and energy-efficient operation in heterogeneous WSN deployments.

title: Machine Learning models for automatic classification of programming tasks in Python
authors: Ginna Viviana Leytón-Yela, Hector Mora, Jesus Insuasti, John Barco-Jimenez
publish: IEEE Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
abstract: This article presents a comparative analysis of automatic error classification in Python programming tasks involving loops, with a focus on machine learning approaches for identifying error types. Three experiments were conducted: the first evaluated traditional machine learning algorithms; the second explored alternative kernel functions in SVM and MLP algorithms; and the third involved two multi-input deep learning models. Following the CRISP-DM methodology, we compiled a dataset of 3000 looped programming tasks, each including a problem description in English, a Python solution, and a classification based on state errors formulated by Gries theory (initial S, final E, state transformation T, and their combinations, totaling seven labels). In preprocessing, the code was partitioned using its AST to align it with Gries states, and both the problem descriptions and code snippets were embedded with Bert, Code-bert, and Graph-code-bert. Ablations were performed to approximate the optimal solutions for each algorithm as closely as possible. The results showed that traditional machine learning algorithms achieved up to 90% accuracy and an MCC of 83%, while alternative kernels marginally improved performance but did not surpass that of traditional algorithms. Deep learning models (ANN-GCB) achieved the best balance, with 94% accuracy and 91% MCC, respectively, demonstrating their superiority for this classification task. The model was validated with external sources from different repositories, yielding an 85% success rate, and with a group of 138 students, achieving a 96.7% correct or partially correct classification rate.

title: DexTac: Learning Contact-aware Visuotactile Policies via Hand-by-hand Teaching
authors: Xingyu Zhang, Chaofan Zhang, Boyue Zhang, Zhinan Peng, Shaowei Cui, Shuo Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: For contact-intensive tasks, the ability to generate policies that produce comprehensive tactile-aware motions is essential. However, existing data collection and skill learning systems for dexterous manipulation often suffer from low-dimensional tactile information. To address this limitation, we propose DexTac, a visuo-tactile manipulation learning framework based on kinesthetic teaching. DexTac captures multi-dimensional tactile data—including contact force distributions and spatial contact regions—directly from human demonstrations. By integrating these rich tactile modalities into a policy network, the resulting contact-aware agent enables a dexterous hand to autonomously select and maintain optimal contact regions during complex interactions. We evaluate our framework on a challenging unimanual injection task. Experimental results demonstrate that DexTac achieves a 91.67% success rate. Notably, in high-precision scenarios involving small-scale syringes, our approach outperforms force-only baselines by 31.67%. These results underscore that learning multi-dimensional tactile priors from human demonstrations is critical for achieving robust, human-like dexterous manipulation in contact-rich environments.

title: Separate Channel Estimation for Transmissive RIS Based on Antenna-Side Favorable Propagation
authors: Ziwei Liu, Wen Chen, Zhendong Li, Yuxuan Zeng, Yanze Zhu, Ying Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract: In this paper, a novel three-stage separate channel estimation framework is proposed for transmissive reconfigurable intelligent surface (TRIS) wireless communication systems. TRIS communication systems achieve multi-stream communication capabilities equivalent to traditional large-scale antenna systems equipping only a single radio frequency (RF) link, but face significant challenges in channel estimation. However, the single-RF constraint and the passivity of TRIS elements pose significant challenges for high-dimensional channel estimation, often resulting in phase ambiguity when estimating the cascaded channel directly. To address these issues, the designed scheme is characterized by low complexity, low storage requirements, and high accuracy. First, the composite channel is constructed and estimated through uplink and downlink pilot transmission, where favorable propagation simplifies signal processing via dimension reduction, facilitating separate estimation and resolving the inherent phase ambiguity. Second, the antenna-to-TRIS channel is acquired with low complexity by exploiting the geometric prior of the TRIS, using a limited-memory Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (L-BFGS) algorithm tailored for large-scale optimization under the single-RF constraint. Thirdly, a subframe estimation scheme is introduced to formulate a sparse recovery problem for the TRIS-to-user channel. A lookahead orthogonal matching pursuit (LAOMP) algorithm is designed to enhance sparse channel recovery accuracy without requiring prior knowledge of sparsity.Simulation results demonstrate that, compared to least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) for normalized mean square error (NMSE) and gradient descent (GD) for convergence metrics, this approach reduces channel estimation NMSE by 42.46%, convergence iterations by 92.73%, and computational time by 85.97%, while exhibiting strong robustness under diverse noise distributions, TRIS phase errors, and Doppler effects.

title: A Lyapunov Characterization of Robust D-Stability with Application to Decentralized Integral Control of LTI Systems
authors: John-Paolo Casasanta, John W. Simpson-Porco
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: The concept of matrix D-stability plays an important role in applications, ranging from economic and biological system models to decentralized control. Here we provide necessary and sufficient Lyapunov-type conditions for the robust (block) D-stability property. We leverage this characterization as part of a novel Lyapunov analysis of decentralized integral control for MIMO LTI systems, providing sufficient conditions guaranteeing stability under low-gain and under arbitrary connection and disconnection of individual control loops.
