title: Uncertainty-Aware Intrusion Detection and Prevention for DDS-Based SCADA Systems
authors: Love Allen Chijioke Ahakonye, Hamza Ibrahim, Jae Min Lee, Dong-Seong Kim
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) deployments increasingly adopt publish–subscribe middleware to improve scalability, latency, and interoperability across heterogeneous field sites. Data Distribution Service (DDS) is a prominent real-time publish–subscribe standard that provides fine-grained Quality-of-Service (QoS) controls and security extensions, yet its data-centric communication model also introduces distinct attack surfaces, including topic spoofing, unauthorized publishers/subscribers, replay and delay injection, QoS abuse, and control-relevant data poisoning. This paper presents an intrusion detection and prevention system tailored to SCADA-over-DDS, emphasizing uncertainty-aware, autonomous decision-making. We evaluate the framework using replayed industrial control system traces in an emulated industrial testbed designed to approximate DDS-SCADA deployments, and additionally perform cross-dataset generalization experiments and a closed-loop digital twin simulation to assess cyber-physical coupling. No live physical process or operational industrial site was used. The framework formalizes SCADA-over-DDS as a closed-loop cyber-physical system with a middleware-mediated observation channel and proposes a multi-layer architecture that (i) fuses protocol, timing, and control-invariant signals; (ii) quantifies epistemic uncertainty for detection outputs; and (iii) executes prevention actions through a risk-sensitive policy constrained by control-safety envelopes. Under the evaluated conditions, the framework demonstrates detection accuracy (F1 > 0.93), inference latency (<250μs), and tamper-evident logging with measurable advantages including 54% reduction in false alarms and 79% fewer operator notifications over deterministic baselines and centralized database alternatives.

title: Geometric Sensitivity and Orientation-Induced Error Amplification in a Mobile Cable-Driven Parallel Robot on Inclined Surfaces
authors: Byeong-Geon Kim, Su-Won Jeong, Stéphane Caro, Kyoung-Su Park
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Mobile cable-driven parallel robots improve deployment flexibility by defining pulley positions through multiple mobile platforms. However, on uneven terrain, tilting of the mobile platforms changes the pulley positions and degrades end-effector tracking accuracy. This paper proposes a tilt-aware real-time kinematics computation method based on three-dimensional localization of each mobile platform. The pulley frames and end-effector anchor points are modeled using homogeneous transformation matrices, and pulley kinematics are incorporated to compute cable lengths more accurately under tilted conditions. A sensitivity analysis is also presented to quantify the effects of platform roll and pitch on cable-length variation caused by pulley offsets. Experiments on an 8-cable, 4-platform mobile cable-driven parallel robot under single- and multi-platform tilting scenarios show that ignoring platform tilting increases the mean tracking error from 5.66 mm to up to 16.77 mm, whereas the proposed method reduces the error to below 6.92 mm while mitigating platform oscillation. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method for accurate end-effector control in uneven-terrain environments.

title: Robust Data-Driven Path Tracking Control for Autonomous Vehicles: A Koopman Operator Approach With High-Order Super-Twisting Observer
authors: Shaobo Liang, Shuguo Pan, ZongLiang Chen, Wang Gao, Xianlu Tao
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Path tracking control is a critical component of autonomous driving technology. However, it faces significant challenges due to the inherent strong nonlinearity and dynamic uncertainties of autonomous vehicles (AVs), which remains a severe challenge in the field. To address the problem, this paper proposes a novel robust data-driven control framework designed to balance modeling accuracy with disturbance rejection performance. First, a control-oriented Deep Koopman model is constructed to map the nonlinear AV path-tracking error dynamics into a high-dimensional lifted space. Second, to address inevitable modeling residuals and external disturbances, a high-order super-twisting observer (HOSTO) is designed within the lifted space, which guarantees finite-time convergence of disturbance estimation, providing strong robust compensation. Finally, an approximate lifted-space MPC with Jacobian-based cost mapping is formulated as a standard Quadratic Program (QP), avoiding repeated nonlinear decoding during online optimization. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through comprehensive simulations and experiments, showing superior control accuracy and robustness against disturbances compared to existing algorithms.

title: Module-Level 3D Motion Perception and Closed-Loop Control of an SMA-Driven Origami Robotic Module for Versatile Robotic Systems_supp1-3703254.mp4
authors: Hu Jin
publish: N/A
abstract: Origami robotic modules offer compactness, compliance, and reconfigurability, but their system-level capabilities are often limited by the lack of reliable self-perception and closed-loop 3D motion control at the module level. This work presents a compact and lightweight shape memory alloy (SMA)-driven origami robotic module that achieves self-sensing and closed-loop three-dimensional motion control. The module integrates a foldable origami skeleton with four symmetrically arranged SMA spring actuators for omnidirectional bending and axial contraction. Embedded Hall-effect sensors enable intrinsic three-dimensional motion perception by measuring the module deformation state. A geometric kinematic model is established to map the skeleton deformation to the central-axis ending position of the module for reliable feedback control. Experimental results show mean calibration errors of 0.21 mm (x), 0.15 mm (y), 0.33 mm (z), and 4.78° for orientation estimation. Benefiting from module-level perception and control, multiple modules can be coordinated to perform crawling, confined-space climbing, walking, and object manipulation tasks. These results demonstrate the importance of closed-loop module-level motion control for reconfigurable modular robotic systems.

title: Joint Link Budget Management and System Performance Coordination for PAM4 Links in Lossless Optical Switching Networks
authors: Boyang Zheng, Henrique Freire Santana, Shiyi Xia, Nicola Calabretta
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: The rapid scaling of large artificial intelligence (AI) models has imposed unprecedented pressure on AI data center (DCs) infrastructures, demanding switching technologies with higher bandwidth, lower latency, and superior energy efficiency. In response to these demands, dynamic optical circuit switching (OCS) has emerged as a compelling architecture for scalable AI DCs by enabling transparent signal routing and eliminating optical–electrical–optical (OEO) regeneration. Nevertheless, the absence of OEO regeneration makes the link insertion loss (IL) and impairments fully path-dependent, determined by both the IL of OCS, optical links, and the number of switching hops. Such large variations in IL introduce fundamental incompatibilities with the fixed link budgets of standardized PAM4 transceivers. To address this challenge, compact optical amplifiers such as semiconductor optical amplifiers (SOAs) can be deployed to compensate for switching-induced loss. However, SOAs may introduce optical-to-signal noise ratio (OSNR) degradation and nonlinear distortions. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate an coordination platform that automatically adapts each link to achieve the required link budget and system performance while maintaining low hardware power consumption, fast data recovery, and lossless operation. The proposed architecture is enabled by transmitter-side dynamic gain provisioning using an SOA and joint digital signal processing (DSP) coordination across the transmitter–receiver. At the transmitter (Tx), a booster SOA is integrated into the Tx to compensate for routing-dependent loss across multi-stage switching fabrics through dynamic pump-current scheduling. At the receiver side (Rx), to mitigate nonlinear distortions induced by the booster SOA, Tx pre-emphasis, asymmetric feedforward equalization (AsFFE), and sparse second-order Volterra nonlinear equalizer (S2NLE) are jointly optimized to balance nonlinearity mitigation, hardware complexity, and power consumption. In addition, a distributed equalizer mechanism is introduced to eliminate DSP reconvergence overhead during dynamic switching. Experimental results at 53-Gbaud PAM4 demonstrate an effective system link budget of 25.2 dB, a reconvergence latency of < 4.9 ns, a 41.1% reduction in equalization power consumption, and an increase in effective packet utilization from 88.7% to 99.4%.

title: The Hyperspectral Microwave Photonic Instrument (HyMPI) and its NEDT Performance
authors: Víctor Torres, Fabrizio Gambini, Priscilla Mohammed, David Robles, Michael Coon, Mark Stephen, Edward Leong, Jeffrey Piepmeier, Antonia Gambacorta, Gary Carter
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: This work presents an overview of a hyperspectral microwave-photonic spectrometer and demonstrates the system end-to-end noise equivalent delta temperature (NEDT) performance. The system aims at augmenting the remote sensing capability from space, with a focus on the Earth's planetary boundary layer (PBL) thermal microwave (MW) spectral region (0-200 GHz). Combining a Photonic Integrated Circuit (PIC) channelizer and an application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) spectrometer, the PIC & ASIC (PICASIC) module can process 40 GHz spectra at hyperspectral (4 MHz) resolution. The photonic technology is agnostic to the spectral region, and multiple photonic modules can cover the entire 200 GHz spectrum. Measured results of the end-to-end system NEDT agree with predicted values, confirming that the NEDT is primarily dominated by the noise figure of the MW front-end, with the optical link adding no significant noise. The data also demonstrate that a single module enables simultaneous super-spectral (4 GHz) and hyperspectral (4 MHz) resolution channel analysis across a 40 GHz range.

title: A Data-Driven Approach for Coherency Identification in Large-Scale Power Systems
authors: Hêmin Golpîra, Bruno Francois
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: This paper proposes a temporal clustering–based approach for coherency identification in power grids that explicitly accounts for instrument transformer uncertainties and PMU measurement errors arising from communication failures. The method models the system using multiple single-bus–infinite bus representations and introduces a metric for the electrical distance between physical system buses and a fictitious infinite bus, which is used to cluster buses into coherent groups. A key challenge of clustering-based methods—namely, the defining the clustering threshold—is systematically addressed by deriving a mathematically rigorous, power system–governed equation, resulting in a consistent method suitable for online applications. The effectiveness of the proposed data-driven approach is demonstrated using both simulated and real PMU signals, including scenarios with packet losses.

title: Single-Phase Grounding Fault Localization for Neutral Non-effectively Grounded Networks with Control-heterogeneous Distributed Generations
authors: Zhenhong Lai, Hao Yi, Zhenxiong Wang, Fang Zhuo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The integration of the distributed generations (DG) stations distorts the conventional single-phase grounding (SPG) fault localization results. Considering the flexibility of DGs, a novel SPG fault localization methodology for the neutral non-effectively grounded distribution networks is proposed in this article. In the proposed method, DGs are controlled to inject the harmonic currents to actively participate in the localization process. Firstly, the SPG fault characteristic at the harmonic frequency is analyzed, and the measured harmonic impedance at the feeder junction is shown to correlate with the fault distance. Subsequently, typical control strategies of the grid-following and grid-forming DGs under the SPG fault are modified to assist the harmonic impedance detection. Then, the coupling effect between DGs at various feeders is studied, and this coupling effect is eliminated by modifying the output impedance of the control-heterogeneous DGs at the special frequency band. Finally, the SPG fault localization can be realized through the coordination operation of the multiple control-heterogeneous DGs, and the simulation model and the hardware-in-loop experimental results verify the effectiveness under various testing conditions.

title: Exploring Critical Testing Scenarios for Decision-Making Policies: An LLM Approach_supp1-3703032.pdf
authors: Huaxin Pei
publish: N/A
abstract: Recent advances in decision-making policies have led to notable progress in various fields such as autonomous driving and robotics. However, testing these policies remains crucial with the existence of critical scenarios that may threaten their reliability. Despite ongoing research, challenges such as low testing efficiency and limited scenario diversity persist, primarily due to the complexity of the policies and their operating environments. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an adaptable Large Language Model (LLM)-driven adaptive testing framework to explore critical and diverse testing scenarios for decision-making policies. Specifically, we introduce a “generate-test-feedback” pipeline, leveraging templated prompt engineering to harness the world knowledge and reasoning abilities of LLMs. Additionally, a multi-scale scenario generation strategy is proposed to overcome the limitations of LLMs in making fine-grained adjustments, thereby further enhancing testing efficiency. Furthermore, during the feedback phase, a well-designed scenario evaluation module assesses the criticality and diversity of the generated scenarios for adaptive testing. The proposed LLM-driven method is extensively evaluated on five widely recognized benchmarks, demonstrating that our proposed method significantly outperforms baseline methods in uncovering both critical and diverse scenarios. These findings highlight the great promise of LLM-driven schemes in advancing the testing of decision-making policies.

title: ICE: Reducing Search Space for Error-inducing Input Detection
authors: Youshuai Tan, Zhanwei Zhang, Zishuo Ding, Jinfu Chen, Weiyi Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering
abstract: Floating-point programs play a crucial role in scientific and engineering applications as well as daily life. Due to the inherent inaccuracies of floating-point arithmetic, these programs are susceptible to errors. While only a small subset of possible inputs may lead to significant inaccuracies in the program’s final results, such errors can have serious consequences. Prior approaches have been developed to identify error-inducing inputs using search algorithms. However, the task is challenging due to the sparsity of the search space. Furthermore, these tools frequently depend on high-precision programs as oracles for computing floating-point errors. This reliance introduces additional challenges, including prolonged execution times and instability. To detect these inputs more effectively and efficiently, we propose a simple yet effective method called ICE, designed to generate reduced and erroneous domains for these approaches. ICE first calculates the errors of the target program by taking all half-precision floating-point numbers as inputs. Instead of calculating errors based on high-precision programs, ICE injects small perturbations into each atomic operation of the program and measures the difference between the perturbed and original results to estimate floating-point errors. Subsequently, ICE selects the numbers with errors in the top n% as the endpoints of the important ranges. These individual ranges, which form domains, can then be used to improve existing tools by reducing the search space. To evaluate ICE, we apply ICE with three state-of-the-art error-inducing input detection approaches, i.e., ATOMU, HSED, and FPCC. Experimental results demonstrate that ICE could improve the performance of existing approaches by effectively reducing the search space, detecting more error-inducing inputs, and significantly shortening the search time.

title: Test-Time Adaptation for Photovoltaic Installation Growth Assessment
authors: Rui Huang, Huanjing Yue, Jianhua Guo, Xiaoqiang Lu, Jingyu Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurately monitoring the growth of photovoltaic power station installation is crucial to assessing its socio-economic benefits and environmental impact. Researchers have constructed benchmark datasets of photovoltaic panels based on high-resolution satellite images to develop methods for detecting areas of change. However, these recent efforts confront great challenges when applied to previously unseen regions due to the limited geographical and photovoltaic panel type diversity in existing datasets, as well as the difficulty of collecting accurate pixel-level annotations. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to introduce test-time adaptation (TTA) to the change detection task. We propose a novel framework, termed PA-TTA (Test-Time Adaptation for Photovoltaic Installation Growth Assessment), which addresses both domain shift and catastrophic forgetting, enabling the detection of photovoltaic installation growth across diverse geographic regions without requiring ground-truth labels. Specifically, PA-TTA designs a cross-image context-aware (CCA) module based on hybrid attention, which utilizes a cross-image prototype bank to provide complementary information across images, thereby generating more accurate pseudo labels. Then, it uses the task-agnostic knowledge of the general segmentation model to adapt pseudo labels. Experimental results demonstrate that PA-TTA achieves significantly improved performance in complex and previously unseen environments. On the Telangana and Bavaria target domain data, the F1 score improves by 9.49% and 5.77%, respectively, over the second-best method. This confirms the robustness and practical applicability of the proposed approach for photovoltaic panels installation assessment without access to ground-truth labels. Code is available at https://github.com/DonMuv/PA-TTA.

title: On-Orbit Traveling-Wave Tube Amplifier Reliability
authors: William Menninger
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Stellant Systems first delivered traveling-wave tubes (TWTs) for mounting on board communication satellites in the early 1960’s. Since then, Stellant has delivered over 10,000 Space TWTs which have accumulated 1 billion operating hours. In addition, Stellant has supplied electronic power conditioners (EPCs) and linearized channel amplifiers (LCAMPs) for these TWTs since the 1990’s, with over 9,000 EPCs delivered having accumulated over 900 million operating hours and over 3500 LCAMPs delivered having accumulated over 300 million operating hours. This paper examines reliability statistics from Stellant Space linearized, channelized TWT amplifiers (LCTWTAs), focusing mainly on production hardware in orbit since 2000.

title: Design and Development of All-metal Vivaldi Phased Array Antenna for Radar Application
authors: R. Venkateswaran, Praveena, Naveen Kumar Sharma, P. Srikrishna, D Kannadasan, M. Sumathy, M. Vijaya Kumar, S K Datta
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: A compact, high power, broadband phased array antenna for high power microwave application is designed and developed at $\mathbf{X}$ band in this present work. It is a square phased array with $\mathbf{1 6}$ Vivaldi elements. The antenna is comprised of the slot line structure and co-axial feed. The design is carried out by 3D electromagnetic simulation. The simulated results show that the VSWR (voltage standing wave ratio) of the array antenna in the operating frequency band (X-band with 2 GHz bandwidth) is less than 2.0, isolation between the ports are less than -20 dB and gain of the array is 18 dBi. Initially, a single element is measured for VSWR and antenna gain parameter which are closely matching with the simulated results. The array antenna is fabricated and measurement for VSWR and antenna gain parameter are in progress, which will be presented at the time of conference.

title: Efficient Modeling of VE Devices Using Interpolation of Circuit Parameters
authors: Vadim J. Jabotinski, Alexander N. Vlasov, Simon J. Cooke, Thomas M. Antonsen
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: A novel general method for computationally efficient modeling of large class of standing and traveling wave structures with series of interaction gaps used in traveling-wave tubes, klystrons, and other VED is presented. We determine circuit parameters of the reference single-gap RF cell for four values of each of the defining dimensions in its range of interest from EM simulation using codes like Analyst or HFSS. The required circuit parameters for all other RF cells, constituting the whole RF structure, which dimensions may differ from the reference cell, one obtains using polynomial or spline interpolation of the reference cell circuit parameters. Joining Equations then define the resulting Z-matrix of the whole multigap RF structure, for which one computes power versus frequency using large signal codes like CRISTINE or TESLA-Z. Computationally efficient modeling is demonstrated for optimization of the example serpentine geometry $K_{a}$-band TWT structure.

title: Containment Control of Nonlinear Multi-Agent Systems: An Output Regulation Approach
authors: Jingyi Hua, Xiaoming Su, Adiya Bao
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper studies the output containment control problem for a class of nonlinear multi-agent systems under directed communication graphs. By constructing a containment error based on the Laplacian matrix, the containment control objective is equivalently transformed into a nonlinear cooperative output regulation problem of a composite system, with the leader dynamics acting as an exosystem. It is shown that the output containment control problem is solvable if and only if the associated regulator equations admit a local solution. For nonlinear affine systems with a well-defined relative degree, the regulator equations are further reduced to a center manifold equation associated with the zero dynamics. Sufficient conditions for the solvability of this equation are established via zero-dynamics analysis and center manifold theory.

title: Gyrotrons for Fusion Power Plants
authors: R. Lawrence Ives, Michael Read, Jeff Neilson, Thuc Bui, George Collins, Thuy Le, Hien Tran
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Fusion power plants using electron cyclotron heating will require high performance gyrotrons to provide the required power. These gyrotrons must exhibit high efficiency and reasonable cost if fusion power is to economically compete with existing energy sources. This presentation will describe designs that achieve the current specifications for these RF sources. This includes efficiencies approaching or exceeding $70 \%$ and output coupling that reduces both gyrotron and system cost. Machine Learning research is also described that will ensure power supplies are optimized for all RF heating scenarios.

title: An FAS-ARMAX Approach to Minimum Variance Control for Nonlinear MIMO Manipulators
authors: Junxiang Zhang, Zhong Chu, Guangren Duan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the optimal regulation problem of nonlinear multi-input multi-output (MIMO) manipulators subject to process and measurement stochastic noises. Achieving regulation is challenging due to the highly coupled nonlinear dynamics and the infinite historical data sequences generated by stochastic disturbances. Although the fully actuated system (FAS) approach can transform the nonlinear dynamics into a linear double-integrator structure, this transformation by itself is insufficient to ensure the optimal regulation performance, as it cannot directly handle unmeasurable states and random white noises. To address this issue, we propose a systematic control framework that transforms the linearized FAS model into a fixeddimension Auto-Regressive Moving Average with eXogenous inputs (ARMAX) formulation for Minimum Variance Control (MVC). First, to jointly address the stochastic noises and infinite historical sequences, we construct a discrete-time input-output model by integrating a steady-state Kalman filter and the Cayley-Hamilton theorem into the system transformation. Besides, a rigorous <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{Z}$</tex>-domain analysis based on the Maximum Modulus Principle is carried out to establish the structural identifiability of the Moving Average (MA) term. Finally, simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively suppresses random disturbances.

title: Adaptive Fault-Tolerant Control for the Speed and Tension System of the Reversible Cold Strip Rolling Mill: A Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Le Liu, Zhuolu Chen, Nuan Shao, Shuzong Chen
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To address the issues of multivariable strong coupling, nonlinearity, and explosion of complexity in the speed and tension system of the reversible cold strip rolling mill (RCSRM), this paper proposes a fault-tolerant tracking control method based on a high-order fully actuated system (HOFAS) approach. By transforming the original mill model into a fully actuated form, the proposed method effectively resolves the limited control performance caused by the conservative handling of coupling terms and high model dependency in traditional approaches. The fully actuated model not only preserves the complete system structure and decoupling characteristics but also provides a parameterized design framework for adaptive fault-tolerant control (FTC). Building on this foundation, the adaptive fault-tolerant controllers are designed to compensate for actuator multiplicative and additive faults. The proposed controllers ensure that the tracking errors of the closed-loop system remain uniformly ultimately bounded under fault conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high-precision tracking of both speed and tension in the rolling mill system, significantly enhancing its dynamic response and fault-tolerant performance.

title: iGDAM-PER: LiDAR-Guided Waypoint Generation with Prioritized TD3 for Goal-Driven Exploration
authors: Yibo Li, Zhe Guan, Man Hao
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In unknown environments, a robot must reach a designated goal while exploring and mapping online. However, classical map-based exploration often relies on occupancy-derived information gain, making it sensitive to mapping quality and computational overhead; in contrast, purely local RL policies lack long-horizon guidance and can become trapped in local minima. To tackle these challenges, we propose iGDAM-PER, a hierarchical framework that performs online SLAM and plans over an incrementally built occupancy map. At the local level, we adopt TD3 with prioritized experience replay to track intermediate waypoints with smooth, safety-aware motion. Building on this reliable local execution, at the global level iGDAM generates waypoint candidates directly from raw 2D LiDAR scans using three complementary strategies: boundary extension, narrow-gap detection, and open-space guidance. Candidates are ranked by a heuristic that balances goal progress and motion cost, and incorporates a LiDAR-derived exploration gain computed from the fraction of max-range (infinite-range) beams, thereby reducing reliance on occupancy-based information gain. Experiments in ROS/Gazebo show that iGDAM-PER reduces navigation time and path length compared with GDAM variants while maintaining reliable goal reaching.

title: Dissipative Filtering of Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Singular Systems Under Stochastic Communication Protocols
authors: Lin Xu, Jiangrong Li, Bohang Shen
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the dissipative filtering problem for discrete-time nonlinear singular systems. Firstly, an interval type-2 fuzzy singular system is constructed to represent the nonlinear singular system with parameter uncertainties. Secondly, considering the impact of communication burden on system performance, an interval type-2 fuzzy filter is designed based on a stochastic communication protocol, and sufficient conditions are derived to ensure the dissipativity of the resulting fuzzy augmented system. Finally, the effectiveness and correctness of the obtained results are verified through numerical simulation examples.

title: Differentially Private Containment Control for Discrete-Time Multi-Agent Systems: A Privacy-Convergence Trade-Off
authors: Chen Zhang, Shuai Liu, Licheng Wang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of differentially private containment control in discrete-time multi-agent systems, where the objective is to steer a group of follower agents into the convex hull formed by multiple leaders while preserving the privacy of their initial states. To achieve this, we propose a control framework in which each follower agent injects exponentially decaying Laplacian noise into its state updates and employs a local noise feedback mechanism. This approach ensures rigorous privacy guarantees over an infinite horizon without compromising closed-loop stability. In contrast, due to the inherent conflict between unstable leader dynamics and the diminishing noise, privacy for leader agents can only be ensured over a finite time horizon. We analyze the resulting trade-off between privacy preservation and containment convergence, and identify a feasible region of design parameters that guarantees mean-square containment consensus before the maximum allowable disclosure time elapses. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed containment control scheme is validated via a numerical simulation.

title: Data-Driven Model Predictive Control for Connected Vehicle Platoons: A Hankel-Based Approach
authors: Xianglong Li, Jiange Wang, Lijuan Chen, Wei Gao, Xiaolei Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Traditional model predictive control (MPC) for connected vehicle platoons often depends on explicit system models and suffers from high computational complexity, especially in heterogeneous vehicle environments. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a data-driven model predictive control (DDMPC) framework that eliminates the need for explicit system identification. By leveraging offline-collected vehicle motion data, we construct a non-parametric predictive model using Hankel matrices, effectively circumventing the challenges associated with nonlinear and uncertain dynamics. Persistently exciting input sequences are generated using multi-frequency sinusoidal signals with additive random noise, followed by noise smoothing and outlier removal to ensure data quality. The control formulation incorporates slack variables, regularization terms, and moving average smoothing within a convex optimization setup, enabling robust and low-latency control. Simulation results in a four-vehicle platoon demonstrate rapid convergence within 10 seconds, with tracking errors below 0.1 m and acceleration standard deviation under 0.06 m/s2 under noisy conditions. The proposed approach exhibits strong generality and scalability, contributing to enhanced traffic efficiency and safety in connected automated vehicle platoons.

title: Long-Tailed Semantic Segmentation via Uninformative Probing and Maximum Entropy Calibration
authors: Huayang Zhuge
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Semantic segmentation models inherently suffer from severe class imbalance, heavily biasing their predictions toward dominant categories. This bias is further exacerbated in Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) scenarios due to domain shifts. While existing calibration methods typically rely on global dataset statistics to adjust predictions, they overlook the complex, spatial-aware implicit priors that deep networks actually parameterize during training. In this paper, we uncover a novel phenomenon: a model's implicit class distribution and spatial bias can be directly extracted by feeding it pure noise images, which completely block the visual likelihood. Grounded in Bayesian probability, we leverage this observation to propose a simple, statistics-free framework comprising Uninformative Probing and Maximum Entropy Calibration. By using noise images as probes, we isolate the network's dynamically learned prior and apply an entropy maximization loss to neutralize it. This joint optimization explicitly penalizes the model for relying on statistical shortcuts, compelling it to learn authentic, robust visual representations for rare classes. As a versatile, plug-and-play module, our approach demonstrates consistent empirical improvements across diverse settings without requiring complex architectural changes. Specifically, our method achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$68.44 \% \text{mIoU}(+0.14 \%)$</tex> on the GTA5 → Cityscapes and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$62.58 \% \text{mIoU}(+1.68 \%)$</tex> on the SYNTHIA → Cityscapes UDA benchmarks, alongside <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$77.01 \% \text{mIoU}(+0.57 \%)$</tex> on the fully supervised Cityscapes benchmark. These results validate that our mathematically grounded, noise-based approach delivers highly competitive performance while maintaining algorithmic simplicity.

title: Fixed-Time Interval Estimation for Descriptor Systems with Application to Fully Actuated Systems
authors: Yang Zhou, Longwen Liu, Wei Zhou, Jinyang Huang, Wenrui Shi, Yunyang Xia
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the interval state estimation problem for a class of discrete-time linear descriptor systems. Componentwise state bounds are computed using a backward propagation representation combined with absolute-value operations, thereby obtaining better computational efficiency than interval hull computation while possessing the fixed-time convergence property. Meanwhile, a linear programming-based optimization strategy is developed to minimize the width of the state estimation interval. Besides, a fully-actuated controller is further introduced to ensure the feasibility of the proposed method. Finally, simulation results are provided to demonstrate the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed method.

title: Dynamic Event-Triggered Fixed-Time Control for Fully Actuated Systems with Unknown Gain
authors: Tianyu Kang, Jinxuan Zhang, Fuyong Wang, Zhongxin Liu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel dynamic event-triggered fixed-time control scheme for high-order fully actuated (HOFA) systems with unknown control gain and parametric uncertainties. First, the system is directly modeled in its original highorder fully actuated form, which preserves physical interpretability and simplifies controller design without requiring model transformation. Then, adaptive laws are are designed to estimate the unknown parameters, ensuring that both the parameter estimates and the closed-loop states converge within a fixed time independent of initial conditions. Additionally, a dynamic event-triggered mechanism is introduced to reduce communication burden while automatically adjusting inter-event intervals based on system dynamics. Zeno behavior is rigorously excluded without relying on the proven boundedness of the closed-loop signals, thus avoiding circular reasoning. Furthermore, fixed-time stability is established through Lyapunov analysis, and the settling time can be explicitly prescribed by tuning design parameters. Ultimately, the effectiveness of the proposed scheme is validated through simulations on a single-link manipulator.

title: Developmental Progress on a Narrowband TWT
authors: Rupa Basu, Anthony Challis, Christopher Gilmour, Evie Hutt, Gareth Pearson, Christopher Loftin, Christopher Howard
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: A Ku-band narrowband traveling-wave tube (TWT) has been developed to enhance efficiency and suppress oscillations encountered in wideband TWTs when used with narrowband RF components. The design employs a simplified helix slow-wave structure (SWS) with optimized pitch profile and uniform dielectric loading, achieving higher interaction impedance, improved beamwave synchronism, and a $20 \%$ reduction in length for equivalent output power. Initial prototypes demonstrate VSWR better than 2:1 and meet design tolerances. Ongoing testing will confirm suitability for integration into 110 W microwave power modules (MPMs).

title: Estimation of Frequency and RoCoF of Non-Stationary Signals in Power Systems: A Fast Convergent Approach
authors: Xuanqi Wang, Chaoyu Yang, Wang Rui, Peng Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a two-step scheme for estimating the frequency and its rate of change of non-stationary signals in modern power electronics-dominated power systems. In the first step, a finite-time identification scheme based on the Volterra integral operators is designed to track the frequency of the non-stationary signals with fast convergence. A novel augmented framework of signal identification is proposed to eliminate the effects of the harmonics and the measurement noise. In the second step, the estimated frequency is injected into a non-asymptotic numerical differentiator to track its rate of change. It has been proven the proposed estimator possesses fast convergence and robustness against the disturbance. Finally, simulation and experiments are conducted, and the estimation results are compared with known algorithms, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.
