title: RIS-Aided E2E Multi-Path Uplink Transmission Optimization for 6G Time-Sensitive Services
authors: Liu Cao, Zisheng Gong, Ziyue Xiao, Zhaoyu Liu, Houtianfu Wang, Lyutianyang Zhang
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: The Access Traffic Steering, Switching, and Splitting (ATSSS) defined in the latest 3GPP Release 19 enables traffic flow over the multiple access paths to achieve the lower-latency End-to-end (E2E) delivery for 6G time-sensitive services. However, the existing E2E multi-path operation often falls short of more stringent QoS requirements for 6G time-sensitive services. This work proposes a Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS)-aided E2E multi-path uplink (UL) transmission architecture that explicitly accounts for both radio link latency and N3 backhaul latency, via the coupled designs of the UL traffic-splitting ratio, transmit power, receive combining, and RIS phase shift under practical constraints to achieve the minimum average E2E latency. We develop an alternating optimization framework that updates the above target parameters to be optimized. The simulations were conducted to compare the effectiveness of the proposed E2E optimization framework that lowers the average E2E latency up to 43% for a single user and 32% for the whole system compared with baselines in our prior work [1].

title: A Novel ACDM Scheme for NOMA Systems in Time–Frequency Dispersive Channels
authors: Khaled Ramadan
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Doubly dispersive and high-mobility channels severely degrade the performance of conventional Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)- and Affine Frequency Division Multiplexing (AFDM)-based Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) systems. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a novel Affine Cosine Division Multiplexing (ACDM) waveform by replacing the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) in AFDM with the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT). The proposed ACDM is integrated into a NOMA framework and evaluated against OFDM-NOMA and AFDM-NOMA over doubly dispersive channels for two values of the maximum normalized Doppler index, fmax = 2 and fmax = 025. Closed-form ACDM phase and spreading factors are derived, and the corresponding modulation–channel–demodulation structures are analytically compared. Performance is assessed in terms of Bit-Error-Rate (BER), achievable bit rate, and Outage Probability (OP). Simulation results show that NOMA-ACDM achieves up to 85% and 69% lower BER, 32–65% BER reduction under imperfect Successive Interference Cancellation (SIC), up to 8.2% and 5.6% higher bit rate, and 63–76% and 56–63% lower OP compared to the reference schemes. These results demonstrate that ACDM significantly enhances reliability and spectral efficiency in doubly dispersive NOMA systems.

title: GT-MARL: Graph- and Transformer-Enhanced Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Cloud-Edge Collaborative Scheduling
authors: Xu Han, Kaiyuan Qi, Li Pan, Shijun Liu
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Scheduling across cloud and edge systems must handle job heterogeneity, dynamic arrivals, and coupled resource constraints. To address the above issues, we present GT-MARL, a reinforcement learning framework with graph and Transformer enhancements for joint task selection and resource allocation across multiple clusters. At each decision epoch, GT-MARL encodes a heterogeneous graph over tasks and resources with Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN) to capture structural dependencies, applies a temporal Transformer to model backlog evolution and delayed interactions, and produces factorized decisions for each cluster through a Transformer-guided task selection head and discrete resource allocation with a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) head. Training follows centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) using a centralized critic and masked action spaces. Experiments on both synthetic and trace-replay workloads show that GT-MARL consistently achieves the best p95 job completion time (JCT), while keeping mean JCT competitive with representative heuristic and learning baselines. Specifically, GT-MARL obtains 35.56 mean JCT and 70.76 p95 JCT on the synthetic workload, and 144.21 mean JCT and 264.47 p95 JCT on the trace-replay workload. On the synthetic workload, this corresponds to a 26.3% p95 JCT reduction relative to the best mean baseline with only a 3.0% increase in mean JCT. Additional queueing and service decomposition across workload intensities indicates that the tail-latency gain mainly comes from alleviating queue accumulation rather than shortening the inherent service time of tasks. GT-MARL provides a favorable tradeoff between mean latency and tail latency for scheduling under SLO constraints across cloud and edge systems.

title: SemAugCom: Semantic Augmented Communication for Few-Shot Learning Tasks
authors: Jincheng Peng, Huanlai Xing, Lexi Xu, Shouxi Luo, Laha Ale, Zonghai Zhu, Zhiwen Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Semantic communication, a promising paradigm for intelligent communication, conveys intent by extracting key semantic information rather than transmitting complete raw bits. However, existing semantic communication systems rely on extensive training data, which is often unavailable in few-shot learning scenarios, causing them to struggle with the pervasive long-tail distribution problem. In such distributions, head classes possess abundant samples, while most tail classes have only a sparse number of samples with limited features. To enhance the adaptability of semantic communication systems to long-tailed data distributions and meet the requirements of few-shot learning tasks, this paper proposes SemAugCom, a semantic-augmented communication framework. It employs a multi-granularity hierarchical semantic encoder to extract and fuse texture, spatial, and semantic features. Additionally, an offline tail-class generation module is designed to augment samples for tail classes. An online feature enhancement mechanism with channel adaptation is adopted to achieve efficient semantic transmission and improve robustness. Furthermore, the semantic encoder and decoder are jointly trained and fine-tuned, enabling system-wide optimization while preserving knowledge acquired from previous tasks. Experimental results indicate that the performance of SemAugCom exceeds the baseline methods in terms of Fréchet inception distance, peak signal-to-noise ratio, and the classification accuracy of tail classes on the CIFAR-100, miniImageNet, and CUB200 datasets.

title: Breaking the CP Limit: Robust Long-Range OFDM Sensing via Interference Cleaning
authors: Umut Utku Erdem, Lucas Giroto, Benedikt Geiger, Taewon Jeong, Silvio Mandelli, Christian Karle, Benjamin Nuss, Laurent Schmalen, Thomas Zwick
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radar Systems
abstract: In orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM)-based radar and integrated sensing and communication systems, the sensing range is traditionally limited by the round-trip time corresponding to the cyclic prefix (CP) duration. Targets whose echoes arrive after this duration induce intersymbol interference (ISI) and associated intercarrier interference (ICI), which significantly degrade detection performance, elevate the interference-noise floor in the radar image, and reduce the useful signal power due to window mismatch. Existing methods face a trade-off between recovering useful signal and suppressing interference, particularly in multi-target scenarios. This paper proposes two frameworks to resolve this dilemma, offering a flexible trade-off between computational cost and target detection performance. First, a signal model is derived, demonstrating that ISI and ICI-oriented interference often dominates thermal noise in high-dynamic-range scenarios. To combat the ISI and ICI-based interference-noise floor increase, the joint-interference cancellation with coherent compensation is proposed. This approach is an efficient evolution of the successive-interference cancellation algorithm, utilizing high-accuracy chirp Z-transform estimation and frequency-domain coherent compensation to recover weak distant targets. For scenarios requiring maximum precision, the full reconstruction-based sliding window scheme is presented, which shifts the receive window to capture optimal signal energy while performing full-signal reconstruction for all detected targets. Numerical results show that both methods outperform state-of-the-art benchmarks.

title: Quantization Robustness of Monotone Operator Equilibrium Networks
authors: James Li, Philip H.W. Leong, Thomas Chaffey
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: Monotone operator equilibrium networks (MonDEQs) are implicit models whose well-posedness and convergence rely on a single scalar margin computed from the weight matrix, which has made them attractive for learned controllers with closed-loop stability guarantees. When such models are deployed on low-precision hardware, weights are quantized, and these structural guarantees can be destroyed. We provide an explicit quantizationpreservation certificate: a single computable check on the weight perturbation determines whether the quantized network retains existence, uniqueness, linear convergence, and a bounded displacement of its equilibrium. The same threshold certifies the backward solve as well as the forward solve, so it governs both training and inference. MNIST experiments confirm a phase transition at the predicted threshold: three- and four-bit post-training quantization diverge, while five-bit and above converge; quantization-aware training recovers provable convergence at four bits.

title: Enhanced Vessel Trajectory Prediction With Novel Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System-Based Alpha–Beta Filter Using AIS Data
authors: Junaid Khan, Umar Zaman, Ahmad ul Hassan, Sajjad Hussain, Eunkyu Lee, Kyungsup Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Shipping is integral to global trade, with increasing maritime traffic elevating safety risks. Accurate trajectory prediction based on Automatic Identification System (AIS) data is essential to prevent collisions and enhance navigational safety. This study introduces a novel Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System (MFIS)-based alpha-beta filter for vessel trajectory prediction, addressing the limitations of conventional static parameter tuning. The proposed method dynamically adjusts alpha and beta parameters using fuzzy logic and processes speed, heading, latitude, and longitude inputs to predict trajectories over extended periods. The fuzzy alpha-beta filter’s performance was evaluated on AIS data from 50 ships using metrics such as Average Displacement Error (ADE), Final Displacement Error (FDE), Non-Linear ADE (NL-ADE), and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. It achieved superior accuracy (ADE: 0.020, FDE: 0.031, NL-ADE: 0.011, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>: 0.97) compared to conventional and deep learning models, demonstrating its efficacy in complex maritime scenarios. These findings mark a significant advancement in trajectory prediction systems, enhancing maritime safety and operational efficiency.

title: Research on Motor Vehicle Conflict Identification at Unsignalized Intersections Based on UAV Video Perception
authors: Xiaoyu Wu, Xiaoqiang Zhang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Traffic control at unsignalized intersections on urban secondary roads is often insufficient, leaving these locations prone to conflicts as demand increases. UAV video perception can detect and track motor vehicles, identify conflict types, and estimate traffic delays, thereby supporting targeted measures to improve intersection efficiency. In this study, a two-lane, two-way unsignalized four-leg intersection was selected. UAV videos were collected during the morning peak, off-peak, and evening peak periods to build a dataset covering cars, trucks, and buses. YOLOv5s and DeepSORT were used for multi-object tracking. After vehicle trajectories were extracted and operational states reconstructed, three conflict types, namely head-on, rear-end, and angle conflicts, were identified. Trajectory-derived parameters and observed traffic volumes were used as inputs to a VISSIM simulation model. By adjusting major-road priority-control modes, 95% confidence intervals and mean delay values were calculated. The calibrated simulation results were then used to compare time-dependent priority-control schemes and provide site-specific operational recommendations.

title: A Plot-to-Track Association Framework Based on Graph Representation Learning for Compact HFSWR
authors: Xiaotong Li, Weifeng Sun, Yonggang Ji, Weimin Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: To address plot-to-track association ambiguities caused by low spatial resolution and measurement uncertainties of compact high-frequency surface wave radar (HFSWR) operating in complex maritime environments, a hybrid framework integrating a target state prediction method based on Transformer model and a plot-to-track association method using graph-based feature representation is proposed. In the target state prediction stage, the Transformer model employs self-attention mechanisms to capture long-term temporal dependencies in historical track data to improve the prediction accuracy of the plot-to-track association gate center. In the plot-to-track association stage, a graph autoencoder (GAE) is used to extract graph structure features of candidate measured plots from their corresponding range-Doppler (R-D) spectra. The GAE encodes the diffusion patterns and spatial correlations of radar echoes on the R-D spectrum into discriminative graph feature vectors. The graph feature vectors, which can enhance both inter-target discrimination and target-clutter differentiation, serve as the basis for association similarity computation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly reduces target state prediction errors and suppresses false plot-to-track associations, achieving an average tracking time on target of 135.6 minutes, which is improved by 36% compared with the baseline method (motion model-based target state prediction combined with nearest neighbor data association using kinematic parameters).

title: Pulse Reverse Voltage Electropolishing of Superconducting Radio Frequency Niobium Cavities in Aqueous, Low Concentration Sulfuric Acid Electrolytes without Hydrofluoric Acid
authors: Timothy D. Hall, Maria Inman, Stephen Snyder
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: Niobium superconducting radio frequency cavities (SRF) are required for the International Linear Collider as well as other high energy physics projects. Conventional electropolishing of SRF cavities is based on the well-established viscous salt film paradigm and utilizes a viscous electrolyte consisting of a mixture of sulfuric acid (95-98%) and hydrofluoric acid (49%) in a 9:1 volume ratio. The hydrofluoric acid is included to remove the niobium oxide film formed during electropolishing. We have demonstrated an innovative electropolishing process for niobium coupons in low concentration (5%) aqueous sulfuric acid without hydrofluoric acid enabled by pulse reverse voltage waveforms electrolyte. In this contribution, we review our efforts to scale-up the electropolishing process to single-cell and three-cell niobium SRF cavities.

title: Neural Adaptive Guaranteed Cost Tracking Control for Monorail Crane Braking System Based on FAS Approach
authors: Tao Xu, Gang Shen, Yajun Gao, Liyao Hu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a neural adaptive guaranteed cost tracking control strategy for the monorail crane braking system (MCBS) in an underground coal mine using the fully actuated system (FAS) approach. The MCBS is subject to various influences during operation, including modeling errors and parameter uncertainties, making it challenging for conventional control methods to guarantee system stability and tracking accuracy. To address this issue, a radial basis function neural network (RBFNN) is employed to approximate the unknown nonlinear components of the system, thereby improving the control performance. By combining the above approximation scheme with the guaranteed cost control approach, a neural adaptive guaranteed cost tracking (NAGCT) controller is developed. The proposed controller effectively attenuates the uncertain nonlinearities of the system, and allows the upper bound of the defined cost function (UBDCF) characterizing the tracking performance of the MCBS to be preset arbitrarily. Simulation results further verify the effectiveness of the proposed control strategy. This work provides a feasible solution for high-precision and high-reliability motion control of mining machinery.

title: Experimental Study of a ms-Level Terahertz Gyrotron Equipped with a Pulsed Magnet
authors: Jia-Ji Feng, Feng Zhang, Xin-Yin Cao, Gao-Feng Liu, Chao-Hai Du
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: The gyrotron equipped with pulsed magnets is one of the most promising candidates to generate high-power electromagnetic wave in the terahertz band. However, the narrow available radiation duration of several to tens of microseconds in conventional pulsed gyrotrons limits their performance. In this paper, a terahertz gyrotron based on the 20-T high-field pulsed magnet is developed and experimentally investigated. Using the natural time-varying pulsed field strength, millisecond-level radiation can be achieved. With a voltage of 18 kV and a current of 1.5 A, cumulative energy near 1 Joule is obtained. The time-varying dynamic process is recorded and exhibits a unique asymmetric waveform, which is in good agreement with the theoretical predictions. This work will promote the development of high-power terahertz sources and high-resolution spectrometers.

title: Multi-UAVs Collaborative Path Planning Based on Improved Particle Swarm Optimization and Artificial Potential Field
authors: Tianxiao Ma, Ke Zhang, Wanglei Cheng, Bin Jiang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To address the challenges of frequent local minima and the difficulty of balancing safety and smoothness in multi-UAV cooperative path planning in complex 3D environments, this paper proposes a cooperative planning scheme that integrates an improved particle swarm optimization (IPSO) algorithm with an artificial potential field (APF) method. Firstly, a static three-dimensional environment with cylindrical obstacles is constructed. Secondly, an improved particle swarm optimization algorithm is proposed based on the integration of a Logistic chaotic initialization strategy, a differential evolution (DE) operator and a priority map, so as to maintain population diversity and enhance path-search performance for bypassing obstacles in complex environments. Then, a hierarchical framework consisting of global search, multi-strategy population optimization and APF-based local refinement is established, and a multi-objective fitness function incorporating path length, potential-field forces and collision penalties is designed. Finally, B-spline trajectory smoothing and yaw/turn-radius constraints are applied to generate dynamically feasible trajectories, and time interpolation combined with collision detection is used to achieve cooperative collision avoidance among multiple UAVs. Simulation results show that the proposed method can efficiently generate safe and smooth cooperative paths.

title: Optimization of Data-Driven Billet Quality Prediction Model Based on DLLCOA Algorithm
authors: Zhang Wenbo, Qian Junlei, Du Xueqiang, Zeng Kai
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Continuous casting serves as a pivotal process in steel production, where the dynamic coupling of process parameters and multi-field interactions directly govern slab quality stability and production efficiency. Traditional mechanistic models often fall short in fully capturing the intricate nonlinear relationships, resulting in constrained predictive accuracy and adaptability under diverse operating conditions. Data-driven methodologies offer a promising avenue for achieving high-precision quality early warning. This paper addresses the critical challenges of low optimization efficiency and proneness to local optima in hyperparameter tuning for neural networks within continuous casting slab crack defect prediction. A Dynamic Double-Lévy Long-nosed Coati Optimization Algorithm (DLLCOA) is proposed. Built upon the original Coati Optimization Algorithm, DLLCOA integrates Tent chaotic mapping combined with Latin hypercube sampling for superior initialization diversity, a double LLévy flight mechanism for cross-scale global exploration, dynamic nonlinear weight adjustment for adaptive exploration-exploitation balance, and periodic perturbation strategies to sustain population diversity and mitigate premature convergence. An integrated DLLCOA-MLP predictive model is developed, which jointly optimizes the number of hidden neurons, regularization coefficient, and classification threshold of the multilayer perceptron. Experiments are conducted using real-world continuous casting production data from a steel plant for Q355 grade steel, employing a 4:1 random train-test split and multiple independent runs. Results demonstrate that DLLCOA-MLP achieves superior performance across all metrics: accuracy (96.75%), precision (95.06%), recall (90.09%), and F1-score (90.24%), markedly outperforming baseline BP, standard MLP, PSO-MLP, and original COA-MLP. The proposed method effectively balances the risks of false positives and false negatives, providing an efficient and robust modeling framework for intelligent quality early warning in continuous casting, with substantial industrial application potential.

title: Fixed-Time Distributed Control for Networked Systems With High-Order Switching Dynamics via the FAS Method
authors: Liang Feng, Ping Wang, Ping Li, WeiZhen Liu, GuangRen Duan, Bibo Zheng
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper studies the fixed-time synchronization problem of networked systems with high-order switching nonlinear node dynamics. Firstly, the fully-actuated system (FAS) framework is employed to model and control the high-order nodes directly, thereby preserving the intrinsic dynamics and physical interpretability of the system. To analyze fixed-time stability of synchronization error system under switching behaviors, a switching accumulation (SA) function is introduced to characterize the cumulative effect of switching nonlinearities without requiring explicit dwell-time or average dwell-time assumptions. Based on the FAS formulation, a distributed switching fixed-time synchronization controller is developed for considered high-order complex networks, and explicit settling-time bounds independent of initial conditions are derived. Simulation results on a switching Kuramoto network verify the effectiveness of the proposed approach and illustrate a practical procedure for estimating the fixed settling time based on the SA function.

title: Complexity-Aware Image Task Allocation in Heterogeneous Industrial Edge Computing via Riemannian Geometry
authors: Wenye Wang, Yazhou Yuan, Shengrui Huang, Kai Ma, Zhixin Liu
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Visual inspection through surveillance cameras is essential for quality control in modern manufacturing, generating massive video streams requiring real-time analysis. In edge based video analytics for industrial monitoring, image content complexity varies significantly, imposing different computational demands on CNN inference. However, traditional task scheduling strategies neglect this relationship between content and computation, causing misallocation between task complexity and node capability. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a method named Complexity-Aware Image Task Allocation in Heterogeneous Industrial Edge Computing via Riemannian Geometry (CAITA-RG). The CAITA-RG algorithm establishes a distributed three-tier architecture. By mapping image features into Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) manifold space and computing geodesic distances via the Affine Invariant Riemannian Metric (AIRM), it quantifies the intrinsic computational complexity of image content. Additionally, an adaptation cost function integrating complexity awareness with node capability profiles is designed to achieve precise matching between tasks and hardware. Simulation experiments demonstrate that CAITA-RG accurately predicts inference latency, reduces average response time by approximately 40 % and energy consumption by 27 % compared with the Least Load baseline, and significantly improves system throughput under high concurrency.

title: Fault-Tolerant Control for Stochastic Fully Actuated Systems With Probability-Prescribed Performance
authors: Xueqing Liu, Li Shen, Donghua Zhou
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates fault-tolerant control for stochastic nonlinear fully actuated systems with component faults. Due to the diffusion terms inherent in stochastic systems, deterministic prescribed performance control is infeasible, as tracking errors inevitably exceed fixed envelopes. To address this, a probability-prescribed performance concept is introduced, ensuring that the tracking error remains within a predefined time-varying envelope with a user-specified probability level. An expectation backstepping control scheme with adaptive fault compensation is developed within the fully actuated system framework. Rigorous analysis via Markov's inequality guarantees that the probability-prescribed bound is satisfied. This work provides a quantifiable performance metric for high-precision control under intense stochastic disturbances. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Design of Magnetron Injection Gun for a 95 GHz Gyrotron for High-Order OAM Beam Generation
authors: Ravinder Beemagani, Kavitha Panjagari, Abhishek K. Jha, S. Yuvaraj, M. V. Kartikeyan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: In this work, design of a diode type magnetron injection gun for a 95 GHz gyrotron, operating at the second harmonic, for the generation of Orbital Angular Momentum beams has been presented. The proposed gyrotron operates at $\mathrm{TE}_{6,2}$ mode, produces an output power 50 kW, with an efficiency of 20%, with an OAM order 5. Design optimization and simulation have been carried out using the EGUN software.

title: Characterizations of Functional Observability, Functional Detectability and the Generalized Separation Principle
authors: Junwei Cao, Bin Zhou
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Functional observability and functional detectability are two important concepts in modern control theory, widely applied in fields such as high-dimensional and complex systems. In this paper, new PBH-like tests for functional observability and functional detectability of a special class of systems are provided. Moreover, the paper provides a characterization of the generalized separation principle from a geometric perspective. This offers fresh insights for the existence conditions of functional observers.

title: Distributed Adaptive Finite-Time Attitude Consensus on $S O(3)$ via Geodesic Convex Hull Contraction
authors: Hui Tang, Xiuhui Peng, Ruizhou Gao, Jixiang Li
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a distributed leaderless attitude consensus protocol on the Special Orthogonal Group <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S O(3)$</tex>. For each agent, we construct a local virtual leader through iterative geodesic interpolation (IGI) using neighboring information. The interpolation mechanism inherently confines all agents within a geodesic convex hull, and an adaptive Riemannian gradientbased control law is designed to guarantee finite-time consensus within the convexity radius under strongly connected topology. Besides, we demonstrate that the geodesic interpolation weights influence the system stability, with the manifold curvature further shaping the effect. Simulations confirm that the strategy works effectively, maintaining consensus even when communication links temporarily fail.

title: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems

title: A Hybrid Multi-Branch Deep Learning Model for Autism Spectrum Disorder Detection Using Spatiotemporal Gait Analysis
authors: Ayagoz Mukhanova, Aizat Amirbay, Zhazira Taszhurekova, Abdugani Kholbekov, Khilola Khaydarova, Sabokhat Kalanova, Gulzira Abdikerimova, Aidana Akhmetova, Rohaya Latip, Makbal Baibulova
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Early and objective diagnosis of autism spectrum disorders (ASD) remains a pressing issue in modern medical and cognitive informatics, where motor behavior analysis is considered a promising source of digital biomarkers. In particular, kinematic gait characteristics obtained with contactless sensors enable the identification of subtle motor impairments that may not be apparent on visual assessment. In this paper, we propose a hybrid multi-branch deep learning model, HMB-TSC, for the automatic classification of children with ASD based on spatiotemporal gait data extracted from the Kinect v2 camera. An open dataset containing gait recordings of children with ASD and typically developing (TD) peers was used for the experiments. Data preprocessing included outlier filtering, feature normalization, feature expansion, and time-series segmentation using a sliding-window method. The HMB-TSC architecture combines three complementary branches: a Transformer for modeling global temporal dependencies, a CNN-BiLSTM for extracting local spatiotemporal patterns, and a CNN-BiGRU for efficient sequential analysis. The performance evaluation was conducted using the holdout, 5-fold, and 5 × 3 nested validation protocols. In a reproducible released run, HMB-TSC achieved accuracy = 0.850 and AUROC = 0.948 in holdout validation, while 5-fold and nested validation yielded Accuracy = 0.850 ± 0.035, AUROC = 0.940 ± 0.029, and F1 = 0.845 ± 0.036. Compared with the baseline CNN, LSTM, and Transformer models, the proposed model demonstrated the best overall performance, especially in terms of AUROC and balanced F1-score.

title: Structural Sensitivity of Graph Neural Networks for Intrusion Detection: An Intervention Study
authors: Ki On Chan, Yoohwan Kim, Ju-Yeon Jo, Shaikh Arifuzzaman
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The rapid increase in Internet of Things (IoT) devices in critical infrastructure and enterprise networks has increased cybersecurity risk and motivated the development of graph-based intrusion detection methods. This work presents an intervention-based analysis of structural sensitivity in GNN-based intrusion detection. Using the ToN-IoT and CIC-IDS-2017 datasets, we represent the network as a flow-based directed multigraph for message passing and a simplified undirected graph for structural analysis. We compare weighted cross-entropy (wCE), focal loss, and a combined loss, and our results suggest that wCE is generally competitive across models and datasets.We also conduct ablation studies, suggesting that both node and edge features influence performance, with sensitivity patterns that vary across architectures and datasets. Finally, we evaluate graph interventions including triangle removal, bridge removal, and degree-preserving rewiring. The results suggest that structural sensitivity depends on both the model and the dataset: bridge removal has limited measurable effect across settings, while degree-preserving rewiring tends to produce relatively larger distributional shifts for some model–dataset pairs. Overall, the findings suggest that graph-based intrusion detection is influenced by the interaction between architecture, feature representation, and graph structure.

title: Fed-GPD: Federated Graph Process Distillation for Anomaly Detection in Lights-out Manufacturing
authors: Jun Cai, Manshan Mo, Zhongwei Huang, F. Richard Yu
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: As an essential component of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), lights-out manufacturing (LoM) relies heavily on the rapid detection of anomalies. However, LoM anomalies often arise from complex, cross-modal correlations, and traditional detection models struggle with the dual challenges of limited local data and stringent data privacy requirements, leading to poor generalization. To address these challenges, this paper introduces a novel Federated Graph Process Distillation framework (Fed-GPD) for multi-modal anomaly detection. Our approach first represents heterogeneous industrial data as unified graph structures to effectively model the underlying device relationships. We then propose a new graph knowledge distillation paradigm designed for Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in a federated setting. Instead of merely distilling final predictions, we introduce two novel distillation mechanisms: 1) Neighborhood Aggregation Process Distillation (NAPD), which transfers the knowledge of how a model processes local neighborhood information at each GNN layer, and 2) Relational Knowledge Matrix Distillation (RKMD), which aligns the global understanding of node-to-node relationships learned by the models. These mechanisms are integrated into an asynchronous mentor-mentee architecture, enabling efficient and deep knowledge transfer from powerful, private mentor models to a lightweight, global mentee model. Simulation results on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate that Fed-GPD significantly outperforms existing federated and graph-based anomaly detection methods. Notably, our in-depth ablation studies validate the effectiveness of the proposed process distillation mechanisms, showing substantial improvements in model accuracy and communication efficiency.

title: Relative Pose Estimation for Nonholonomic Robot Formation With UWB-IO Measurements_supp1-3691378.mp4
authors: Kexin Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: This article studies the problem of distributed formation control for multiple robots by using onboard ultra wideband (UWB) distance and inertial odometer (IO) measurements. Although this problem has been widely studied, a fundamental limitation of most works is that they require each robot’s pose and sensor measurements are expressed in a common reference frame. However, it is inapplicable for nonholonomic robot formations due to the practical difficulty of aligning IO measurements of individual robot in a common frame. To address this problem, first, a concurrent-learning-based estimator is first proposed to achieve relative localization between neighboring robots in a local frame. Different from most relative localization methods in a global frame, both relative position and orientation in a local frame are estimated with only UWB ranging and IO measurements. Second, to deal with information loss caused by directed communication topology, a cooperative localization algorithm is introduced to estimate the relative pose to the leader robot. Third, based on the theoretical results on relative pose estimation, a distributed formation tracking controller is proposed for nonholonomic robots. Both 2-D and 3-D real-world experiments conducted on aerial robots and grounded robots are provided to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Two-Stage Probabilistic Penalty for Expensive Constrained Multiobjective Optimization Problems
authors: Zhiyao Zhang, Yong Wang, Guangyong Sun, Junjie Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation
abstract: Handling constraints is the key of solving expensive constrained multiobjective optimization problems (ECMOPs) by evolutionary algorithms. However, existing constraint-handling techniques (CHTs) struggle to effectively explore multiple feasible parts in the early stage and exploit them in the later stage. Therefore, this paper proposes a new CHT for ECMOPs, named two-stage probabilistic penalty (TSP). TSP constructs a penalty objective for each original objective to compare two solutions, defined as the weighted sum of two probabilities (named PoB and PoF) calculated based on Kriging models. For each solution, PoB and PoF measure its optimality on the original objective and its feasibility, respectively. In the first stage, the weights are defined based on the objective-space distance between the two compared solutions. The bigger the distance, the higher the weight of PoB, thereby maintaining some valuable infeasible solutions with good diversity to explore unknown feasible parts. In the second stage, PoB and PoF are assigned equal weights, but their values are modified based on the feasibility of solutions, in order to give feasible solutions higher priority. When enough truly evaluated feasible solutions have been obtained, their decision-space distribution information is integrated into the penalty objectives to further emphasize feasibility. As a result, the penalty objectives of the second stage can effectively guide the population to converge in the already located feasible parts. Moreover, two conditions are designed for the stage transition. Further, we incorporate TSP into a Kriging-assisted evolutionary framework to solve ECMOPs, obtaining TSP+. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of TSP and TSP+.

title: Smart-Grid Load Forecasting with Variational Quantum Algorithms
authors: Asif Iqbal, Muhammad Naveed Aman, Biplab Sikdar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Accurate load forecasting is critical for effective energy management, infrastructure planning, and ensuring the stability of modern power systems. With growing interest in Quantum Computing (QC), recent efforts have demonstrated the applicability of Quantum Machine Learning (QML) across various domains, including supervised and unsupervised learning. However, the use of QML models for Time Series Forecasting (TSF) remains relatively underexplored. In this work, we present a complete framework for TSF based on Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs), capable of both single-step and multi-step ahead forecasting. We use tensor product encoding to encode the classical input samples into quantum states, and use the data re-uploading strategy to introduce temporal dependencies across each layer of the quantum circuit. Within this framework, we systematically evaluate multiple Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC) architectures to assess their suitability for forecasting problem. Among the tested configurations, the nLocal and its modified variant QuReUp consistently outperformed other quantum ansatz designs. Notably, in recursive multi-step forecasting scenarios, these models not only matched but surpassed the performance of a classical Artificial Neural Network (ANN). For example, the nLocal model achieved a 8% reduction in mean absolute error (MAE) over ANN for a 24-hour horizon. These results underscore the potential of VQAs for efficient and accurate TSF, particularly in scenarios requiring short to medium-term predictions. The proposed framework offers a promising foundation for future research, including extensions to diverse datasets and deployment on real quantum hardware to study the impact of quantum noise and scalability.

title: HOFA-Based Backstepping Control for Underactuated Overhead Cranes under Actuator Saturation
authors: Ce Zhang, Zhe Guan, Weili Ding, Changchun Hua
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper addresses the control problem of underactuated overhead crane systems with nonlinear coupling and actuator saturation. A High-Order Fully Actuated (HOFA)-based backstepping strategy is proposed to simplify the system dynamics through nonlinear coordinate transformation. As a result, the original crane model is converted into a fourth-order fully actuated integrator chain, enabling systematic recursive controller design. The physical control force is recovered via inverse mapping with explicit consideration of input constraints. Furthermore, Lyapunov analysis proves global asymptotic stability in the unsaturated case and guarantees uniform ultimate boundedness under actuator saturation. Finally, comparative simulations with an LQR controller demonstrate faster position convergence and improved swing suppression performance of the proposed method.

title: Prescribed-Time Control of the Inverted Pendulum Based on a Fully Actuated System Approach
authors: Ziyi Liu, Zhijie Li, Zelong Zhang, Jian Zhang, Jiaqiang Chen
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the stabilization problem of a cart-inverted pendulum system within a prescribed time using a fully actuated system approach. Through a series of transformations, the inverted pendulum model is converted into a fully actuated system, and a prescribed-time controller suitable for fully actuated nonlinear systems is designed. Based on a prescribedtime tuning function and Lyapunov analysis, it is proven that the cart-inverted pendulum can be stabilized within the preset time. Finally, simulation results are provided to verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Lightweight Meta-Transfer Learning With Mobile-Former for Few-Shot Driver Facial Expression Recognition
authors: Tian Qin, Man Hao, Zitong Lei, Lingdi Fu, Zhe Guan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Facial expression recognition for drivers is challenged by limited annotated data and substantial cross-subject variation in real-world driving scenarios. This paper proposes a lightweight meta-transfer learning framework for few-shot driver facial expression recognition. Mobile-Former is adopted as the backbone and is first pretrained on the FERPlus dataset to learn transferable facial representations. A metric-based Prototypical Network is employed for few-shot adaptation on the KMU-FED dataset through prototype-based classification. Experimental show that the proposed method achieves 96.90 % accuracy under a random split setting. Under the identity-disjoint evaluation protocol, it achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$59.0 \%, 77.2 \%$</tex>, and 82.0 % accuracy in 5 -way 1 shot, 5 -shot, and 10 -shot tasks, respectively, outperforming the counterpart without metric-based meta-learning. Moreover, the model contains only 2.48 M parameters and runs at 155 FPS, demonstrating its potential for real-time in-vehicle deployment.

title: A Memory-Augmented Reinforcement Learning Framework for Visual Navigation Based on Short- and Long-Term Memory Fusion
authors: Haizheng Pei, Lei Lv, Tianyue Wang, Yubo Wu, Mengyang Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: To address the challenges of partial observability and long-horizon decision-making commonly encountered in visual navigation tasks within complex and unknown environments, as well as the redundancy and computational inefficiency of existing memory structure designs, this paper proposes a lightweight memory-augmented reinforcement learning framework. The framework employs a gated recurrent unit (GRU) - based short-term memory module to efficiently model recent environmental dynamics, and introduces a long-term memory module built upon an episodic memory buffer with single-layer self-attention to capture key historical information across temporal scales. In addition, a pose - rotation interaction attention module is designed to adaptively fuse positional and orientation information, thereby enhancing the agent's spatial perception capability. The short-term and long-term memory representations are collaboratively integrated through a gated fusion mechanism and subsequently fed into an A3C-based Actor - Critic network for end-to-end policy learning. Experimental results in simulated environments demonstrate that the proposed lightweight framework consistently outperforms memory-free reinforcement learning methods, recurrent neural network - based policy models, and existing memory-augmented approaches across multiple core metrics, including average path length, collision rate, success rate, and cumulative reward. Meanwhile, the proposed method effectively reduces model complexity while maintaining navigation performance, highlighting its strong potential for practical deployment and real-world applications.

title: FAS-Based Trajectory Tracking Control of Robotic Manipulators Using PINN Modeling and Extended Disturbance Observer
authors: Zhiang Yuan, Yiheng Yang, Ying Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper proposes a trajectory tracking control scheme for robotic manipulators under the fully actuated system (FAS) framework. A physics-informed neural network (PINN) is used to reconstruct system dynamics from offline data, while an extended disturbance observer compensates for lumped uncertainties in real time without acceleration measurements. An FAS-based controller is designed based on the reconstructed model and disturbance estimation. Lyapunov analysis guarantees uniform ultimate boundedness of the closed-loop system. Simulations verify improved tracking performance and robustness.

title: Neuroadaptive Fully Actuated Attitude Tracking for Robot Manipulators with Asymmetric Full-State Constraints
authors: Guangtai Tian, Xiaoyi Guan, Bin Li, Guangren Duan
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: This paper studies the neuroadaptive attitude tracking problem for robot manipulators systems with asymmetric time-varying full-state constraints, model uncertainties, and external disturbances. Most existing robot manipulator attitude control works are constructed through the state-space approach. In this paper, a neuroadaptive attitude control strategy is developed based on the fully actuated system (FAS) approach, which has shown its simplicity and flexibility for controller design of nonlinear systems. We adopt adaptive multilayer neural network (NN) to tackle lumped uncertainties due to the model uncertainties and external disturbances. On this basis, a nonlinear transformation is further integrated into the FAS approach-based control design to achieve exact asymptotic convergence while satisfying full-state constraints. The attitude tracking error is rigorously proven to asymptotically converge to zero, which is a notable improvement over existing NN control works. Moreover, using the FAS approach, stability analysis and controller design are much simpler compared to the conventional backstepping or sliding mode design. Stability analysis and numerical simulations validate the control performance of the proposed control strategy.

title: GAPD-Net: Efficient Pothole Detection Via Low-Rank Adaptation and Gradient-Aware Decoderse
authors: Yikai Men, Shiqi Zou, Jingwei Xu, Chenyi Wang
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: Timely and accurate pavement pothole detection is very important to ensure driving safety. However, due to the scarcity of high-quality labeled data and the interference of complex environment, it is difficult for existing methods to balance high precision and real-time performance on edge devices. To this end, this paper proposes a lightweight gradient-aware pothole detection network (GAPD-Net). The frozen Depth Anything V2 pre-training model is used as the backbone network, and the low-rank adaptation (LoRA) strategy is introduced. This design not only efficiently transfers the three-dimensional geometric prior of the large model to the current task, but also successfully overcomes the over-fitting bottleneck in the small sample scenario through a very small amount of trainable parameters. In addition, a gradient-aware decoder (GAB) is designed to solve the problem of easy loss of features in shadow and low-contrast regions. The module uses LeakyReLU to construct a gradient flow channel in the negative half axis, which effectively alleviates the phenomenon of neuron death and significantly enhances the pixel-level reconstruction ability of the network to blur boundaries. Extensive experiments on the Annotated Potholes Image Dataset show that GAPD-Net achieves 81.21 % mIoU, surpassing mainstream benchmark models such as U-Net and DeepLab V3+. The proposed method achieves an optimal balance between accuracy and computational efficiency, which fully verifies the great potential of the visual basic model in the field of road anomaly detection.

title: Simultaneous Algebraic Estimation of Pseudo-State and Disturbance for a Class of Fractional Order Nonlinear Systems*
authors: Yan-Qiao Wei, Da-Yan Liu, Chang-Chun Hua
publish: 2026 5th Conference on Fully Actuated System Theory and Applications (FASTA)
abstract: In this paper, the pseudo-state and the disturbance of a class of fractional order nonlinear systems are simultaneously estimated via an algebraic approach. To achieve this, an augmented fractional order system model is provided for the studied fractional order nonlinear system. Then, the pseudo-state of the augmented fractional order nonlinear system is estimated using the fractional order modulating functions method. Further, the pseudo-state and the disturbance of the original system are recovered based on the augmented fractional order nonlinear system. Simulation example illustrates the efficiency of the proposed method under the condition of noisy output measurements.

title: Energy and Spectral Characteristics for Large-Signal Impulse Amplification in a Wideband Traveling Wave Tube
authors: Halil Topözlü, John Lambrecht, Nader Behdad, John Booske
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: We present experimental energy and spectral characterization of large impulse amplification in a wideband traveling wave tube (TWT) using metrics spanning both timeand frequency-domain responses. These include input-output energy conversion, and power spectral density comparisons that distinguish in-band amplification from out-of-band spectral growth. We present the results for both positive and negative bipolar impulse excitations to assess waveform asymmetry, distortion, and polarity-dependent electron-wave interaction effects in the large-signal regime. Additionally, during the talk we will discuss the influences of beam energy and impulse center frequency on impulse amplification, revealing their roles in shaping gain saturation, temporal compression, and spectral broadening. This work aims to identify the key parameters governing efficient wideband impulse amplification in TWTs and provide a framework for comparing transient amplification performance beyond traditional small-signal metrics.

title: Experimental Measurements of High-Power Microwave (HPM) Power with Directional Coupler and Crystal Detector: Calibration of Crystal Detectors
authors: Andrey D. Andreev
publish: 2026 IEEE International Vacuum Electronics Conference (IVEC)
abstract: An HPM power generated and extracted through an output waveguide either into a free space through an HPM antenna or into an HPM load, like it is done in a relativistic magnetron with a radial output, for example, may be relatively easily measured by a crystal detector and a waveguide directional coupler inserted between an output waveguide and an HPM antenna/load. Both the crystal detector and the directional coupler need to be calibrated at a known frequency of the measured HPM power. And while a directional coupler may be relatively easily calibrated with a network analyzer in the frequency domain, calibration of a crystal detector requires using (i) an RF source operating in the time domain at a given frequency and supplying different levels of input RF power to a crystal detector, and (ii) an oscilloscope measuring an output voltage from a crystal detector. The new innovative method of crystal detector calibration using a sole network analyzer is developed, tested, and compared with the traditional method involving both an RF power source and an oscilloscope. The new calibration method markedly simplifies the entire crystal detector calibration process and may be used in practical experiments with different HPM sources/amplifiers. It may be also used by the crystal detector manufacturers for fast, accurate and cost-inexpensive “cherry-picking” and calibrating the new, freshly-made crystal detectors before marketing them out.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Robotics & Automation Magazine

title: Performance Analysis of Dynamically Routed O-ISLs under PAT Constraints in LEO Constellations
authors: Manus Pengnoo, Rachata Maneekut, Pasu Kaewplung
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Optical Inter-Satellite Links (O-ISLs) in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) constellations enable low-latency, high-throughput global communication and are a key enabler of future Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTN) under 5G Advanced, 6G and beyond. This paper presents a simulation framework that integrates realistic orbital propagation from Two-Line Element (TLE) data with physical layer constraints, including Doppler shift, geometric visibility, and hardware limits on Pointing, Acquisition, and Tracking (PAT) terminals, to evaluate dynamic, latency-aware routing. Our results show that Doppler shifts are governed by relative velocity vectors rather than link distance, with most values within±5 GHz, confirming compatibility with current optical transceiver capabilities. Our analysis spans the full spectrum of routing distances to characterize network performance. At the regional scale, a Bangkok–Tokyo route demonstrates consistent latencies of 65–80 ms and bit rates above 6.5 Gbps under a two-PAT limit. Progressing to the global scale, a nearly antipodal route (Bangkok–Los Angeles) reveals a quantifiable trade-off between latency and throughput as a function of maximum ISL distance. These findings validate the feasibility of O-ISL-enabled NTN architectures and provide quantitative guidance for routing strategies and physical layer design in large-scale LEO constellations.

title: Workload-Oriented Computation Offloading Game for UAV-Assisted Mobile Edge Computing: A Game-Theoretic Approach
authors: Jieming Zhou, Xiaolong Xu, Guangming Cui, Shucun Fu, Muhammad Bilal
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The rapid development of the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) has led to a surge of computation-intensive and latency-sensitive vehicular applications, which pose significant challenges to resource-constrained vehicles and conventional mobile edge computing (MEC) infrastructures. In dense urban scenarios, the high mobility of vehicles and the spatiotemporally uneven distribution of vehicular workloads often result in severe load imbalance among edge servers, degrading system performance. To address these challenges, this paper investigates a three-tier heterogeneous MEC-enabled IoV architecture that integrates vehicles, terrestrial base stations (BSs), and an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV). In this architecture, the UAV acts as an elastic computing node that is dynamically deployed over hotspot regions to complement BS-based MEC, while task migration among edge servers is leveraged to alleviate localized overload. We formulate the multi-vehicle computation offloading and migration problem as a distributed game, where each vehicle autonomously selects among local computing, BS offloading, and BS-to-BS migration, while BS-to-UAV migration is available only when its nearest BS lies within the current UAV coverage region. A joint cost function that captures both computation latency and energy consumption is developed. By constructing an appropriate potential function, we prove that the proposed game admits a Nash equilibrium. Building on this property, a Workload-oriented Computation Offloading Game for UAV-assisted MEC (WCOG) is designed to enable scalable and distributed decision-making. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves near-optimal system performance, effectively balances server workloads, and significantly reduces the computation cost compared with benchmarks, while maintaining strong scalability under increasing vehicular density or expanding server scales.

title: Discovering Cyclists’ Street Visual Preferences From Shared Bike Trajectories and Street View Images Using XAI-Enhanced Inverse Reinforcement Learning
authors: Kezhou Ren, Meihan Jin, Huiming Liu, Mengwei Xin, Yongxi Gong, Yu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Cycling has gained global popularity due to its health benefits and positive impacts on urban areas. While prior work has examined how urban built environments influence cyclists’ behaviors, cyclists’ route-choice mechanisms remain insufficiently understood largely due to overlooking the complex and continuous nature of cycling. To address this gap, we introduce a data-driven framework that integrates Maximum Entropy Deep Inverse Reinforcement Learning (MEDIRL) with explainable artificial intelligence (XAI). This framework is designed to quantify and interpret cyclists’ street visual preferences, which are their preferences for the street-level built environment during cycling. Our approach uses MEDIRL to approximate reward functions that reflect cyclists’ street visual preferences by integrating dockless bike-sharing (DBS) trajectories with street view images (SVIs). Subsequently, we apply XAI techniques to reveal the complex effects of street visual elements on those quantified street visual preferences. We validate our framework in the Bantian Subdistrict, Shenzhen. The results demonstrate that the quantified street visual preferences significantly outperform shortest-path models and other baselines in explaining cyclists’ route decision-making. Besides, cyclists exhibit strong preferences towards elements associated with perceived safety, street enclosure, and cycling comfort. Moreover, our detailed analysis discovers nonlinear relationships among these street visual elements, providing a scalable, data-driven foundation for understanding cyclists’ behaviors and guiding streetscape design.

title: Learning Visually Interpretable Oscillator Networks for Soft Continuum Robots from Video_supp1-3703241.mp4
authors: Henrik Krauss
publish: N/A
abstract: Learning soft continuum robot (SCR) dynamics from video offers flexibility but existing methods lack interpretability or rely on prior assumptions. Model-based approaches require prior knowledge and manual design. We bridge this gap by introducing: (1) The Attention Broadcast Decoder (ABCD), a plug-and-play module for autoencoder-based latent dynamics learning that generates pixel-accurate attention maps localizing each latent dimension's contribution while filtering static backgrounds, enabling visual interpretability via spatially grounded latents and on-image overlays. (2) Visual Oscillator Networks (VONs), a 2D latent oscillator network coupled to ABCD attention maps for on-image visualization of learned masses, coupling stiffness, and forces, thereby enabling mechanical interpretability. We validate our approach on single- and double-segment SCRs, demonstrating that ABCD-based models significantly improve multi-step prediction accuracy with 5.8<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$times$</tex-math></inline-formula> error reduction for Koopman operators and 3.5<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$times$</tex-math></inline-formula> for oscillator networks on a two-segment robot. VONs autonomously discover a chain structure of oscillators. This fully data-driven approach yields compact, mechanically interpretable models with potential relevance for future control applications.

title: Enhancing Multi-Robot Exploration Using Probabilistic Frontier Prioritization with Dirichlet Process Gaussian Mixtures_supp1-3703222.mp4
authors: John Lewis
publish: N/A
abstract: Multi-agent autonomous exploration is essential for applications such as environmental monitoring, search and rescue, and industrial-scale surveillance. However, effective coordination under communication constraints remains a significant challenge. Frontier exploration algorithms analyze the boundary between the known and unknown regions to determine the next-best view that maximizes exploratory gain. This article proposes an enhancement to existing frontier-based exploration algorithms by introducing a probabilistic approach to frontier prioritization. By leveraging Dirichlet process Gaussian mixture model (DP-GMM) and a probabilistic formulation of information gain, the method improves the quality of frontier prioritization. The proposed enhancement, integrated into two state-of-the-art multi-agent exploration algorithms, consistently improves performance across environments of varying clutter, communication constraints, and team sizes. Simulations showcase an average exploration time improvement of 10% and 14% for the two algorithms across all combinations. Successful deployment in real-world experiments with a dual-drone system further corroborates these findings.

title: LRTV-PLA: Lightweight and Robust Physical-Layer Authentication With Time-Varying Distribution Shift in Dynamic Wireless Environments
authors: Yanru Guo, Xiaolan Yi, Yuanyuan Zhang, Liangyin Chen, Yanru Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: CSI-based physical-layer authentication (PLA) is a promising solution for securing access in wireless communication systems. However, distribution shifts caused by time-varying CSI and computational limitations due to constrained edge resources jointly degrade authentication accuracy. Existing methods lack effective modeling of the temporal dynamics and long-range dependencies in time-varying CSI and high computational costs, resulting in limited robustness and generalization in dynamic environments. To address these challenges, we propose LRTV-PLA, a lightweight and robust PLA with time-varying distribution shift in dynamic wireless environments. Specifically, we propose temporal interpolation with contrastive regularization (TICR) to address sparse CSI sequences by interpolating missing data and constraining cross-time variation. We also adopt a shallow autoencoder for nonlinear dimensionality reduction (SAE-NLDR) to reduce cost while preserving discriminative features. We further introduce LinA-BiLSTM, which combines bidirectional LSTM for short-term dependencies with linformer-based mulit-head attention for efficient long-range modeling, reducing attention complexity from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(n^{2})$ </tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(n)$ </tex-math></inline-formula> and enabling lower latency and memory usage on edge devices. Finally, extensive experiments conducted in real-world scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches in accuracy, robustness, and resource efficiency. For example, in the street scenario, our method improves authentication accuracy by 1.47% compared to the best baseline, shortens authentication time by approximately 60%,decreases memory usage by around 36%, and reduces the standard deviation from 1.52 to 0.95.

title: Position and Velocity Estimation Method for Non-Orthogonally Passing Charged Particles in Aero-Engine Exhaust Systems Based on an Electrostatic Sensor Array
authors: Kai Tang, Shengxin Rao, Xiao Sun, Zikang Song, Jiamao Tian, Xuqiang Lang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This paper addresses the reduction in particle localization and velocity-measurement accuracy caused by the non-orthogonal traversal of high-speed charged particles through an electrostatic sensor array in aero-engine exhaust systems. A dual-row arc-shaped current-type electrostatic sensor array measurement scheme is developed, a global cross-correlation velocity-measurement method is proposed, and particle localization is achieved based on compressed sensing. First, an induced-current forward model of the arc-shaped electrodes is established based on the electric-field induction mechanism. Then, a cross-sectional spatial sensitivity matrix is constructed using the amplitude features of the induced charge pulses in each channel, and the spatial position of the particle at a fixed cross-section is reconstructed using the compressed-sensing method. A multi-channel global cross-correlation algorithm is further proposed to determine the particle velocity. A pneumatic high-speed particle launching platform is built for experimental validation. The experimental results show that, under the selected 13 parallel-motion trajectory conditions, the relative localization errors of the compressed-sensing method are within 1.52% when the particle is located near the central region, while the average experimental error reaches 10.38% when the particle is close to the wall. In the velocity-measurement experiments under the selected nine inclination-angle conditions, the global cross-correlation algorithm achieves an average relative error of 5.56%, demonstrating stronger robustness and consistency than the independent cross-correlation algorithm when an inclination angle exists between the particle trajectory and the sensor.

title: Fundamentals of NOMA in Low-Earth Orbit Coordinated Multi-Satellite Networks
authors: Xiangyu Li, Bodong Shang, Junchao Ma, Qingqing Wu, Jie Feng, Deshuang Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Coordinated multi-satellite (CoMS) transmission and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) are envisioned to jointly enhance coverage, capacity, and spectrum efficiency for satellite networks. Their integration into a unified CoMS-NOMA framework will allow more efficient, reliable, and energy-efficient multi-user access. This paper investigates the downlink performance of CoMS-NOMA networks from a system-level perspective, in which multiple satellites cooperatively serve multiple users via NOMA. Leveraging tools from stochastic geometry, related angles and distances in CoMS-NOMA are first derived as intermediate results. Then, we obtain the combined signal power distributions and analyze coverage and spectrum performance under both inter- and intra-satellite interference, accounting for potential imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC). The analytical model is validated across a range of system parameters, including the number of satellites, service region angle, error-propagation factor, and power allocation coefficients. Numerical results indicate that increasing the number of cooperative satellites does not always improve coverage and spectrum efficiency. Additionally, while a higher main-lobe gain improves coverage, a near-perfect SIC provides only slightly greater benefits than a reasonably good SIC. With properly selected power allocation coefficients, CoMS-NOMA achieves up to a 270% improvement in coverage and a 56% gain in sum spectral efficiency, compared with conventional orthogonal and single-satellite schemes, indicating potential for green, energy-efficient satellite networking.

title: Design and Performance Analysis of Virtual MIMO for IoT/WSNs with Heterogeneous Symbol Rates
authors: Tessema Tariku Terefe, Arafat Al-Dweik, Shihab Jimaa, Sean Swei
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This article presents the design and performance analysis of uplink Internet of things (IoT) and wireless sensor networks (WSNs) with virtual multiple-input multiple-output (V-MIMO). In contrast to existing literature, this work addresses scenarios where the network nodes transmit non-uniform symbol rates, allowing each node to have a unique symbol period. Such flexibility enables nodes to manage power consumption and data transmission rates more efficiently, significantly boosting system adaptability and ensuring longer network operation. To reduce receiver complexity, a zero-forcing (ZF) equalizer is employed for symbol demultiplexing, followed by an maximum ratio combining (MRC) to combine the samples from low-rate nodes efficiently. The system performance is evaluated using bit error rate (BER) where an exact closed-form expression is derived for an arbitrary number of nodes in Rayleigh time-varying fading channels. The obtained analytical results, verified by Monte Carlo simulation, demonstrate that the proposed V-MIMO framework enables low-rate users to achieve more than 99% transmission power savings in specific scenarios while achieving a BER comparable to the high-rate users.

title: Flexible Silicon Nanoribbon PIN Diodes with Printed Contacts for Self-Powered UV Photodetection
authors: Abhishek Singh Dahiya, Ayoub Zumeit, Nail Günaltay, Adamos Christou, Ravinder Dahiya
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: Inorganic semiconductor nanostructures offer a compelling route toward high-performance flexible electronics, overcoming the intrinsic limitations of solution-processed organic and metal-oxide semiconductors in carrier mobility, operational stability, and switching speed. However, achieving performance comparable to conventionally fabricated silicon devices require not only high-mobility material but also high-quality metal contacts. Although significant progress has been made in large-area-compatible integration techniques that enable high-yield transfer of such nanostructures onto compliant substrates, metal contact formation still largely relies on conventional photolithography and vacuum-based metallization, representing a key bottleneck toward scalable and cost-effective large-area electronics (LAE). Here, we report a high-resolution direct ink writing (DIW) strategy for metal contact formation on direct roll printed (DRP) silicon p<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup>–i–n<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> (PIN) nanoribbons (NRs), enabling the fabrication of flexible PIN diodes on a printing-compatible platform. The electrical characteristics of devices with printed contacts are systematically benchmarked against counterparts fabricated via conventional microfabrication. The printed diodes exhibit a comparable turn-on voltage (≈ 1 V) and rectification ratio (>10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup>), with approximately one order of magnitude lower forward current at 2 V relative to conventionally fabricated devices. Furthermore, under ultraviolet (UV) illumination, the printed diodes demonstrate stable zero-bias photoresponse (I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on/off</sub> ≈40 and responsivity ≈0.01 A W<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> at UV light intensity of ∼37 mW cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-2</sup>), confirming self-powered photodetection capability. These results establish that high-resolution printed contacts can preserve diode performance while substantially enhancing manufacturing scalability, providing a practical pathway toward fully printed, high-performance inorganic nanostructure-based flexible electronic and optoelectronic systems.

title: Correntropy Based Multi-Stage Resilient Distributed Estimation Over Adversarial Multitask Networks Against Noisy Interference
authors: Senran Peng, Lijuan Jia, Zi-Jiang Yang, Xiaobin Zhao, Ran Tao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper studies robust and resilient distributed estimation in adversarial multitask wireless sensor networks (WSNs) under input noise and output impulsive interference. Under these conditions, malicious attacks and noises degrade network estimation performance by tampering with node parameters and disrupting communication. To resolve these challenges, we propose the Neighboring Node Resilience Acquisition-based Secure Multitask Diffusion Bias-Compensated Least Mean M-Estimate (NRAS-MDBCLMM) algorithm. To eliminate the influence of input noises, the MD-LMM algorithm is augmented with a bias compensation term that can estimate noise variance in real-time without prior knowledge. To mitigate the impact of malicious attacks in the multitask network, we introduce a node task similarity based multi-stage reliable neighbor node resilience acquisition. Firstly, we propose a neighboring node relative state perception and detection method using generalized correntropy. Secondly, a resilience-driven distributed detection framework is also developed for dynamic malicious node reduction. Finally, the performance of the proposed algorithm is analyzed and derived. Simulations confirm that the proposed NRAS-MDBCLMM algorithm outperforms state-of-the-art multitask estimation methods in both complex attack scenarios and impulsive interference environments.

title: Data-Driven Disassembly Line Allocation via RoBERTa Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Xiwang Guo, Tongyuan Zhang, Jiacun Wang, Shixin Liu, Shujin Qin, Liang Qi, Yingjun Ji, Jiangtao Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: In modern manufacturing, efficient disassembly line allocation is critical for productivity and recycling value but is often hindered by manual bottlenecks. To address this, this work proposes RoBERTa-based disassembly allocation system (RoDAS), a unified framework synergizing RoBERTa-based semantic perception and reinforcement learning (RL) control. By leveraging a pretrained language model to generate task-specific datasets, RoBERTa is fine-tuned to process complexity-augmented prompts, integrating semantic features and product priors to generate precise initial policies. This encoder integrates graph-based knowledge retrieval and vector databases to construct multisource prompt, enabling the generation of a precise, knowledge-driven initial policy. Subsequently, an RL agent utilizes this strategy as a warm-start mechanism to simulate the disassembly process and dynamically calibrate decisions under real-time constraints. Extensive experiments validate that RoDAS achieves a Pareto-optimal balance, significantly reducing completion time while maintaining recycling value compared to leading state-of-the-art methods, including DeepSeek and GPT-4o.

title:

                    PD Dataset: Enabling Dual-view Photoplethysmography on Smartphones in Lab and Clinical Settings
authors: Jiankai Tang, Tao Zhang, Jia Li, Yuntao Wang, Yiru Zhang, Mingyu Zhang, Kegang Wang, Yuming Hao, Bolin Wang, Haiyang Li, Xingyao Wang, Yuanchun Shi, Sicong Qian
